# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define Responsible AI. List any **four core principles** of Responsible AI.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- **Definition (1 mark)**: Responsible AI is the practice of designing, developing, and deploying AI systems in a way that is ethical, fair, transparent, and accountable, while minimizing harm to individuals and society.
- **Four Principles (2 marks — 0.5 each)**:
  1. **Fairness** — no discrimination based on protected attributes.
  2. **Transparency** — decisions must be explainable.
  3. **Accountability** — clear ownership of AI outcomes.
  4. **Privacy** — protection of personal and sensitive data.

> (Acceptable alternatives: Safety, Human-centricity, Sustainability.)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
What is **algorithmic bias**? Give **one real-world example**.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: Algorithmic bias is the systematic and repeatable error in an AI system that creates **unfair outcomes**, such as privileging one group over another, due to biases in training data, model design, or deployment context.
- **Example (1.5 marks)**: Amazon's AI recruiting tool (2018) was found to be biased against women because it was trained on resumes submitted over 10 years — most of which came from men — causing it to downrank resumes containing words like *"women's"*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **six core principles of Responsible AI** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **positive and negative societal impacts of AI** across at least **four domains**. Suggest **Responsible AI mitigations** for each negative impact. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU expects each principle to be defined (1 mark) and illustrated with an example (≈0.2 marks). Allocate roughly **1.1 marks per principle**.

1. **Fairness (1.1 marks)**: AI must treat all individuals and groups equitably. *Example*: AI recruitment tools must not favor one gender/caste.
2. **Transparency & Explainability (1.1 marks)**: AI decisions should be interpretable. *Example*: SHAP values in loan-approval AI showing which features contributed to rejection.
3. **Accountability (1.1 marks)**: Humans and organizations must be answerable for AI outcomes. *Example*: Hospital is accountable when AI-assisted diagnosis causes harm.
4. **Privacy (1.1 marks)**: Personal data must be protected. *Example*: Differential privacy in Apple iOS data collection.
5. **Safety & Robustness (1.1 marks)**: AI must resist adversarial attacks and edge cases. *Example*: Tesla's autopilot tested against adversarial road signs.
6. **Human-Centricity (1.1 marks)**: AI augments humans and respects dignity. *Example*: AI scribes assisting doctors without replacing clinical judgment.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks)**: The six principles form an **interconnected framework** — neglecting one often harms others (e.g., lack of transparency makes accountability impossible).

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

> [!TIP]
> Use a **table format** in the exam — KTU examiners reward structured answers. Cover **4 domains × (1 negative impact + 1 mitigation) ≈ 7 marks**.

| Domain | Negative Societal Impact | Responsible AI Mitigation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Employment** | Resume-screening AI rejects female candidates. | Bias audits, gender-blind preprocessing, human review. |
| **Criminal Justice** | COMPAS recidivism predictor is racially biased. | Transparency reports, judicial oversight, fairness re-training. |
| **Healthcare** | Diagnostic AI performs poorly on darker skin tones. | Diverse training data, subgroup accuracy testing. |
| **Media** | Deepfakes spread political misinformation. | Watermarking (C2PA), provenance metadata, content moderation AI. |

**Conclusion (0.5 marks)**: Responsible AI is the bridge between **technological progress** and **societal well-being**; without it, AI risks amplifying existing inequalities.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **lifecycle of a Responsible AI system**. Describe each phase in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A facial recognition system has the following results:
- Out of 1000 males, 850 are correctly identified.
- Out of 1000 females, 650 are correctly identified.

Compute the **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** and **Disparate Impact (DI)**. Is the system fair as per the **4/5 rule**? Suggest **two mitigation techniques**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

Refer to **Section 3.4** and **Section 4.1** for the lifecycle diagram. The 6 phases:

1. **Problem Definition (1 mark)**: Identify stakeholders, define the use case, conduct **ethical risk assessment** (e.g., Datasheets for Datasets).
2. **Data Collection (1 mark)**: Ensure **informed consent**, **privacy** (GDPR/DPDP), and **representativeness** across demographic groups.
3. **Model Design (1.5 marks)**: Embed **fairness constraints** (e.g., demographic parity, equal opportunity) and **explainability modules** (LIME, SHAP, Counterfactual explanations).
4. **Training & Validation (1.5 marks)**: Apply **fairness metrics** (SPD, DI, EOD), conduct **robustness tests** (adversarial perturbations), and **cross-group accuracy** evaluation.
5. **Deployment with HITL (1 mark)**: Implement **Human-in-the-Loop** controls, log decisions, enable **appeals/redressal** mechanisms.
6. **Post-deployment Audit (1 mark)**: Continuous monitoring for **model drift**, **data drift**, and **emergent harms**; publish **Model Cards** and **Transparency Reports**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify rates**:

$$
P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85 \quad \text{(males)}
$$

$$
P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) = \frac{650}{1000} = 0.65 \quad \text{(females)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SPD (2 marks)**:

$$
\text{SPD} = 0.85 - 0.65 = 0.20
$$

**Step 3 — Compute DI (2 marks)**:

$$
\text{DI} = \frac{0.65}{0.85} \approx 0.7647
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 4/5 rule (1.5 marks)**:

Since $\text{DI} = 0.7647 < 0.80$, the system **violates** the 4/5 rule. **The system is unfair to females**.

**Step 5 — Two mitigation techniques (1.5 marks)**:

1. **Re-weighting / Re-sampling** — rebalance the training dataset to have equal representation of genders; or assign higher loss weights to the underperforming group.
2. **Adversarial Debiasing** — train the model with an adversary network that tries to predict the protected attribute (gender) from the predictions; the main model is penalized when the adversary succeeds, forcing the predictions to be gender-invariant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**:
> 1. **Mixing up numerator/denominator in DI** — KTU strictly expects **unprivileged/privileged** order; reversing it loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4/5 rule threshold** — merely computing DI without stating $\text{DI} \geq 0.80$ loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **No "Conclusion" line** — KTU always rewards a concluding statement. End with: *"Hence, the system is unfair and requires mitigation."*
> 4. **Generic mitigations** — saying *"use more data"* gets 0 marks; specific techniques (re-weighting, adversarial debiasing, fairness constraints) get full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in (a)** — KTU mandates a diagram for lifecycle questions; missing it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 (KTU 2024 Scheme)** — read this **30 minutes before the exam**.

- ✅ **AI = Artificial Intelligence**: branch of CS that simulates human intelligence.
- ✅ **Responsible AI = Ethical + Fair + Transparent + Accountable + Private + Safe AI**.
- ✅ **6 Core Principles**: Fairness, Transparency, Accountability, Privacy, Safety, Human-centricity.
- ✅ **AI History** (must remember dates): 1950 Turing Test, 1956 Dartmouth, 2012 Deep Learning boom.
- ✅ **Types of AI**: Narrow vs. General vs. Super; Reactive, Limited Memory, Theory of Mind, Self-Aware.
- ✅ **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** $= P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$; ideal $= 0$.
- ✅ **Disparate Impact (DI)** $= \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}$; fair if $\in [0.8, 1.25]$ (4/5 rule).
- ✅ **Positive Impacts**: healthcare, education, accessibility, environment, economy.
- ✅ **Negative Impacts**: bias, job loss, deepfakes, surveillance, environmental cost, weaponization.
- ✅ **Real-world cases to memorize**:
  - Amazon recruiting AI (gender bias)
  - COMPAS (racial bias in criminal justice)
  - Microsoft Tay (deployment failure)
  - Apple Card (gender-based credit limit controversy)
  - Clearview AI (privacy violations)
- ✅ **Regulations**: EU AI Act 2024, India DPDP Act 2023, US AI Bill of Rights, OECD AI Principles, UNESCO Recommendation.
- ✅ **Lifecycle stages**: Problem → Data → Design → Train → Deploy → Audit.
- ✅ **Bias mitigation techniques**: re-weighting, re-sampling, adversarial debiasing, fairness constraints, causal fairness.
- ✅ **Explainability tools**: SHAP, LIME, Counterfactual explanations, Model Cards, Datasheets.
- ✅ **Privacy techniques**: Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, Homomorphic Encryption.
- ✅ **CO1–CO3 mapping**: Module 1 tests **understanding** (CO1) and **analysis** (CO2/CO3) at Bloom's L2–L4.
- ✅ **Most-likely 14-mark question**: *"Discuss societal impact + Responsible AI principles"* OR *"Compute fairness metrics for a given case."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fairness and Bias: Sources of Biases, Exploratory Data Analysis, Limitations of a Dataset, Preprocessing, Inprocessing and Postprocessing to Remove Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fairness and Bias: Sources of Biases, Exploratory Data Analysis, Limitations of a Dataset, Preprocessing, Inprocessing and Postprocessing to Remove Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bias in AI?

**Bias** in Artificial Intelligence refers to systematic errors or skewed tendencies in a model's predictions that arise from prejudiced assumptions present in the training data, the learning algorithm itself, or the way outputs are interpreted. When a model consistently favors one group over another based on sensitive attributes such as gender, race, age, or socioeconomic status, the model is said to be **biased**.

**Fairness** is the corresponding ethical and mathematical property that demands an AI system should treat individuals and groups equitably, without discrimination based on protected attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Formal Definition**
> According to the 2024 KTU syllabus for PECST752, *bias* is defined as a **systematic deviation** in the output of an AI system caused by **flawed data, flawed assumptions, or flawed model design**, which leads to **unfair, inaccurate, or unethical outcomes** for certain subgroups in the population.

## 1.2 What is Fairness?

**Fairness** is the property of an AI system that ensures predictions are independent of sensitive/protected attributes (like race, gender, caste, religion, age, disability, etc.). It is mathematically expressed through several **group fairness** and **individual fairness** criteria, which we will derive rigorously in Section 2.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The Broken Ruler Problem

Imagine two students — **Aravind** and **Bhavya** — sitting for an exam, but the ruler (measuring instrument) used to grade them was **manufactured in a factory that accidentally stretched the markings for one section**. Every time Aravind's answer sheet is measured, the ruler gives the correct length, but Bhavya's answer sheet (from a different batch) is measured incorrectly.

Even though both students answered perfectly, Bhavya is unfairly penalized. This is exactly what **algorithmic bias** does:

- The **ruler** → the AI model
- The **stretched markings** → biased training data or biased assumptions
- The **penalized student** → the discriminated subgroup

The fix is **not** to blame the student — it is to **recalibrate the ruler**, or to build a new, calibrated one. This is what **debiasing techniques** (preprocessing, inprocessing, postprocessing) achieve.

## 1.4 Core Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Protected/Sensitive Attribute (S):** A feature that must not be the basis for discrimination (e.g., gender, race, caste, religion). Examples: $\text{S} = \{\text{Male}, \text{Female}\}$.
>
> **Privileged Group:** The group that historically receives favorable outcomes (e.g., males in some hiring datasets).
>
> **Unprivileged Group:** The group that historically faces disadvantage (e.g., females in the same dataset).
>
> **Target Variable (Y):** The actual ground-truth label (e.g., "hired" or "not hired").
>
> **Predicted Variable ($\hat{Y}$):** The output predicted by the model.
>
> **Disparate Impact (DI):** A ratio that measures whether outcomes are equitable across groups. A value of **1.0** means perfect equity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Biases – The 7 Major Categories

Bias does not magically appear; it **originates from somewhere** in the AI pipeline. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify and explain these sources. Here is the exhaustive list:

### 🔹 1. Historical Bias
- Pre-existing societal inequities embedded in the world **before** any data is collected.
- **Example:** Amazon's recruitment AI (2018) downranked resumes containing the word "women's" because the historical hiring data reflected a male-dominated tech industry.
- **Why it happens:** The data is a mirror of past discriminatory decisions.

### 🔹 2. Representation Bias
- Occurs when certain groups are **under-represented or over-represented** in the dataset.
- **Example:** A face recognition system trained predominantly on lighter-skinned faces fails on darker-skinned faces.
- **Metric impact:** Model accuracy drops sharply for the minority class.

### 🔹 3. Measurement Bias
- Arises when **features or labels are measured inconsistently** across groups.
- **Example:** A "creditworthiness" label may reflect arrests, but policing intensity varies by neighborhood, making the label itself biased.

### 🔹 4. Aggregation Bias
- Occurs when a single model is applied to **heterogeneous subgroups**, but the model is too simplistic to capture subgroup-specific patterns.
- **Example:** A diabetes prediction model trained on aggregated data may work for adults but fail for adolescents.

### 🔹 5. Learning Bias
- Introduced **during model training** due to algorithmic choices (loss function, regularization, optimization method).
- **Example:** A neural network amplifying spurious correlations between gender and job performance.

### 🔹 6. Evaluation Bias
- Occurs when **benchmark datasets** used to evaluate the model do not represent the deployment population.
- **Example:** NLP models benchmarked only on Western English text failing on Indian English dialects.

### 🔹 7. Deployment Bias
- Arises when a model is used in a context **different** from the one it was designed for.
- **Example:** A criminal risk-assessment model trained for sentencing being misused for pre-trial bail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** In ESE (End Semester Exam), a 7-mark question often asks: *"Explain any four sources of bias in AI systems."* Always use the **specific name + definition + real-world example** triplet for full marks.

## 2.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) – The Detective Phase

**Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** is the systematic process of **summarizing, visualizing, and understanding** the main characteristics of a dataset **before** feeding it to a model. In Responsible AI, EDA is the **first line of defense** against bias.

### Purpose of EDA:
1. Detect **class imbalance** (e.g., 95% male, 5% female).
2. Identify **missing values** that may be non-random (MAR — Missing At Random).
3. Uncover **outliers and anomalies**.
4. Examine **correlations** between protected attributes and target.
5. Validate **representativeness** of the dataset.

### Standard EDA Techniques:

| Technique | Purpose | Bias Detected |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| `value_counts()` / Frequency tables | Count class distribution | Representation bias |
| Histograms / Box plots | Visualize spread and skew | Measurement bias |
| Correlation heatmap | Find dependencies | Proxy discrimination |
| Pair plots | Spot interactions | Aggregation bias |
| Missing-value heatmap | Visualize missingness patterns | Sampling bias |
| Group-wise statistics | Compare means across subgroups | Historical bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EDA matters for fairness:** A model trained on biased data without EDA will simply **learn and amplify** the bias. EDA exposes the problem so it can be fixed.

## 2.3 Limitations of a Dataset – KTU Exam Favorite

Even after EDA, datasets may have intrinsic limitations. KTU examiners test these explicitly:

### 🔸 1. Sample Size Limitations
- Small datasets → high variance, unreliable fairness metrics.
- Subgroup sizes may be too small for **statistically significant** fairness conclusions.

### 🔸 2. Sampling Bias
- Data collected via **convenience sampling** (e.g., online surveys) excludes offline populations.

### 🔸 3. Temporal Limitations
- Data from 2010 may not represent 2024 realities (concept drift).

### 🔸 4. Label Noise
- Human annotators may apply subjective or inconsistent labels.

### 🔸 5. Missing Protected Attributes
- Sometimes the sensitive attribute is **not collected** (e.g., due to privacy laws), making fairness auditing difficult.

### 🔸 6. Selection Bias
- The data describes only those who **chose to participate** (e.g., job applicants ≠ general population).

### 🔸 7. Proxy Variables
- Even if "race" is removed, **ZIP code** can act as a proxy for race (redlining effect).

## 2.4 Bias Mitigation Strategies – The Three Pillars

The bias mitigation pipeline is divided into **three stages**:

### 🧊 Pillar 1: PREPROCESSING (Fix the Data Before Training)

Goal: Modify the training data so that bias is removed **before** any model sees it.

| Technique | Mechanism |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Reweighing** | Assign weights to training samples so that privileged and unprivileged groups contribute equally to the loss. |
| **Sampling (Oversampling/Undersampling)** | Balance class frequencies across subgroups. |
| **Suppression** | Remove the protected attribute and its strongest proxies. |
| **Disparate Impact Remover** | Edits feature values to remove correlation with S while preserving rank-order within groups. |
| **Learning Fair Representations (LFR)** | Maps data to a latent space where protected attributes cannot be recovered, but utility is preserved. |

### 🧊 Pillar 2: INPROCESSING (Fix the Model During Training)

Goal: Modify the learning algorithm itself to incorporate fairness constraints.

| Technique | Mechanism |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Adversarial Debiasing** | Trains a predictor and an adversary simultaneously; adversary tries to predict S from $\hat{Y}$; predictor learns to fool the adversary. |
| **Fairness Regularization** | Adds a penalty term to the loss: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{accuracy}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}$. |
| **Constrained Optimization** | Optimizes accuracy subject to a fairness constraint (e.g., demographic parity $\leq \epsilon$). |
| **Multitask Learning** | Learns the main task and a fairness task jointly. |

### 🧊 Pillar 3: POSTPROCESSING (Fix the Output After Training)

Goal: Adjust the model's predictions **after** training without retraining.

| Technique | Mechanism |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Reject Option Classification** | For uncertain predictions near the decision boundary, favor the unprivileged group. |
| **Calibrated Equalized Odds** | Adjusts thresholds per group so that TPR and FPR are equal. |
| **Threshold Adjustment** | Uses different decision thresholds for different groups. |
| **Output Relabeling** | Flips a small fraction of predictions to satisfy fairness constraints. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet – Fairness Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used throughout:**
> - $S$ = Sensitive/Protected attribute (e.g., $S \in \{0, 1\}$)
> - $Y$ = True label (1 = positive outcome)
> - $\hat{Y}$ = Predicted label
> - $P$ = Probability notation
> - $s$ = A specific value of S (e.g., $s = 0$ for unprivileged, $s = 1$ for privileged)

| # | Metric | Mathematical Formula | Interpretation |
|---|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Statistical / Demographic Parity** | $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_1)$ | Positive prediction rate must be equal across groups. |
| 2 | **Disparate Impact (DI)** | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_0)}{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_1)}$ | A value between **0.8 and 1.25** is considered fair (the "80% rule"). |
| 3 | **Equal Opportunity** | $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, S = s_1)$ | True Positive Rate (TPR) must be equal across groups. |
| 4 | **Equalized Odds** | $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, S = s_1)$ for all $y$ | Both TPR and FPR must be equal across groups. |
| 5 | **Predictive Parity** | $P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1, S = s_0) = P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1, S = s_1)$ | Precision must be equal across groups. |
| 6 | **Predictive Equality** | $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 0, S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 0, S = s_1)$ | False Positive Rate (FPR) must be equal across groups. |
| 7 | **Counterfactual Fairness** | $P(\hat{Y}_{S \leftarrow s}(U) = y \mid X = x, S = s) = P(\hat{Y}_{S \leftarrow s'}(U) = y \mid X = x, S = s)$ | Prediction for an individual must remain the same in a counterfactual world where S is flipped. |
| 8 | **Individual Fairness** | $d(\hat{Y}(x_i), \hat{Y}(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ | Similar individuals receive similar predictions (Lipschitz condition). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova, 2017 / Kleinberg et al., 2016):** Except in degenerate cases, **calibration**, **equalized odds**, and **demographic parity cannot all hold simultaneously** when base rates differ across groups. KTU expects students to know this trade-off exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Disparate Impact from First Principles

The **Disparate Impact (DI)** ratio is the most widely used group fairness metric. Let us derive its fairness threshold step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Positive Prediction Rates

Let $R_s$ denote the positive prediction rate for group $s$:

$$R_s = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s)$$

For a binary sensitive attribute ($S \in \{0, 1\}$), we have:

$$R_0 = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = 0)$$

$$R_1 = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = 1)$$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio

The Disparate Impact is defined as the ratio of the unprivileged group's positive rate to the privileged group's positive rate:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{R_0}{R_1}$$

### Step 3: Perfect Fairness Condition

If both groups receive positive predictions at exactly the same rate, $R_0 = R_1$, and therefore:

$$\text{DI}_{\text{fair}} = \frac{R_0}{R_1} = 1$$

### Step 4: The 80% Rule

The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) established the **4/5ths Rule** (also called the 80% rule) for adverse impact:

$$\text{Fair} \iff 0.8 \leq \text{DI} \leq 1.25$$

If $\text{DI} < 0.8$, then the selection rate for the unprivileged group is **less than 80%** of that of the privileged group, indicating **adverse impact** (illegal discrimination).

### Step 5: Numerical Verification Example

> **Worked Example (KTU Typical 7-mark style):**
>
> In a hiring dataset, 1000 males were shortlisted, and 8000 males applied. 200 females were shortlisted, and 4000 females applied.

Positive prediction rates:

$$R_{\text{male}} = \frac{1000}{8000} = 0.125$$

$$R_{\text{female}} = \frac{200}{4000} = 0.05$$

Now compute the Disparate Impact:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{R_{\text{female}}}{R_{\text{male}}} = \frac{0.05}{0.125}$$

$$\text{DI} = 0.40$$

Since $0.40 < 0.80$, the system exhibits **adverse impact against females**. To remediate, we can either:
1. Increase female selection rate to $\geq 0.80 \times 0.125 = 0.10$ (i.e., 400+ female selections), or
2. Decrease male selection rate (less desirable).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Equal Opportunity from the Confusion Matrix

**Equal Opportunity** requires that the True Positive Rate (TPR) is the same across groups. TPR is also called **Sensitivity** or **Recall**.

### Step 1: Define TPR

$$\text{TPR}_s = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, S = s) = \frac{\text{TP}_s}{\text{TP}_s + \text{FN}_s}$$

### Step 2: Equal Opportunity Constraint

$$\text{TPR}_0 = \text{TPR}_1$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\text{TP}_0}{\text{TP}_0 + \text{FN}_0} = \frac{\text{TP}_1}{\text{TP}_1 + \text{FN}_1}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Verification

| Group | TP | FN | TPR |
|-------|----|----|------|
| Female ($s=0$) | 80 | 20 | 0.80 |
| Male ($s=1$) | 90 | 10 | 0.90 |

$$\text{TPR}_0 = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = 0.80$$

$$\text{TPR}_1 = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = 0.90$$

Since $0.80 \neq 0.90$, **Equal Opportunity is violated**. Remediation via threshold adjustment:

$$\hat{Y}_{\text{female}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if probability} \geq 0.40 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$\hat{Y}_{\text{male}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if probability} \geq 0.50 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Lowering the threshold for the unprivileged group increases its TPR.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Reweighing (Preprocessing) Weight Formula

In the **Reweighing** algorithm (Kamiran & Calders, 2012), each training instance $(x, y)$ gets a weight $W(x, y, s)$ such that the **expected outcome** becomes independent of $S$.

### Step 1: Expected Outcome

We want $P(Y = 1, S = s)$ under the weighted distribution to equal the **product of marginals**:

$$P_{\text{exp}}(Y = 1, S = s) = P(Y = 1) \cdot P(S = s)$$

### Step 2: Observed Probability

The observed joint probability:

$$P_{\text{obs}}(Y = 1, S = s) = \frac{\text{count}(Y = 1, S = s)}{N}$$

### Step 3: Reweighing Factor

The weight assigned is the ratio:

$$W(y, s) = \frac{P_{\text{exp}}(Y = y, S = s)}{P_{\text{obs}}(Y = y, S = s)}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Suppose total samples $N = 1000$. Counts:

| | $S = 0$ (Female) | $S = 1$ (Male) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Y = 1$ | 50 | 450 | 500 |
| $Y = 0$ | 150 | 350 | 500 |
| Total | 200 | 800 | 1000 |

Marginal probabilities:

$$P(Y = 1) = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5$$

$$P(S = 0) = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2$$

$$P(Y = 1, S = 0) = \frac{50}{1000} = 0.05$$

Expected joint (under independence):

$$P_{\text{exp}}(Y = 1, S = 0) = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.10$$

Reweighing factor:

$$W(Y=1, S=0) = \frac{0.10}{0.05} = 2.0$$

**Interpretation:** Female-positive samples are **under-represented** (their observed frequency is half of the expected frequency). So each such sample's weight is **doubled** during training, making the model treat them as if they were balanced.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – EDA Checklist for Fairness

Before training, run this checklist:

| # | Check | Statistical Test | Red Flag |
|---|-------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | Class balance per group | $\chi^2$ test | Any group < 20% of dataset |
| 2 | Missing value pattern | Little's MCAR test | Missingness depends on S |
| 3 | Feature distribution shift | KS test | $p < 0.05$ between groups |
| 4 | Correlation with S | Point-biserial | $\vert r \vert > 0.3$ |
| 5 | Label consistency | Cohen's kappa | $\kappa < 0.7$ |
| 6 | Proxy identification | Mutual information | High MI with S |
| 7 | Base rate parity | Ratio | $\dfrac{P(Y=1 \mid S=0)}{P(Y=1 \mid S=1)} < 0.8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This matrix is used in fairness audits before model deployment, especially in high-stakes domains like **loan approval (BFSI)**, **hiring (HR Tech)**, and **medical diagnosis (Healthcare AI)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – The Complete Bias Mitigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Dataset with Bias"] --> B["Exploratory Data Analysis"]
    B --> C{"Bias Detected?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Preprocessing Stage"]
    C -- No --> H["Train Model"]
    D --> D1["Reweighing"]
    D --> D2["Sampling"]
    D --> D3["Suppression"]
    D --> D4["Fair Representations"]
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    D4 --> H
    H --> I{"Fairness During Training?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Inprocessing Stage"]
    J --> J1["Adversarial Debiasing"]
    J --> J2["Fairness Regularization"]
    J --> J3["Constrained Optimization"]
    J1 --> K["Trained Model"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L{"Fairness Constraints Satisfied?"}
    L -- No --> M["Postprocessing Stage"]
    M --> M1["Threshold Adjustment"]
    M --> M2["Reject Option Classification"]
    M --> M3["Calibrated Equalized Odds"]
    M1 --> N["Final Fair Predictions"]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    L -- Yes --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Sources of Bias Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1["Historical Bias - Societal pre-existing inequality"] --> R["Root Causes"]
    S2["Representation Bias - Sampling skew"] --> R
    S3["Measurement Bias - Inconsistent labeling"] --> R
    S4["Aggregation Bias - Subgroup heterogeneity"] --> R
    R --> DATA["Biased Dataset"]
    DATA --> EDA["Exploratory Data Analysis"]
    EDA -->|"Detect & Quantify"| MIT["Mitigation Pipeline"]
    MIT --> OUT["Fair AI System"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Fairness Metric Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["What is the application?"] --> Q2{"High-stakes decision?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3["Use Equalized Odds"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4["Use Demographic Parity"]
    Q1 --> Q5{"Is individual-level fairness required?"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6["Use Counterfactual Fairness"]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7{"Are base rates similar across groups?"}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8["Calibration + Equalized Odds feasible"]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9["Trade-off inevitable: choose priority"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Preprocessing Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Input: Biased Training Data"] --> MOD1["Sensitive Attribute Identifier"]
    MOD1 --> MOD2["Group Statistics Calculator"]
    MOD2 --> MOD3["Weight Assignment: Reweighing"]
    MOD3 --> MOD4["Feature Transformation"]
    MOD4 --> MOD5["Output: Debiased Training Set"]
    MOD4 --> MOD5
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Inprocessing Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Input: Debiased Data"] --> P2["Main Predictor Network"]
    P2 --> P3["Predictions Y-hat"]
    P3 --> P4["Adversary Network"]
    P4 --> P5{"Adversary can predict S?"}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6["Update Predictor to fool Adversary"]
    P5 -- No --> P7["Fairness Achieved: Stop Training"]
    P6 --> P2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🅰️ PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define "Disparate Impact" in the context of AI fairness. State the threshold value below which adverse impact is identified.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Disparate Impact (DI)** is a group fairness metric that measures the ratio of positive prediction rates between the unprivileged and privileged groups.
>
> $$\text{DI} = \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_0)}{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_1)}$$
>
> According to the **4/5ths Rule (80% Rule)** established by the U.S. EEOC, adverse impact is identified when $\text{DI} < 0.80$ (or $\text{DI} > 1.25$ by symmetry). At this threshold, the selection rate for the unprivileged group is less than 80% of that of the privileged group, indicating potential systemic discrimination.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **Preprocessing** and **Postprocessing** bias mitigation techniques. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Preprocessing | Postprocessing |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Stage** | Applied **before** model training | Applied **after** model training |
| **What is modified** | The training **data** | The model's **predictions** |
| **Example** | **Reweighing** — assigning sample weights to neutralize group disparities | **Reject Option Classification** — flipping predictions near the decision boundary in favor of the unprivileged group |

---

## 🅱️ PART B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a)** Explain the various **sources of bias** in AI systems with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** in detecting bias. List and explain any four EDA techniques used for fairness auditing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

The major sources of bias in AI systems are:

1. **Historical Bias:** Pre-existing societal inequities reflected in data.
   - *Example:* Amazon's recruiting engine (2018) downranked resumes containing words like "women's chess club" because the training data reflected a male-dominated tech industry.

2. **Representation Bias:** Certain demographic groups are under- or over-represented.
   - *Example:* Face recognition systems (e.g., Joy Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018) showed 34.7% error rate for darker-skinned females vs. 0.8% for lighter-skinned males.

3. **Measurement Bias:** Inconsistent measurement of features or labels across groups.
   - *Example:* Using arrest records as a proxy for criminality is biased because policing intensity varies by neighborhood.

4. **Aggregation Bias:** Applying one global model to heterogeneous subgroups.
   - *Example:* Diabetes prediction models trained on aggregated data often fail for adolescent subgroups.

5. **Learning Bias:** Bias introduced by the algorithm itself.
   - *Example:* A neural network learning spurious correlations between accent and employability.

6. **Evaluation Bias:** Benchmark datasets not representing deployment populations.
   - *Example:* NLP sentiment models benchmarked only on Standard American English failing on Indian English.

7. **Deployment Bias:** Model used outside its intended context.
   - *Example:* COMPAS used for pre-trial bail despite being designed for sentencing.

> *(Note: For 7 marks, students should cover at least 4–5 sources with examples — each source carrying ~1.5 marks.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** is the critical first step in Responsible AI. It involves summarizing and visualizing data to detect patterns of bias **before** training begins. Its roles include:

- Detecting class imbalance across protected groups.
- Identifying non-random missing data.
- Uncovering proxy variables for sensitive attributes.
- Validating dataset representativeness.

**Four EDA Techniques for Fairness Auditing:**

1. **Frequency Tables (`value_counts`)** — Tabulate class distribution per protected group. If a group constitutes less than 20% of samples, representation bias is flagged.

2. **Correlation Heatmap** — Compute Pearson/Spearman correlation between protected attribute $S$ and other features. Any $\vert r \vert > 0.3$ signals potential proxy discrimination.

3. **Group-wise Statistical Comparison** — Compare mean, median, and standard deviation of features across privileged and unprivileged groups using the **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test**. A $p < 0.05$ indicates significant distributional shift.

4. **Missing-Value Heatmap** — Visualize the pattern of missing data. If missingness depends on $S$, it indicates **MAR (Missing At Random)** with respect to protected attributes, requiring imputation strategies that do not introduce additional bias.

> *(Each technique carries ~1.5 marks; together with introduction ~7 marks.)*

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss **Preprocessing, Inprocessing, and Postprocessing** techniques used to mitigate bias in AI systems. Give at least two examples for each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Statistical Parity**, **Equal Opportunity**, and **Equalized Odds** as fairness metrics. State one scenario where each would be the most appropriate metric. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

The bias mitigation pipeline operates at three distinct stages:

**1. Preprocessing (Data-level):** Modifies the training data so that bias is neutralized before model training.

- **Reweighing (Kamiran & Calders, 2012):** Assigns weights $W(y, s)$ to training instances so that the weighted distribution becomes independent of the sensitive attribute.
- **Sampling Techniques:** Oversampling minority groups (e.g., SMOTE for the unprivileged class) or undersampling the majority group.
- **Suppression:** Removes the protected attribute $S$ and its strongest proxies from the feature set.
- **Learning Fair Representations (LFR):** Maps data to a latent space where $S$ cannot be recovered but utility is preserved.

**2. Inprocessing (Algorithm-level):** Modifies the learning algorithm to incorporate fairness constraints directly.

- **Adversarial Debiasing:** Trains a predictor and an adversary network simultaneously; the adversary attempts to predict $S$ from $\hat{Y}$, and the predictor learns to fool it.
- **Fairness Regularization:** Adds a penalty term to the loss function:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{accuracy}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}$$

- **Constrained Optimization:** Maximizes accuracy subject to a demographic parity constraint $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=1) \leq \epsilon$.

**3. Postprocessing (Output-level):** Adjusts predictions after the model has been trained.

- **Reject Option Classification:** For uncertain predictions (near the decision boundary), the prediction is flipped in favor of the unprivileged group.
- **Threshold Adjustment:** Uses different decision thresholds per group to equalize TPR or FPR.
- **Calibrated Equalized Odds:** Adjusts thresholds so that both TPR and FPR are equalized across groups.

> *(2 techniques per stage × ~1 mark + 1 mark for overview = 7 marks.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**1. Statistical Parity (Demographic Parity):**

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = s_1)$$

- The positive prediction rate is independent of $S$.
- **Best suited for:** **Hiring and college admissions** where the goal is proportional representation of demographic groups in the selected pool, regardless of underlying base rates.

**2. Equal Opportunity:**

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, S = s_1)$$

- Among those who **deserve** the positive outcome ($Y=1$), the model must predict positively at equal rates across groups.
- **Best suited for:** **Loan approval systems** where, among truly creditworthy individuals, the model must not favor one demographic group over another.

**3. Equalized Odds:**

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, S = s_0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, S = s_1), \quad \forall y \in \{0, 1\}$$

- Both TPR and FPR are equal across groups.
- **Best suited for:** **Medical diagnosis systems** where both missed diagnoses (FN) and false alarms (FP) carry serious consequences and must be balanced across patient demographics.

> *(Each metric: definition ~1.5 marks + formula ~0.5 marks + scenario ~0.5 marks ≈ 7 marks total.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Equal Opportunity with Equalized Odds:** Equal Opportunity only requires TPR equality. Equalized Odds requires both TPR **and** FPR equality. Mixing these up loses ~2 marks.
>
> 2. **Using Disparate Impact incorrectly:** Some students write the privileged group's rate in the numerator. The correct convention is **unprivileged ÷ privileged**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 80% Rule threshold:** Simply stating DI without mentioning the 0.8–1.25 range is incomplete and costs marks.
>
> 4. **Not giving real-world examples:** KTU ESE marks explicitly require **examples**. A bias source without an example = 0.5–1 mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Conflating Inprocessing and Postprocessing:** Inprocessing modifies the **model training loop**, not the data or predictions. Students often blur this boundary.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Impossibility Theorem:** In long-answer questions about fairness metrics, examiners expect students to acknowledge that **no single metric can guarantee all notions of fairness simultaneously**. Omitting this shows shallow understanding.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bias** is a systematic error favoring certain groups; **Fairness** is the mitigation of that error.
- ✅ There are **7 major sources of bias**: Historical, Representation, Measurement, Aggregation, Learning, Evaluation, Deployment.
- ✅ **EDA** is the first defense: detect imbalance, missing patterns, proxies, and correlations before training.
- ✅ **Dataset limitations** include small sample sizes, sampling bias, label noise, missing protected attributes, and proxy variables.
- ✅ Bias mitigation has **3 stages**: **Preprocessing** (fix data), **Inprocessing** (fix algorithm), **Postprocessing** (fix outputs).
- ✅ **Disparate Impact (DI)** uses the **80% Rule**: $0.8 \leq \text{DI} \leq 1.25$.
- ✅ **Statistical Parity** = equal positive rates; **Equal Opportunity** = equal TPR; **Equalized Odds** = equal TPR + FPR.
- ✅ **Impossibility Theorem:** All fairness metrics cannot hold simultaneously when base rates differ.
- ✅ Always write formulas using **LaTeX** and never inside table pipes.
- ✅ For 7-mark answers, use the **Name → Definition → Example** structure.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, structure as **(a)** and **(b)** with clear sub-headings and balanced depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpretability and Explainability: Interpretability through Simplification and Visualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interpretability and Explainability: Interpretability through Simplification and Visualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Interpretability and Explainability

In the context of **Responsible AI (RAI)**, the terms *interpretability* and *explainability* are often used interchangeably, but KTU 2024 Scheme distinguishes them with surgical precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretability** is the degree to which a human can **consistently predict** the model's behavior and understand the cause-and-effect relationship between input features and model outputs **without requiring additional post-hoc tools**. It is an **intrinsic property** of the model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explainability** refers to the set of **post-hoc techniques and methods** that attempt to summarize the reasons behind a model's predictions in human-understandable terms, especially for *black-box* models (e.g., Deep Neural Networks, Large Language Models). It is an **external, derived property**.

### Mathematical/Conceptual Statement

Let the model be a function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ mapping from input space to output space.

- **Interpretability** demands that for any $x \in \mathcal{X}$, the user can mathematically trace $f(x)$ via transparent internal parameters $\theta$.
- **Explainability** introduces an external explanation function $g(f, x)$ that produces a surrogate, human-readable representation of the decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST752 Module 2):** A high-yield exam point is the *contrast* between intrinsic transparency and post-hoc explanation. Memorize this distinction — questions of 3–7 marks are directly framed on this comparison.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Glass Box vs. The Oracle

Imagine two doctors:

- **Doctor A (Interpretable Model):** Uses a simple flowchart — *"If BP > 140 AND Cholesterol > 240 → Prescribe Statin."* You can **see the reasoning** because the internal logic is exposed. This is a **linear regression or decision tree**.
- **Doctor B (Black-Box Model with Explainability):** Is a genius neurologist who consults 10,000 patient files in seconds and prescribes a drug. You **cannot see the internal logic**, but after the fact, the doctor points to a specific X-ray region and says *"This shadow influenced my decision."* This is a **CNN with Grad-CAM saliency maps**.

| Property | Interpretability (Doctor A) | Explainability (Doctor B) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Built into the model | Added on top of the model |
| User Trust | Passive / Structural | Active / Inferred |
| Example Model | Linear Regression, Decision Tree | Deep Network + SHAP/LIME |
| Audit Cost | Low | High |
| Accuracy Trade-off | Usually lower | Usually higher |

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of "Interpretability through Simplification"

Simplification-based interpretability rests on three foundational pillars that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Inheritable Transparency** — The model itself is simple (linear, tree, rule-based).
2. **Local Surrogate Approximation** — A complex model is locally approximated by a simple one (e.g., LIME).
3. **Global Knowledge Distillation** — A simple student model is trained to mimic a complex teacher model (e.g., Decision Tree approximation of a Neural Net).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of "Interpretability through Visualization"

Visualization-based interpretability leverages human visual cognition to expose model internals:

1. **Feature-Level Visualization** — Saliency maps, heatmaps, attribution overlays.
2. **Model-Level Visualization** — Partial Dependence Plots, ICE plots, decision boundaries.
3. **Dimensionality-Reduction Visualization** — t-SNE, UMAP, PCA projections of learned embeddings.

> [!NOTE]
> These 6 pillars form the spine of Module 2. Any Part B (14 mark) question will combine a theoretical pillar with an applied visualization technique.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interpretability through Simplification — The Three Operational Layers

### Layer 1: Inherently Simple Models
These are models whose mathematical structure is human-readable **by design**.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Linear Regression:** $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$. The user directly reads coefficients $\beta_i$ to quantify the influence of each feature $x_i$. A positive $\beta_i$ means increasing $x_i$ raises $y$.
2. **Logistic Regression:** $P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(\beta^T x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\beta^T x}}$. The log-odds are linear, so coefficients are still interpretable as multiplicative risk factors.
3. **Decision Trees:** Recursively partition the feature space using axis-aligned splits. Each internal node is an `if-then-else` rule, and each leaf is a class label or regression value.
4. **Rule-Based Systems (OneR, RIPPER):** Disjunctive Normal Form rules such as `(Age > 30) AND (Income > 50K) → Approve Loan`.

### Layer 2: Local Surrogate Models (LIME)
Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations approximate the complex model's behavior **only in a small neighborhood** of a specific input.

**Operational steps of LIME:**

1. Select the instance $x$ to be explained.
2. Generate a perturbed dataset $\mathcal{Z}$ around $x$ by sampling from a normal/uniform distribution.
3. Obtain predictions $f(z)$ for every $z \in \mathcal{Z}$ using the black-box model.
4. Weight each $z$ by its **proximity to $x$** using a kernel function, typically an exponential kernel:
   $$ \pi_x(z) = \exp\left(-\frac{D(x,z)^2}{\sigma^2}\right) $$
   where $D(x,z)$ is the distance (e.g., cosine for text, Euclidean for tabular) and $\sigma$ is the kernel width.
5. Train an interpretable surrogate $g \in G$ (e.g., linear model or decision tree) on the weighted perturbations by minimizing the loss:
   $$ \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \pi_x(z) \cdot \left(f(z) - g(z)\right)^2 $$
6. Return $g$ as the local explanation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** The complex model's *global* decision surface may be wildly nonlinear, but over a tiny local region, it is approximately linear. LIME exploits this **manifold linearity assumption**.

### Layer 3: Global Surrogate Models (Knowledge Distillation)
Train an interpretable model to mimic a complex model **over the entire input space**.

**Steps:**

1. Train the complex "teacher" model $f_{teacher}$ on the training data.
2. Use $f_{teacher}$ to generate predictions (soft labels) on a large unlabeled dataset.
3. Train the interpretable "student" model $f_{student}$ to match these soft labels.
4. The student inherits the predictive power of the teacher but retains human-readable structure.

## 2.2 Interpretability through Visualization — The Engineering Toolkit

### Technique 1: Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)
A PDP shows the **marginal effect** of one or two features on the predicted outcome, integrating out the influence of all other features.

### Technique 2: Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE) Plots
ICE plots are the **disaggregated counterpart** of PDP. Each curve represents a single instance, showing how its prediction changes as one feature varies.

### Technique 3: Saliency Maps (Pixel-Level Attribution)
For deep networks on images, saliency maps compute the gradient of the class score $S_c$ with respect to the input pixel $x_{ij}$:
$$ M_{ij} = \left| \frac{\partial S_c}{\partial x_{ij}} \right| $$
High gradient magnitude indicates the pixel strongly influences the prediction.

### Technique 4: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)
Rooted in cooperative game theory, SHAP assigns each feature a contribution value $\phi_i$ summing to the total prediction deviation from the baseline.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you need for Module 2 exam calculations. Do not confuse the kernel width $\sigma$ in LIME with the standard deviation in statistics — they are unrelated parameters.

| # | Concept | Equation / Metric | Boundary / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Model | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i$ | $\beta_i \in \mathbb{R}$ | Intrinsic interpretability |
| 2 | LIME Proximity Kernel | $\pi_x(z) = \exp(-D(x,z)^2 / \sigma^2)$ | $\sigma > 0$, dimensionless | Local weight assignment |
| 3 | LIME Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_z \pi_x(z) (f(z) - g(z))^2$ | Sum is non-negative | Training surrogate $g$ |
| 4 | PDP Marginal Function | $\bar{f}_S(x_S) = \mathbb{E}_{X_C} [f(x_S, X_C)]$ | $S$ = target set, $C$ = complement | Global feature effect |
| 5 | ICE Curve | $f^{(i)}_{ICE}(x_S) = f(x_S, x^{(i)}_C)$ | One curve per instance | Heterogeneity detection |
| 6 | Saliency Magnitude | $M_{ij} = \vert \partial S_c / \partial x_{ij} \vert$ | Pixel-wise, normalized [0,1] | Image attribution |
| 7 | SHAP Shapley Value | $\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert ! (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert !} [f(S \cup \{i\}) - f(S)]$ | $\sum_i \phi_i = f(x) - f(\emptyset)$ | Local attribution |
| 8 | Fidelity Metric | $\text{Fid} = 1 - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \vert f(x_k) - g(x_k) \vert$ | $0 \leq Fid \leq 1$ | Surrogate quality |
| 9 | t-SNE Probability | $p_{ij} = \frac{\exp(-\vert x_i - x_j \vert^2 / 2\sigma^2)}{\sum_{k \neq l} \exp(-\vert x_k - x_l \vert^2 / 2\sigma^2)}$ | Symmetric, $\sum p_{ij} = 1$ | Embedding visualization |
| 10 | UMAP Cross-Entropy | $CE = \sum_{i,j} [p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}} + (1 - p_{ij}) \log \frac{1 - p_{ij}}{1 - q_{ij}}]$ | Optimized during training | Embedding preservation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare (Diagnostic AI):** Saliency maps on CT scans allow radiologists to verify the model is focusing on pathological tissue and not on hospital letterhead.
- **Finance (Loan/Credit Scoring):** SHAP values satisfy the EU GDPR "right to explanation" by quantifying each applicant's contribution to their credit score.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** ICE plots reveal how a self-driving car's brake decisions vary across different pedestrian densities.
- **Cybersecurity:** LIME on a malware classifier explains why a specific executable was flagged, helping analysts validate the reasoning.
- **Agriculture:** PDPs on crop yield predictors show the marginal influence of nitrogen, rainfall, and pH, guiding farmer decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note:** Real-world applications are *high-mark yielders* in Part B answers. Always tie theory to a domain (healthcare, finance, defense) to earn the application/utility marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shapley Value Attribution (SHAP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most mathematically dense derivation in Module 2. KTU examiners often ask for either the formula or the four axiomatic properties (Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, Additivity). Memorize both.

The Shapley value $\phi_i$ for feature $i$ is derived from cooperative game theory. We treat each feature as a "player" in a coalition whose payout is the model prediction $f(x)$.

### Step 1: Define the Set of Features
Let $F = \{1, 2, \dots, M\}$ be the set of all $M$ features. We consider every subset $S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}$ that does not contain feature $i$.

### Step 2: Define the Value Function
For any subset $S$, define $v(S)$ as the expected model output when only features in $S$ are known. The marginal contribution of adding feature $i$ to subset $S$ is:
$$ \Delta_i(S) = v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S) $$

**Explanation:** This measures how much the prediction improves when feature $i$ "joins" the coalition $S$.

### Step 3: Average Over All Permutations
The number of ways to order $M$ features is $M!$. When feature $i$ is placed at a specific position, the players before it form a subset $S$ of size $\vert S \vert = k$, where $0 \leq k \leq M-1$. The number of subsets of size $k$ from $F \setminus \{i\}$ is $\binom{M-1}{k}$.

Therefore, the probability that a random permutation has exactly $k$ players before feature $i$ is:
$$ P(k) = \frac{k! (M - k - 1)!}{M!} $$

### Step 4: Apply the Weighting
Multiplying the marginal contribution by this probability and summing over all subset sizes:
$$ \phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert ! (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \cdot [v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)] $$

In cleaner aligned form:
$$ \phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \, (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \bigl[ v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S) \bigr] $$

### Step 5: Efficiency Property
A remarkable property is that Shapley values sum to the total prediction deviation:
$$ \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i = v(F) - v(\emptyset) $$

**Explanation:** Feature $i$'s attribution is its *average marginal contribution* across all possible coalitions. This is the *unique* attribution scheme satisfying four axioms: efficiency, symmetry, dummy, and additivity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Partial Dependence Function from First Principles

### Step 1: Setup
Let the feature set be partitioned into $S$ (the features we want to visualize) and $C$ (the remaining features). Let $x_S$ be a value we fix for the target features.

### Step 2: Define the Joint Function
The model's output for a complete input is $f(x_S, x_C)$, where $x_C$ are the complement features.

### Step 3: Marginalize Over $C$
To isolate the effect of $x_S$, we average over the marginal distribution of $x_C$:
$$ \bar{f}_S(x_S) = \mathbb{E}_{X_C} \left[ f(x_S, X_C) \right] $$

### Step 4: Approximate by Monte Carlo
In practice, the expectation is approximated using a sample of $N$ training instances:
$$ \bar{f}_S(x_S) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_S, x_C^{(j)}) $$

**Explanation:** PDP assumes **independence** between $S$ and $C$. If they are correlated, the plot can produce unrealistic samples, which is a known limitation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LIME Loss Minimization Path

### Step 1: Generate Perturbations
Given instance $x$, create $N$ perturbed samples $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_N$ by sampling from a Gaussian noise distribution $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ added to $x$.

### Step 2: Compute Black-Box Predictions
For each $z_k$, obtain $f(z_k)$ using the complex model.

### Step 3: Compute Distance Weights
$$ w_k = \pi_x(z_k) = \exp\left( -\frac{\vert\vert x - z_k \vert\vert_2^2}{\sigma^2} \right) $$

### Step 4: Weighted Linear Regression
For a linear surrogate $g(z) = w_0 + \sum w_i z_i$, solve the weighted least squares:
$$ \min_{w} \mathcal{L}(w) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \cdot \left( f(z_k) - g(z_k) \right)^2 $$

### Step 5: Closed-Form Solution
The optimal weights are given by the normal equation:
$$ w^* = (Z^T W Z)^{-1} Z^T W f(Z) $$

where $W$ is the diagonal weight matrix with $W_{kk} = w_k$ and $Z$ is the matrix of perturbed samples.

**Explanation:** The coefficients $w^*$ form the local explanation. A large positive $w_i^*$ means increasing feature $i$ locally raises the prediction.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Simplification Techniques

| Method | Model-Agnostic? | Local / Global | Computation | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | No (intrinsic) | Global | $O(nd)$ | Baseline tabular data |
| Decision Tree | No (intrinsic) | Global | $O(nd \log n)$ | Rule extraction, audit |
| LIME | Yes | Local | $O(N \cdot d^3)$ | Explaining single prediction |
| SHAP (Kernel) | Yes | Local | $O(2^M)$ exact, $O(NM^2)$ approx | Fairness, credit scoring |
| Anchors | Yes | Local | $O(N)$ rules | High-precision rules |
| Global Surrogate | Yes | Global | $O(N \cdot d)$ | Auditing ensemble models |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Visualization Techniques

| Method | Modality | Output | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| PDP | Tabular | 1D / 2D curve | Ignores feature correlation |
| ICE | Tabular | Multiple 1D curves | Cluttered for large $N$ |
| Saliency Map | Image | 2D heatmap | Suffers from saturation |
| Grad-CAM | Image | Class activation map | Coarse resolution |
| t-SNE | Any | 2D scatter | Distorts global structure |
| UMAP | Any | 2D / 3D scatter | Stochastic, parameter-sensitive |
| Activation Atlas | Image | Grid of feature visualizations | Requires very deep nets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Framework: The Interpretability-Explainability Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[AI Model] --> nodeB{Is the model inherently simple?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Interpretability via Simplification]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD[Explainability via Post-Hoc Tools]
    nodeC --> nodeE1[Linear Models]
    nodeC --> nodeE2[Decision Trees]
    nodeC --> nodeE3[Rule Lists]
    nodeD --> nodeF1[Local Surrogates LIME]
    nodeD --> nodeF2[Attribution SHAP]
    nodeD --> nodeF3[Counterfactuals]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Visualization Aids]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH1[PDP and ICE]
    nodeG --> nodeH2[Saliency and Grad-CAM]
    nodeG --> nodeH3[t-SNE and UMAP]
    nodeE1 --> nodeOut[Human Understanding]
    nodeE2 --> nodeOut
    nodeE3 --> nodeOut
    nodeF1 --> nodeOut
    nodeF2 --> nodeOut
    nodeF3 --> nodeOut
    nodeH1 --> nodeOut
    nodeH2 --> nodeOut
    nodeH3 --> nodeOut
```

## 4.2 LIME Local Surrogation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput[Original Instance x] --> nodePerturb[Generate Perturbations Z]
    nodePerturb --> nodePredict[Black-Box Predictions f z]
    nodePredict --> nodeWeight[Distance Weighted Kernel pi x z]
    nodeWeight --> nodeTrain[Train Interpretable Surrogate g]
    nodeTrain --> nodeExplain[Local Explanation g coefficients]
    nodeExplain --> nodeValidate[Evaluate Fidelity]
    nodeValidate -- Low --> nodePerturb
    nodeValidate -- High --> nodeOutput[Return Explanation to User]
```

## 4.3 SHAP Attribution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Features x1 x2 ... xM] --> nodeB[Enumerate all 2 to the M coalitions]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Train surrogate on each coalition]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute marginal contribution v S union i minus v S]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Shapley weighting]
    nodeE --> nodeF[phi i for each feature i]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Waterfall Plot Visualization]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Force Plot Visualization]
    nodeG --> nodeUser[User]
    nodeH --> nodeUser
```

## 4.4 PDP vs ICE Visualization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeData[Training Data x_S and x_C] --> nodeGrid[Grid of x_S values]
    nodeGrid --> nodeAvg[Average f over x_C distribution]
    nodeGrid --> nodeTrace[One line per instance]
    nodeAvg --> nodePDP[Partial Dependence Plot]
    nodeTrace --> nodeICE[ICE Plot with many curves]
    nodePDP --> nodeCompare[Compare average trend with individual heterogeneity]
    nodeICE --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare --> nodeDecision[Feature Engineering and Model Diagnosis]
```

## 4.5 Saliency Map and Grad-CAM Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput[Input Image I] --> nodeCNN[Convolutional Neural Network]
    nodeCNN --> nodeScore[Class Score S_c]
    nodeScore --> nodeGrad[Compute partial derivative of S_c with respect to I]
    nodeGrad --> nodeAbs[Take absolute value M_ij]
    nodeAbs --> nodeNorm[Normalize to 0 to 1]
    nodeNorm --> nodeOverlay[Overlay heatmap on original image]
    nodeOverlay --> nodeCAM[Grad-CAM uses final conv layer feature maps A_k]
    nodeCAM --> nodeWeights[Weights alpha_k equals GAP of partial derivative of S_c with respect to A_k]
    nodeWeights --> nodeHeatmap[L ReLU of sum of alpha_k A_k]
    nodeHeatmap --> nodeOverlay
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree Simplification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Complex Black-Box Model] --> nodeB[Generate Predictions on Validation Set]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Train Shallow Decision Tree Student]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Pruning needed?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Apply Reduced Error Pruning]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[Extract If-Then Rules]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Rule Surrogate g]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Fidelity Check vs Teacher]
    nodeH -- High --> nodeI[Deploy Surrogate]
    nodeH -- Low --> nodeC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between *Interpretability* and *Explainability* in the context of Responsible AI. Provide one example model for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Interpretability (1.5 marks):** It is an *intrinsic* property of a model that allows a human to understand its internal decision-making logic without external aids. The model's structure is transparent by design. *Example:* A Decision Tree, where each split condition is a readable `if-then-else` rule.
- **Explainability (1.5 marks):** It is a *post-hoc* process applied to a black-box model to produce a human-readable summary of its predictions. *Example:* Applying LIME to explain a single prediction of a Deep Neural Network that classifies chest X-rays.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> What is a Partial Dependence Plot (PDP)? State its key mathematical formulation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A PDP visualizes the **marginal effect** of one (or two) feature(s) on the predicted outcome of a machine learning model, by averaging the predictions over the distribution of all other features.
- **Mathematical Formulation (1.5 marks):**
$$ \bar{f}_S(x_S) = \mathbb{E}_{X_C}\bigl[ f(x_S, X_C) \bigr] \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} f\!\left(x_S,\, x_C^{(j)}\right) $$
where $S$ is the target feature set and $C$ is the complement feature set, with $N$ training samples used for the Monte Carlo estimate.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Important KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions on this topic *always* carry an **internal choice**. Attempt any ONE of the following two questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)** framework. Discuss the role of the kernel function in defining locality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **LIME and SHAP** as post-hoc explanation techniques across five parameters: theoretical foundation, locality, computational complexity, consistency, and output type. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Problem Setup (1 mark):**
Given a black-box model $f$ and a specific instance $x$, LIME aims to build an interpretable surrogate $g$ that approximates $f$ **locally** around $x$.

**Step 2 — Perturbation Generation (1.5 marks):**
Generate $N$ perturbed samples $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_N$ around $x$ by adding Gaussian noise:
$$ z_k = x + \epsilon_k, \quad \epsilon_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I) $$

For text, perturbations are random word removals; for images, super-pixels are randomly turned on/off.

**Step 3 — Prediction & Weighting (1.5 marks):**
- Obtain black-box predictions $f(z_k)$ for every perturbation.
- Compute proximity weight using exponential kernel:
$$ \pi_x(z_k) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\| x - z_k \|_2^2}{\sigma^2} \right) $$

The kernel width $\sigma$ controls the **radius of locality** — small $\sigma$ gives a very tight local region; large $\sigma$ approximates a global explanation.

**Step 4 — Surrogate Training (1.5 marks):**
Train an interpretable model (typically sparse linear regression) by minimizing the weighted loss:
$$ \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \pi_x(z_k) \left( f(z_k) - g(z_k) \right)^2 $$

**Step 5 — Output (1 mark):**
The coefficients of $g$ are returned as the local explanation. The student should draw a block diagram (see SECTION 4.2).

**Step 6 — Why locality works (0.5 mark):**
The complex model's decision surface is approximately **linear in a small neighborhood**, justifying the local linear surrogate.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | LIME (3.5 marks) | SHAP (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Foundation | Heuristic, kernel-based local approximation | Cooperative game theory (Shapley values) |
| Locality | Strictly local — defined by kernel radius | Local but with **global consistency** via game-theoretic axioms |
| Computational Complexity | $O(N \cdot d^3)$ — fast | $O(2^M)$ exact — exponential in features |
| Consistency Guarantee | None — different runs may give different explanations | Satisfies **local accuracy, missingness, consistency** |
| Output Type | Coefficient vector of $g$ | $\phi_i$ per feature, summing to prediction deviation |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain **Interpretability through Visualization**. With suitable equations, describe **Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)** and **Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE) plots**. Highlight the key difference between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Saliency Map** in deep learning? Derive the gradient-based saliency map equation for an image classifier and discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):**
Visualization-based interpretability translates abstract model behavior into **visual artifacts** (curves, heatmaps, scatter plots) that leverage human visual reasoning.

**Step 2 — PDP Derivation (2.5 marks):**
For a feature subset $S$ and complement $C$:
$$ \bar{f}_S(x_S) = \mathbb{E}_{X_C}\bigl[ f(x_S, X_C) \bigr] \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} f\!\left(x_S, x_C^{(j)}\right) $$

This curve shows the **average** effect of $x_S$ on the prediction across all instances. **Assumption:** $S$ and $C$ are independent — a known limitation when features are correlated.

**Step 3 — ICE Derivation (2.5 marks):**
For each instance $i$, plot a curve as $x_S$ varies while fixing the other features of that instance:
$$ f^{(i)}_{ICE}(x_S) = f\!\left(x_S, x_C^{(i)}\right) $$

ICE plots show one curve per data point, revealing **heterogeneity** that PDP averages out.

**Step 4 — Key Difference (1 mark):**
PDP shows the **average** marginal effect (single curve, hides heterogeneity). ICE shows **per-instance** effects (many curves, reveals individual variation and interaction effects). ICE is essentially the "disaggregated" version of PDP.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A saliency map is a 2D heatmap overlaid on an input image, where each pixel's intensity indicates how much that pixel contributes to the model's prediction for a specific class.

**Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Expansion (2 marks):**
For a classifier with score function $S_c(I)$ for class $c$ and input image $I$, the first-order Taylor expansion around $I_0$ is:
$$ S_c(I) \approx S_c(I_0) + \left( \frac{\partial S_c}{\partial I} \Big|_{I_0} \right)^T (I - I_0) $$

The gradient $\partial S_c / \partial I$ encodes how a small change in each pixel affects the class score.

**Step 3 — Saliency Map Equation (2 marks):**
$$ M_{ij} = \left| \frac{\partial S_c}{\partial I_{ij}} \right| $$

Pixels with large $M_{ij}$ are the most influential. A typical pipeline normalizes the map to $[0,1]$ and overlays it on the original image.

**Step 4 — Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Gradient saturation:** ReLU and sigmoid functions kill the gradient when saturated, hiding important features.
2. **Noise sensitivity:** Small input perturbations cause wildly different saliency maps.
3. **Class-agnostic behavior:** Gradient may highlight edges relevant to *any* class, not just class $c$.
4. **Resolution:** Vanilla saliency is pixel-level; coarser methods like Grad-CAM are often preferred for object localization.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points (Read carefully before writing your exam):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Intrinsic vs. Post-Hoc (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students incorrectly call LIME an *interpretable model*. It is an **explainability tool** applied to a black-box. Get this right or lose 2 marks instantly.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the Kernel Width Explanation (–1 mark):** When describing LIME, you MUST explain the role of $\sigma$ in the exponential kernel. Examiners specifically test for the link between $\sigma$ and locality radius.
> 
> 3. **Memorizing SHAP Formula Without Axioms (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing $\phi_i$ without mentioning the four Shapley axioms (efficiency, symmetry, dummy, additivity) is incomplete. Examiners look for the *why*, not just the *what*.
> 
> 4. **Confusing PDP with ICE (–2 marks):** Saying "PDP is the same as ICE" is a fatal error. The key distinction is *averaging* (PDP) vs. *per-instance curves* (ICE).
> 
> 5. **Omitting Block Diagram (–1 mark):** In a 7-mark sub-question on LIME architecture, a missing block diagram costs at least 1 mark. Always draw even a rough flowchart.
> 
> 6. **Using Vague Real-World Examples (–0.5 mark):** Saying "this is used in healthcare" is weaker than "this is used in radiology CNNs to highlight tumor regions in CT scans." Be specific.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your exam notes):**

- **Interpretability = Intrinsic Transparency.** Linear models, decision trees, rule lists.
- **Explainability = Post-Hoc Description.** LIME, SHAP, Anchors, counterfactuals.
- **LIME** trains a local linear surrogate weighted by an exponential kernel $\exp(-D^2/\sigma^2)$. Output is the coefficient vector of the surrogate.
- **SHAP** uses Shapley values from cooperative game theory. The 4 axioms are: Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, Additivity. Sum of $\phi_i$ equals total prediction deviation.
- **PDP** computes the marginal effect of a feature by averaging over the complement's distribution. Assumes feature independence.
- **ICE** plots one curve per instance, showing heterogeneity hidden by PDP's averaging.
- **Saliency Map** = absolute gradient of class score w.r.t. input pixel. Suffers from gradient saturation.
- **Grad-CAM** uses the final convolutional feature maps weighted by global-average-pooled gradients. Coarser but more class-discriminative than vanilla saliency.
- **t-SNE and UMAP** are dimensionality-reduction visualizations for learned embeddings. UMAP preserves more global structure than t-SNE.
- **Fidelity** measures how well a surrogate mimics the original model: $\text{Fid} = 1 - \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert f(x_k) - g(x_k) \vert$.
- **Trade-off axis:** Interpretable models sacrifice accuracy for transparency; black-box models sacrifice transparency for accuracy. Post-hoc explainability is the *bridge* between the two.
- **GDPR / EU AI Act** implication: SHAP and LIME help satisfy the *right to explanation* for high-stakes AI decisions.
- **High-yield exam keywords to memorize:** *Surrogate, Kernel Width, Shapley Axioms, Marginal Effect, Feature Correlation Assumption, Gradient Saturation, Manifold Linearity Assumption, Fidelity Score, Local Accuracy, Class Activation Map*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
