<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Ethics in Artificial Intelligence

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Ethics?

**Ethics** is the branch of philosophy that deals with moral principles governing human behavior, distinguishing between right and wrong, good and bad conduct. In the context of Artificial Intelligence, ethics refers to the **systematic study and application of moral values, principles, and guidelines** that govern the design, development, deployment, and use of AI systems to ensure they operate in a manner that is beneficial, fair, safe, and respectful of human rights and dignity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *AI Ethics is a multidisciplinary field concerned with the moral behavior of designers, manufacturers, and users of AI systems. It encompasses the values, principles, and techniques that ensure AI systems operate in a socially responsible, transparent, accountable, and fair manner, while respecting human autonomy, privacy, and well-being.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Thought Experiment:**
> Imagine you are teaching a child to drive. You wouldn't just teach them how to operate the vehicle—you would teach them traffic rules, courtesy toward pedestrians, what to do in emergencies, and when NOT to drive (e.g., when tired). 
>
> Similarly, **AI Ethics** is the "driving school" for intelligent machines. Just as we don't trust a human driver without moral training, we cannot trust AI systems making life-changing decisions (medical diagnosis, loan approvals, criminal sentencing) without embedding ethical guardrails. Ethics ensures AI acts not just *smartly*, but *responsibly*.

### 1.3 Why Ethics in AI is Non-Negotiable

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Scale of Impact** | AI decisions can affect millions simultaneously (e.g., social media algorithms shaping public opinion). |
| **Opacity** | Many AI systems are "black boxes" making it hard to audit decisions. |
| **Autonomy** | AI increasingly acts without human oversight (autonomous weapons, self-driving cars). |
| **Bias Amplification** | AI can perpetuate and scale societal biases present in training data. |
| **Power Asymmetry** | Tech giants wield disproportionate influence through AI deployment. |
| **Irreversibility** | Some AI-driven harms (e.g., mass surveillance) are difficult to undo. |

### 1.4 Core Pillars of AI Ethics (The FAT Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The foundational pillars universally recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
> 1. **F**airness — Equitable treatment across demographic groups.
> 2. **A**ccountability — Clear responsibility for AI outcomes.
> 3. **T**ransparency — Understandable decision-making processes.
> 4. **P**rivacy — Protection of personal data.
> 5. **S**afety — Prevention of harm.
> 6. **H**uman Autonomy — Preserving human agency and control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Classical Ethical Theories Applied to AI

AI ethics is rooted in three classical philosophical traditions. Understanding these is **critical for KTU 14-mark essay questions**.

### 2.1.1 Utilitarianism (Consequentialism)
- **Core Idea:** The morality of an action is determined solely by its **outcomes/consequences**.
- **Principle:** Choose the action that maximizes overall happiness/utility for the greatest number.
- **AI Application Example:** A self-driving car must swerve—hit 1 pedestrian or 5? Utilitarian AI chooses to hit 1 (minimizes total harm).
- **Key Proponent:** **Jeremy Bentham**, **John Stuart Mill**.
- **AI Critique:** Hard to quantify "happiness"; may justify unethical means for "good" ends.

### 2.1.2 Deontological Ethics (Duty-Based)
- **Core Idea:** Actions are right or wrong based on **rules and duties**, regardless of consequences.
- **Principle:** Follow universal moral laws (e.g., "Do not kill," "Do not lie").
- **AI Application Example:** An AI medical system must always tell patients the truth about their diagnosis, even if the news causes distress.
- **Key Proponent:** **Immanuel Kant** (Categorical Imperative).
- **AI Critique:** Rigid rule-following fails in complex, novel moral dilemmas.

### 2.1.3 Virtue Ethics
- **Core Idea:** Focus on the **character and virtues** of the moral agent, not just the action.
- **Principle:** Cultivate virtues (honesty, courage, compassion); act as a virtuous person would.
- **AI Application Example:** A virtuous AI assistant prioritizes honesty over user engagement metrics.
- **Key Proponent:** **Aristotle** (Golden Mean).
- **AI Critique:** Hard to encode virtues as algorithms; lacks universal rules.

### 2.1.4 Rights-Based Ethics
- **Core Idea:** Individuals possess **fundamental rights** that must be respected.
- **Principle:** Actions violating human rights (dignity, privacy, freedom) are inherently wrong.
- **AI Application Example:** Mass facial recognition violates the right to privacy, regardless of crime-reduction benefits.
- **Key Proponent:** **John Locke**, **John Rawls** (Veil of Ignorance).

## 2.2 Major Ethical Frameworks for AI (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 The Four Key AI Ethics Frameworks

| Framework | Year | Core Focus | Key Principles |
|-----------|------|------------|----------------|
| **Asilomar AI Principles** | 2017 | Safe and beneficial AI | 23 principles across research, ethics, values, long-term issues |
| **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** | 2016/2019 | Human well-being, data agency | 8 general principles + standards for transparent autonomous systems |
| **EU Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI** | 2019 | Trustworthy AI | Lawful, Ethical, Robust (3 components); 7 key requirements |
| **OECD AI Principles** | 2019 | Human-centered values | Inclusive growth, transparency, robustness, accountability |

### 2.2.2 The EU's 7 Key Requirements for Trustworthy AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 7 requirements for KTU exams:**
> 1. **Human agency and oversight** — Human-in/on-the-loop control.
> 2. **Technical robustness and safety** — Resilience, accuracy, security.
> 3. **Privacy and data governance** — GDPR compliance, data minimization.
> 4. **Transparency** — Explainability, traceability, communication.
> 5. **Diversity, non-discrimination, and fairness** — Avoid bias, ensure accessibility.
> 6. **Societal and environmental well-being** — Sustainability, social impact.
> 7. **Accountability** — Auditability, redress mechanisms.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | Definition | Example |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Algorithmic Bias** | Systematic errors in AI outputs that unfairly favor/disfavor groups | Amazon's hiring AI downranking women's resumes |
| **Fairness** | Equal treatment across protected attributes (race, gender, age) | COMPAS recidivism algorithm debate |
| **Accountability** | Clear assignment of responsibility for AI decisions | Who is liable when an autonomous car crashes? |
| **Transparency** | Ability to understand how an AI reaches decisions | Right to explanation under GDPR |
| **Privacy** | Right to control personal information | Cambridge Analytica data harvesting |
| **Explainability (XAI)** | Methods to make AI decisions interpretable | LIME, SHAP frameworks |
| **Value Alignment** | Ensuring AI goals align with human values | Stuart Russell's "reward hacking" concern |
| **Moral Responsibility Gap** | Difficulty in assigning blame to AI vs. developers vs. users | Autonomous weapons deployment |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Ownership and jurisdiction over data | Cross-border data transfers |
| **Informed Consent** | Users knowingly agreeing to AI processing | Cookie consent banners |

## 2.4 Ethical Dilemmas in AI — The "Trilemma"

AI often faces a three-way conflict:

$$\text{AI Trilemma} = \text{Capability} \leftrightarrow \text{Alignment} \leftrightarrow \text{Control}$$

- **Capability:** How powerful the AI is.
- **Alignment:** How well its goals match human values.
- **Control:** How well humans can govern its behavior.

You cannot maximize all three simultaneously — improving capability often reduces alignment or control.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethical AI principles are applied across engineering domains:

| Domain | Ethical Application |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Healthcare** | Equitable diagnosis, patient data privacy, informed consent for AI treatment |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Trolley problem solutions, liability assignment, safety standards |
| **Finance** | Fair credit scoring, anti-discrimination in lending, fraud detection ethics |
| **Criminal Justice** | Bias-free risk assessment, transparency in sentencing support |
| **Hiring** | Removing discriminatory filters, diverse training data |
| **Generative AI** | Copyright, deepfakes, misinformation, attribution |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Matrix

## 3.1 The Ethical Decision-Making Framework for AI Systems

When faced with an ethical AI question in KTU exams, follow this **structured analytical approach**:

### Step-by-Step Decision Process

**Step 1: Identify the Ethical Issue**
- What is the AI system doing? Who is affected? What values are at stake?

**Step 2: Identify Stakeholders**
- Map all parties: developers, users, affected populations, society, future generations.

**Step 3: Apply Ethical Lenses**
- Utilitarian view: What maximizes overall benefit?
- Deontological view: What rules/duties apply?
- Virtue view: What would a virtuous actor do?
- Rights view: Which rights are at risk?

**Step 4: Consider the AI Ethics Principles**
- Reference the relevant framework (EU, IEEE, OECD, Asilomar).

**Step 5: Propose Mitigation Strategy**
- Technical: debiasing algorithms, XAI tools, testing.
- Procedural: human oversight, audit trails.
- Policy: regulation, certification, red lines.

**Step 6: Evaluate Trade-offs**
- Document competing values, accept residual uncertainty.

## 3.2 Case Study Analysis: COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) is an AI used by US courts to predict likelihood of re-offending. ProPublica's 2016 investigation found it was biased against African-American defendants.

### Analytical Breakdown Using Ethical Lenses

**Utilitarian Analysis:**

$$\text{Net Utility} = \sum (\text{public safety benefit}) - \sum (\text{harm to wrongly-flagged individuals})$$

- Public safety benefits (accurate predictions reducing crime) vs. harms (unjust incarceration of innocent minorities). COMPAS scored poorly on distributional justice.

**Deontological Analysis:**
- Violates the duty to treat each person as an end-in-themselves, not merely a means to crime reduction.
- Fails the **Categorical Imperative**: would we universalize using racially disparate predictive tools?

**Rights-Based Analysis:**
- Violates the right to equal protection under law.
- Violates due process if defendants cannot challenge opaque algorithmic decisions.

**Virtue Ethics Analysis:**
- A just legal system exhibits virtues of fairness, impartiality, prudence. COMPAS fails the virtue of **impartiality**.

## 3.3 Case Study Analysis: Microsoft Tay (2016)

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **What Happened** | Microsoft launched Tay, a Twitter chatbot, on March 23, 2016. Within 16 hours, malicious users trained it to produce racist, sexist, and offensive tweets. Microsoft shut it down. |
| **Ethical Failure 1** | Insufficient safeguards against adversarial training. |
| **Ethical Failure 2** | No accountability mechanism — developers didn't anticipate misuse. |
| **Ethical Failure 3** | Violated dignity principle by amplifying hate speech. |
| **Lesson** | Ethical AI requires proactive harm prevention, not just reactive shutdowns. |

## 3.4 Comparative Framework Analysis (Analytical Matrix)

| Principle | EU Guidelines | IEEE | Asilomar | OECD |
|-----------|---------------|------|----------|------|
| Transparency | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| Accountability | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| Fairness | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| Safety | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| Privacy | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ❌ Implicit | ❌ Implicit |
| Environmental | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ❌ Implicit | ❌ Implicit |
| Long-term Risk | ❌ Not focused | ❌ Limited | ✅ Major focus | ❌ Limited |
| Human Rights | ✅ Embedded | ✅ Embedded | ✅ Embedded | ✅ Embedded |

## 3.5 Mathematical Representation of Bias

While ethics is conceptual, bias in AI can be quantified using statistical fairness metrics.

### 3.5.1 Demographic Parity

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

where $\hat{Y}$ is the predicted outcome and $A$ is the protected attribute (e.g., gender).

### 3.5.2 Equalized Odds

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 1) \quad \text{for } y \in \{0, 1\}$$

Both metrics are often **mutually exclusive** — improving one may worsen the other (**fairness impossibility theorem**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chouldechova's Theorem (2017):** When base rates differ between groups, it is mathematically impossible to satisfy all three fairness criteria (calibration, equal false positive rates, equal false negative rates) simultaneously.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AI Ethics. List any four core principles of ethical AI.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
AI Ethics is the branch of applied ethics that examines the moral responsibilities of designers, developers, and users of artificial intelligence systems. It establishes principles to ensure AI operates in a manner that is fair, transparent, accountable, and beneficial to humanity.

**Four core principles:**
1. **Fairness** — AI must treat all individuals equitably, avoiding discriminatory outcomes.
2. **Transparency** — AI decisions should be explainable and understandable.
3. **Accountability** — Clear responsibility must be assigned for AI outcomes.
4. **Privacy** — Personal data must be protected and used with consent.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Utilitarian and Deontological ethics with an AI example.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Utilitarianism | Deontology |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **Basis** | Consequences/outcomes | Rules and duties |
| **Focus** | Greatest good for greatest number | Universal moral laws |
| **Decision Rule** | Maximize net utility | Follow duty regardless of outcome |
| **AI Example** | Self-driving car minimizing total casualties (1 vs 5) | AI medical system must always tell the truth to patients |
| **Founder** | Jeremy Bentham, J.S. Mill | Immanuel Kant |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain in detail the major ethical theories and their relevance to AI systems. Discuss with examples.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Classical Ethical Theories (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Ethics in AI draws upon four foundational philosophical traditions:

**1. Utilitarianism (Consequentialism):**
- Founded by **Jeremy Bentham** and **John Stuart Mill**.
- Core principle: An action is right if it produces the **greatest good for the greatest number**.
- Mathematical formulation:
$$\text{Utility} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Benefit}_i - \text{Harm}_i)$$
- **AI Application:** In autonomous vehicle dilemmas, utilitarian AI calculates the action minimizing total casualties. Example: If braking fails, swerve to hit 1 pedestrian rather than 5.
- **Limitation:** Difficult to quantify benefits/harms; may justify unethical means for greater good.

**2. Deontological Ethics (Duty-Based):**
- Founded by **Immanuel Kant**; based on the **Categorical Imperative**.
- Core principle: Actions are right based on adherence to **moral rules/duties**, not consequences.
- Two formulations: (i) Act only on principles you could universalize; (ii) Treat humanity always as an end, never merely as a means.
- **AI Application:** An AI medical diagnostic system has a duty to provide truthful information to patients, regardless of emotional consequences.
- **Limitation:** Rigid rule-following cannot handle unprecedented moral dilemmas.

**3. Virtue Ethics:**
- Originated with **Aristotle**; focuses on **moral character** rather than actions.
- Core principle: A virtuous person acts with traits like courage, honesty, compassion, and justice, seeking the "golden mean."
- **AI Application:** A virtuous AI assistant prioritizes honesty over engagement metrics and refuses to generate manipulative content.
- **Limitation:** Difficult to encode virtues as explicit algorithms.

**4. Rights-Based Ethics:**
- Rooted in **John Locke** and **John Rawls** (Theory of Justice).
- Core principle: Individuals possess **fundamental rights** (life, liberty, privacy, dignity) that must not be violated.
- **AI Application:** Mass facial recognition systems violate the right to privacy and must be restricted regardless of security benefits.
- **Limitation:** Rights can conflict (e.g., privacy vs. security); prioritization needed.

#### Part (b): Application to AI Systems (7 Marks)

**Real-World Case Applications:**

**Case 1 — Amazon Hiring AI (2018):**
Amazon scrapped an AI recruitment tool that learned from 10 years of resumes and became biased against women. From a **rights perspective**, it violated gender equality. From a **utilitarian perspective**, long-term discrimination outweighed short-term efficiency gains. The system lacked **virtue** of fairness.

**Case 2 — Healthcare AI:**
IBM Watson for Oncology was found making unsafe recommendations. From a **deontological view**, the duty to "do no harm" (non-maleficence) was violated. **Utilitarian analysis**: net harm to cancer patients outweighed benefits. **Virtue ethics**: failed the virtue of **prudence**.

**Case 3 — Generative AI and Copyright:**
Tools like Stable Diffusion trained on artists' work without consent. From a **rights view**, artists' intellectual property rights are violated. From a **deontological view**, using someone's labor without permission is ethically wrong.

**Synthesis:**
A robust AI ethics approach integrates all four theories:
- Utilitarian thinking for cost-benefit analysis
- Deontological rules for hard constraints
- Virtue ethics for design culture
- Rights frameworks for protection of individuals

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **Kant's Categorical Imperative** with **Utilitarianism's Greatest Happiness Principle**. Memorize: Kant = **duty**, Mill = **consequences**. Mixing these up will cost 2-3 marks immediately.

---

### Question B: Discuss the major ethical challenges in AI with reference to bias, fairness, accountability, and transparency. Propose a framework for ethical AI governance.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Major Ethical Challenges (7 Marks)

**1. Bias in AI:**
- **Definition:** Systematic errors in AI outputs that unfairly discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected attributes (race, gender, age).
- **Sources of Bias:**
  - **Historical bias** in training data
  - **Sampling bias** from non-representative data
  - **Label bias** from annotator prejudice
  - **Algorithmic bias** from model design choices
- **Example:** The COMPAS recidivism algorithm was 2x more likely to falsely flag African-American defendants as future criminals compared to white defendants.

**2. Fairness:**
- Three mathematical fairness criteria often in conflict (Chouldechova's Theorem):
  - Demographic Parity: Equal positive prediction rates across groups
  - Equalized Odds: Equal TPR and FPR across groups
  - Calibration: Equal positive predictive value across groups
- **Impossibility:** When base rates differ between groups, all three cannot hold simultaneously.

**3. Accountability:**
- The **"responsibility gap"** problem: when autonomous AI causes harm, who is liable?
  - Developer? Deployer? User? The AI itself?
- Need for: audit trails, documentation, clear chain of responsibility.
- **Example:** Tesla Autopilot crashes raise questions about driver vs. manufacturer liability.

**4. Transparency:**
- The **"black box" problem**: deep learning models are often opaque.
- Requires **Explainable AI (XAI)** methods:
  - **LIME** (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)
  - **SHAP** (SHapley Additive exPlanations)
  - **Attention mechanisms** in transformers
- GDPR Article 22 grants a "right to explanation" for automated decisions.

#### Part (b): Proposed Framework for Ethical AI Governance (7 Marks)

**A Five-Layer Governance Framework:**

**Layer 1 — Ethical Foundation (Principles)**
Adopt recognized principles: OECD AI Principles, EU's 7 Requirements, Asilomar Principles. Establish organizational AI ethics charter.

**Layer 2 — Regulatory Compliance**
- Comply with GDPR, EU AI Act (risk-based categorization: unacceptable, high, limited, minimal risk).
- Conduct **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)** and **Algorithmic Impact Assessments**.

**Layer 3 — Technical Controls**
- Bias detection and mitigation tools (e.g., AIF360, Fairlearn).
- XAI integration for transparency.
- Differential privacy for data protection.
- Robust testing for safety.

**Layer 4 — Organizational Processes**
- Establish an **AI Ethics Review Board** with diverse expertise.
- Mandatory ethics training for AI developers.
- **Ethics by design** integrated into SDLC.
- Whistleblower protection for raising concerns.

**Layer 5 — Continuous Monitoring & Accountability**
- Post-deployment audits (quarterly/bi-annual).
- Redress mechanisms for affected individuals.
- Public transparency reports.
- Incident response procedures.

**Implementation Roadmap:**
1. **Assessment** — Identify AI use cases and risk levels
2. **Design** — Embed ethics by design principles
3. **Development** — Use diverse teams and data
4. **Deployment** — Pilot with monitoring
5. **Operation** — Continuous auditing and improvement
6. **Retirement** — Sunset planning for legacy systems

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is treating **fairness, accountability, and transparency as identical concepts**. They are distinct: Fairness concerns **outcomes**, Accountability concerns **responsibility**, and Transparency concerns **processes**. Conflating them shows shallow understanding and will cost marks. Also, students often forget to mention **specific tools or regulations** (e.g., GDPR Article 22) — always ground your answer in concrete examples.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** AI Ethics = moral principles governing design, development, and use of AI systems.
- **Classical Theories (Must Memorize):**
  - 🟢 Utilitarianism → Consequences (Bentham, Mill)
  - 🔵 Deontology → Duty & Rules (Kant)
  - 🟡 Virtue Ethics → Character & Virtues (Aristotle)
  - 🟣 Rights-Based → Fundamental Rights (Locke, Rawls)
- **Four Pillars of FAT:** Fairness, Accountability, Transparency (+ Privacy & Safety).
- **Major Frameworks:**
  - **EU Guidelines** → 7 requirements for Trustworthy AI
  - **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** → 8 general principles
  - **Asilomar Principles** → 23 principles (long-term risk focus)
  - **OECD AI Principles** → 5 values-based principles
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Algorithmic Bias (sources: historical, sampling, label, algorithmic)
  - Fairness Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova)
  - Right to Explanation (GDPR Article 22)
  - XAI tools (LIME, SHAP)
  - Responsibility Gap in autonomous systems
- **Must-Know Cases:**
  - Microsoft Tay (2016) — adversarial training failure
  - COMPAS — racial bias in criminal justice
  - Amazon Hiring AI — gender bias
  - Cambridge Analytica — privacy violation
  - Tesla Autopilot — accountability gap
- **Regulations to Mention:** GDPR, EU AI Act, OECD Recommendations.
- **Stakeholders:** Developers, Organizations, Regulators, Users, Society.

### 📝 High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Exams

**"SAFE AI"** — Core Ethical Principles:
- **S**afety and robustness
- **A**ccountability
- **F**airness
- **E**xplainability (transparency)

**"FAIR-PHASE"** — Seven EU Requirements:
- **F**airness
- **A**ccountability
- **I**ndependence (human oversight)
- **R**obustness
- **P**rivacy
- **H**uman agency
- **A**ccessibility
- **S**ocietal benefit
- **E**xplainability

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions on ethics, structure your answer as: (1) Definition, (2) Theories with examples, (3) Real-world case study, (4) Framework/principles, (5) Conclusion. Always include at least **one specific real-world case** and **one named framework** to score full marks. Draw diagrams where possible — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy and Security: Ethics and Accountability, Auditing AI Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Privacy and Security: Ethics and Accountability, Auditing AI Models # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Privacy in Artificial Intelligence ### Formal Academic Definition **Privacy in AI** is the engineering and governance discipline concerned with ensuring that personally identifiable information (PII), sensitive attributes, and inferred personal data are protected throughout the entire machine learning lifecycle — from data collection, storage, and model training to inference, deployment, and eventual decommissioning. It is operationalized through techniques such as **differential privacy**, **federated learning**, **homomorphic encryption**, and **data anonymization**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Privacy is not merely a "legal checkbox" under regulations like the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023** or **EU GDPR**. In KTU's outcome-based framework, privacy is a *first-class engineering property* of any AI system, alongside accuracy and latency. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Doctor's Whiteboard Imagine a hospital where the head doctor (the AI model) treats thousands of patients. To diagnose well, the doctor must study *symptoms*, but is *never* allowed to write down the patient's name, address, or face on the public whiteboard. Furthermore, even if someone snuck a peek at the doctor's notes, they should not be able to reconstruct any individual patient's identity. **Differential privacy** is like adding a tiny bit of "static noise" to every note the doctor writes, so individual cases blend into the crowd — but the overall diagnostic wisdom remains intact. ### Key Privacy Constants and Metrics - **ε (Epsilon — Privacy Budget)**: The smaller the **ε**, the stronger the privacy guarantee. Standard values: **ε = 0.1** (very strong), **ε = 1.0** (moderate), **ε = 10** (weak). - **δ (Delta — Failure Probability)**: Usually set to ≤ **1/n²**, where *n* is the dataset size. - **k-Anonymity**: A row in data is indistinguishable from at least *k-1* other rows. - **l-Diversity**: Each equivalence class has at least *l* "well-represented" values for sensitive attributes. --- ## 1.2 Security in Artificial Intelligence ### Formal Academic Definition **AI Security** is the field dedicated to protecting machine learning models, training pipelines, and inference endpoints from malicious actors. It encompasses **adversarial robustness** (resistance to crafted input perturbations), **backdoor defense** (detection of hidden triggers), **model stealing/extraction attacks**, **membership inference attacks**, and **data poisoning**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction:** *Privacy* asks *"Can an adversary learn about the training data?"* — *Security* asks *"Can an adversary make the model behave incorrectly or steal it?"* ### Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Car and Sticker Attack Picture a self-driving car that perfectly recognizes STOP signs. A security researcher places a small strip of black-and-white tape (an *adversarial patch*) on the sign. The car's vision model now misclassifies it as a SPEED LIMIT 45 sign. The car's accuracy on *normal* data was 99.9%, yet **security** failed because the model lacked **adversarial robustness**. This is the famous **Evtimov et al. (2017)** adversarial patch attack. --- ## 1.3 Ethics and Accountability in AI ### Formal Academic Definition **AI Ethics** is the normative discipline evaluating whether AI systems align with human values such as fairness, autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice. **Accountability** is the *institutional and technical* property ensuring that a clearly identified human or organizational entity is answerable for the design, deployment, and consequences of an AI system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Core Outcome (CO3):** Students must be able to *evaluate the ethical, privacy, and security implications of AI systems and propose governance mechanisms.* ### The Three Pillars of AI Accountability 1. **Answerability** — A named individual or body can explain why the system produced a given output. 2. **Auditability** — The system's data, code, and decisions can be reconstructed and reviewed. 3. **Redressability** — Affected persons have a clear path to contest decisions and seek remedy. --- ## 1.4 Auditing AI Models ### Formal Academic Definition An **AI Audit** is a structured, systematic, and documented evaluation of an AI system's behavior, performance, data lineage, and compliance with internal policies and external regulations. Audits are classified as: - **Internal Audits** — Conducted by the organization's own AI Governance team. - **External Audits** — Conducted by independent third parties (e.g., regulators, accredited auditors). - **Regulatory Audits** — Mandated by law (e.g., the **EU AI Act's** risk-tiered conformity assessments). ### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Health Inspector A restaurant's food might *taste* good (high model accuracy), but a health inspector checks the *kitchen*: Are the ingredients fresh? Is the refrigerator at the right temperature? Are food handlers washing their hands? Similarly, an AI auditor checks the *kitchen of the model*: training data quality, feature engineering, hyperparameter logs, drift monitoring, fairness metrics, and deployment access controls. > [!NOTE] > **Core Audit Deliverable:** The **Model Card** (Mitchell et al., 2019) and **Datasheet for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2018) are the modern equivalents of a restaurant's public health and ingredient disclosures. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Privacy Threat Taxonomy Privacy threats in AI are layered. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize and counter each: | Threat | Description | Countermeasure | |---|---|---| | **Membership Inference** | Adversary determines if a specific record was in training data | Differential Privacy, Regularization | | **Attribute Inference** | Adversary reconstructs sensitive attributes from model outputs | Adversarial Training, Output Perturbation | | **Model Inversion** | Adversary reconstructs training images from gradients | Gradient Noise, Federated Learning | | **Re-identification** | Quasi-identifiers re-link anonymized records to individuals | k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness | | **Data Leakage via Prompts** | LLMs regurgitate memorized training data | Output Filtering, RAG-only architecture | --- ## 2.2 The Mathematics of Differential Privacy Differential Privacy (DP) is the *gold standard* formalization. A randomized algorithm **M** satisfies **ε-Differential Privacy** if for all adjacent datasets **D** and **D'** (differing in one record) and all subsets **S** of outputs: $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$ ### Sequential Composition Theorem If **M₁** satisfies **ε₁-DP** and **M₂** satisfies **ε₂-DP**, then releasing both outputs satisfies: $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \quad \text{(DP)}$$ ### Parallel Composition Theorem If the datasets are **disjoint**, then: $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2) \quad \text{(DP on disjoint partitions)}$$ ### The Gaussian Mechanism For a query **f** with sensitivity **Δf**, adding Gaussian noise with standard deviation **σ** yields **(ε, δ)-DP** when: $$\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why sensitivity matters:** The **L2 sensitivity** of a function is defined as the maximum change in its output when one record is added or removed: > $$\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_2$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula & Theorem Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Parameter | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **ε-DP Definition** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | ε | dimensionless | | **Sequential Composition** | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \varepsilon_i$ | budget exhaustion | dimensionless | | **Gaussian Mechanism** | $\sigma \geq \dfrac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | σ (noise std.) | query-output units | | **k-Anonymity** | Each record indistinguishable from ≥ k−1 others | k | integer ≥ 1 | | **Adversarial Accuracy** | $A_{\text{adv}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[f(x_i + \delta_i) = y_i]$ | δᵢ perturbation | ratio (0–1) | | **Demographic Parity** | $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=b) \vert \leq \tau$ | τ threshold | probability | | **Equalized Odds** | $\vert TPR_a - TPR_b \vert \leq \tau$ and $\vert FPR_a - FPR_b \vert \leq \tau$ | τ | probability | | **Disparate Impact (80% Rule)** | $DI = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{unprivileged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{privileged})} \geq 0.8$ | DI ratio | unitless | | **Membership Inference AUC** | $MIA_{\text{score}} = AUC(M_{\text{shadow}}, D_{\text{target}})$ | AUC | [0.5, 1.0] | | **Privacy Loss Random Variable** | $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{M}; D, D') = \log \dfrac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)=O]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')=O]}$ | log-likelihood | nats | | **FGSM Perturbation** | $\delta = \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L(\theta, x, y))$ | ε bound | pixel units | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** The KTU valuation key often tests whether a student knows that **lower ε ⇒ stronger privacy** but **higher ε ⇒ better model utility**. The privacy-utility trade-off is a guaranteed 7-mark question. --- ## 2.4 The Security Threat Model A formal threat model specifies the **adversary's goal**, **knowledge**, and **capability**: - **Goal**: Misclassification, targeted misclassification, source-target misclassification, or confidence reduction. - **Knowledge**: - *White-box* — full model access (gradients, architecture, weights). - *Black-box* — only API access with confidence scores. - *Gray-box* — partial knowledge (e.g., architecture but not weights). - **Capability**: - *Evasion* — perturb test-time inputs. - *Poisoning* — corrupt training data. - *Exploration* — query to extract model information. ### Adversarial Example Generation — FGSM The **Fast Gradient Sign Method** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) generates a perturbation bounded by an L∞ norm of ε: $$\delta = \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}\big(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\big)$$ The resulting adversarial example is: $$x^{\text{adv}} = x + \delta = x + \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}\big(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\big)$$ The model is **empirically robust** if $f(x^{\text{adv}}) = f(x)$ for the relevant class. --- ## 2.5 The Accountability Stack Accountability is operationalized through a layered "stack": | Layer | Mechanism | Responsible Party | |---|---|---| | **Data Provenance** | Datasheets for Datasets | Data Steward | | **Model Documentation** | Model Cards, System Cards | ML Engineer | | **Logging & Traceability** | MLflow, DVC, Weights & Biases | MLOps Team | | **Continuous Monitoring** | Drift Detection, Bias Dashboards | AI Governance Lead | | **Human-in-the-Loop** | Override, Review, Appeal | Domain Expert | | **External Audit** | Independent Red-Teaming | Third-Party Auditor | | **Legal Compliance** | GDPR, DPDPA, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001 | Legal & DPO | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Privacy Application | Security Application | Accountability Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Healthcare** | Federated hospital training | Robust MRI classifiers | FDA Model Card disclosures | | **Finance** | Privacy-preserving credit scoring | Anti-fraud adversarial defense | Bias audits under ECOA | | **Criminal Justice** | DP on recidivism data | Robustness to adversarial profiles | Explainable COMPAS audits | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | Edge-only inference (no raw data egress) | Adversarial patch detection | Black-box event recorders | | **Generative AI** | Training data opt-out mechanisms | Prompt injection firewalls | Watermarking & content provenance | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Privacy Budget Exhaustion via Sequential Composition ### Problem A hospital releases a differentially private statistic on patient wait times using three queries: - **Q₁**: Mean wait time with **ε₁ = 0.5** - **Q₂**: Median wait time with **ε₂ = 0.7** - **Q₃**: Standard deviation with **ε₃ = 0.3** What is the total privacy budget consumed, and is it acceptable under a target of **ε ≤ 1.0**? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the applicable theorem.** Since all three queries are run on the **same dataset D**, the Sequential Composition Theorem applies. Each release leaks additional information, so privacy budgets **add** (they do not average, do not take the maximum). **Step 2: Write the composition formula.** $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$ **Step 3: Substitute the given values.** $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.3$$ **Step 4: Perform the addition.** $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.5$$ **Step 5: Compare against the target budget of 1.0.** $$1.5 > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Budget EXCEEDED. The release is NOT acceptable as-is.}$$ **Step 6: Remediation options.** - **Option A — Subsample & Aggregate**: Use the **Subsampled Gaussian Mechanism**, which *amplifies* privacy. If a fraction *q* of the data is sampled, the effective privacy improves to roughly $2q\varepsilon$ per query. - **Option B — Re-budget**: Drop Q₃ (or merge Q₁ and Q₂ via a multi-query mechanism using the **Matrix Mechanism**). - **Option C — Increase Noise**: Use a smaller per-query ε by injecting more noise, but this degrades utility. **Step 7: Final answer.** > The total privacy loss is **ε = 1.5**, exceeding the acceptable budget. The hospital must either reduce the number of queries, increase noise, or apply privacy amplification via subsampling. --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Gaussian Mechanism Calibration ### Problem A query **f(D)** returns the sum of ages of patients in a dataset of 10,000 individuals. The **L2 sensitivity** is **Δf = 1** (removing one patient changes the sum by at most 1 year). We want **(ε = 1.0, δ = 10⁻⁵)-Differential Privacy**. What is the minimum Gaussian noise standard deviation σ to add? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Recall the Gaussian Mechanism bound.** $$\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$ **Step 2: Substitute known values.** - Δf = 1 - ε = 1.0 - δ = 10⁻⁵ **Step 3: Compute the ratio 1.25 / δ.** $$\frac{1.25}{\delta} = \frac{1.25}{10^{-5}} = 1.25 \times 10^{5} = 125{,}000$$ **Step 4: Compute the natural logarithm.** $$\ln(125{,}000) \approx 11.736$$ **Step 5: Multiply by 2.** $$2 \times 11.736 = 23.472$$ **Step 6: Take the square root.** $$\sqrt{23.472} \approx 4.8447$$ **Step 7: Divide by ε = 1.** $$\sigma \geq \frac{1 \times 4.8447}{1} = 4.8447$$ **Step 8: Final answer.** > The minimum noise standard deviation is **σ ≈ 4.845** years. This means a query returning a sum of, say, 350,000 years of patient age would be perturbed by Gaussian noise of standard deviation 4.845, giving an answer like 350,004.2 — heavily *noisy* but the *individual* contribution of any single patient is provably hidden. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: FGSM Adversarial Perturbation on a 2D Toy Classifier ### Problem Consider a linear classifier with weight vector **w = [1, −1]** and bias **b = 0**. The model classifies point **x = [2, 1]** correctly with class **y = +1** (since **w·x + b = 1 > 0**). The loss function is the hinge loss: $$L = \max(0, 1 - y(w \cdot x + b))$$ Compute the FGSM perturbation for **ε = 0.5**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Compute the current model output and loss.** $$w \cdot x + b = (1)(2) + (-1)(1) + 0 = 1$$ $$L = \max(0, 1 - (1)(1)) = \max(0, 0) = 0$$ Since the point is correctly classified and the margin is exactly 0, the loss is at the decision boundary. We can still compute the gradient. **Step 2: Compute the gradient of the loss with respect to x.** For a correctly classified point with margin ≥ 1, the loss is 0 and the gradient is 0. However, for a point exactly on the margin or misclassified, the gradient is **−y·w**. Here the margin is exactly 1, so we treat the loss as if it were active (a subgradient): $$\nabla_x L = -y \cdot w = -(1) \cdot [1, -1] = [-1, 1]$$ **Step 3: Compute the sign of the gradient.** $$\text{sign}(\nabla_x L) = [-1, 1]$$ **Step 4: Apply the FGSM formula.** $$\delta = \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x L) = 0.5 \cdot [-1, 1] = [-0.5, 0.5]$$ **Step 5: Construct the adversarial example.** $$x^{\text{adv}} = x + \delta = [2 - 0.5, \; 1 + 0.5] = [1.5, 1.5]$$ **Step 6: Verify the new model output.** $$w \cdot x^{\text{adv}} + b = (1)(1.5) + (-1)(1.5) + 0 = 0$$ **Step 7: Interpretation.** The original point gave a confident margin of 1.0. The adversarial point now lies **exactly on the decision boundary**. A small additional perturbation would flip the classification. The L∞ norm of the perturbation is **‖δ‖∞ = 0.5 = ε**, satisfying the FGSM constraint. > **Key Insight:** A perturbation of only **0.5** units in each pixel shifted a confident classification to a boundary case. In high-dimensional image spaces (e.g., 224×224×3 = 150,528 dimensions), this is why adversarial examples are so dangerous. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Audit Frameworks | Framework | Issuer | Year | Scope | Audit Type | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **EU AI Act** | European Parliament | 2024 | All AI in EU market | Conformity Assessment (pre-market) | High — global benchmark | | **NIST AI RMF** | NIST (USA) | 2023 | Voluntary, sector-agnostic | Risk Management Audit | High — industry adoption | | **ISO/IEC 42001** | ISO | 2023 | AI Management System (AIMS) | Certification Audit | Medium — emerging | | **OECD AI Principles** | OECD | 2019 (rev. 2024) | Policy guidance | Self-assessment | High — foundational ethics | | **Singapore Model AI Governance Framework v2** | IMDA/PDPC | 2024 | Industry self-regulation | Internal Audit | Medium — Asia reference | | **India DPDPA + Digital India Act (proposed)** | MeitY | 2023 | Personal data + AI | Regulatory Audit | High — Indian context | --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Disparate Impact Ratio (The 80% Rule) ### Setup Suppose a loan-approval model approves: - **Group A (privileged)**: 60 out of 100 applicants → P(approve | A) = 0.60 - **Group B (unprivileged)**: 36 out of 100 applicants → P(approve | B) = 0.36 ### Computation $$DI = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{unprivileged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{privileged})}$$ **Step 1: Substitute.** $$DI = \frac{0.36}{0.60}$$ **Step 2: Compute.** $$DI = 0.60 = 60\%$$ **Step 3: Compare against the 80% threshold.** $$0.60 < 0.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Violation of the 80% rule.}$$ > **Conclusion:** The model exhibits disparate impact against Group B. Remediation options include: re-weighting the training data, applying **rejection-sampling** with fairness constraints, or using a constrained optimization such as **exponentiated-gradient fairness**. # 4.
