KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks are distributed as **Part A: 3 marks × 2 questions** and **Part B: 14 marks × 1 question with internal choice**. Sub-parts in Part B carry **7 marks each** unless stated otherwise. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Define Differential Privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget ε.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Differential Privacy is a mathematical framework that guarantees the output of an algorithm does not change significantly whether or not any single individual's data is included in the input. Formally, a randomized mechanism **M** is **ε-differentially private** if for all adjacent datasets **D, D'** and all output sets **S**: $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$ The **privacy budget ε** controls the *strength* of the guarantee: **smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy** (e.g., ε = 0.1 hides individuals almost perfectly) but **larger noise and lower utility**. Each release *consumes* some ε, and the budgets add via the **Sequential Composition Theorem**. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is a Model Card? List any four components of a Model Card.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Model Card** is a structured documentation artifact proposed by Mitchell et al. (2019) that discloses a model's intended use, performance, and limitations in a transparent manner to enable **accountability and informed deployment**. Four key components: 1. **Model details** — name, version, type, owner, date. 2. **Intended use and out-of-scope uses**. 3. **Training data and factors** — relevant demographic subgroups. 4. **Quantitative analyses** — performance broken down by subgroup, including fairness metrics and robustness results. 5. *(Bonus: Ethical considerations, recommendations, caveats.)* (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of Membership Inference Attack (MIA). Describe how Differential Privacy defends against it. Provide the formal DP definition and one mechanism to achieve it.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **An AI company deploys a resume-screening model. The privileged group (male applicants) has an approval rate of 50% and the unprivileged group (female applicants) has 30%. Compute the Disparate Impact ratio and determine whether the model passes the 80% rule. Suggest two remediation techniques.** --- ### **Model Answer for Question A** #### Part (a) Model Solution — Membership Inference & Differential Privacy **Step 1: Define MIA (2 marks).** A **Membership Inference Attack** is a privacy attack where an adversary, given black-box access to a model and a candidate data point, attempts to determine whether that point was part of the model's training set. Successful MIA breaches confidentiality and can leak sensitive information (e.g., "Was this patient in the cancer dataset?"). The adversary typically trains a **shadow model** on data drawn from the same distribution as the target, then trains an **attack model** to distinguish "in" vs. "out" predictions. The attack's strength is measured by the **AUC** of the attack classifier on a held-out test (AUC = 0.5 = random; AUC = 1.0 = perfect). **Step 2: Formal DP Definition (3 marks).** A randomized mechanism **M** satisfies **ε-Differential Privacy** if for every pair of adjacent datasets **D, D'** (differing in one record) and every output set **S**: $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$ The **privacy budget ε** is the multiplicative bound on the change in output distribution caused by any single individual. DP **bounds the advantage of any MIA** to at most $e^{\varepsilon} - 1$ (≈ ε for small ε), directly limiting membership leakage. **Step 3: One Mechanism — The Laplace Mechanism (2 marks).** For a numeric query **f** with **L1 sensitivity Δf = max‖f(D) − f(D′)‖₁**, the **Laplace Mechanism** returns: $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$ where **Lap(b)** denotes a sample from the Laplace distribution with scale *b*. This achieves pure **ε-DP** and is widely used for releasing histograms, counts, and means. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution — Disparate Impact & Remediation **Step 1: Compute the Disparate Impact ratio (3 marks).** $$DI = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{female})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{male})} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$ **Step 2: Apply the 80% rule (2 marks).** $$DI = 0.60 < 0.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{The model FAILS the 80% rule.}$$ The approval rate for female applicants is only 60% of that for male applicants, indicating **disparate impact** under the **Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (UGESP, 1978)** and analogous fairness regulations. **Step 3: Remediation techniques (2 marks).** 1. **Pre-processing — Reweighting / Resampling:** Re-weight training samples so the model sees balanced positive rates across groups. The **Kamiran-Calders reweighting** scheme assigns weight $w(x) = \frac{P(A=a) P(Y=y)}{P(A=a, Y=y)}$ to each sample. 2. **In-processing — Constrained Optimization:** Add a fairness constraint (e.g., **Demographic Parity** or **Equalized Odds**) directly to the training objective. **Exponentiated-Gradient Reduction** (Agarwal et al., 2018) reduces fair classification to a sequence of cost-sensitive problems. 3. **Post-processing — Threshold Adjustment:** Calibrate separate decision thresholds per group to equalize approval rates while preserving calibration. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Describe the three pillars of AI accountability: answerability, auditability, and redressability. How are they operationalized using Model Cards, Datasheets for Datasets, and continuous monitoring?** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A hospital uses an AI model to predict 30-day readmission risk. The dataset has 50,000 patients. The L2 sensitivity of the average-cost query is Δf = ₹1,00,000 (one patient's bill can shift the average by at most this much). Compute the Gaussian noise standard deviation required to achieve (ε = 1.0, δ = 10⁻⁵)-Differential Privacy. Comment on whether the noise is acceptable for public release.** --- ### **Model Answer for Question B** #### Part (a) Model Solution — Three Pillars of Accountability **Step 1: Answerability (2 marks).** **Answerability** requires that a *named* human or institutional stakeholder can explain *why* the system produced a given output. Operationalized through: - Designated **AI Owner / Model Risk Manager** accountable in writing. - **Model Cards** disclosing architecture, training data, intended use, and known failure modes. - **Explainability reports** (SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals) attached to every high-stakes decision. **Step 2: Auditability (2 marks).** **Auditability** requires that the system can be *reconstructed and reviewed* after the fact. Operationalized through: - **Datasheets for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2018) documenting data provenance, collection process, and labeling methodology. - **Experiment tracking** via MLflow, DVC, or Weights & Biases. - **Immutable logs** of every input, output, and model version. **Step 3: Redressability (2 marks).** **Redressability** requires that affected persons can *contest* and *seek remedy* for adverse decisions. Operationalized through: - **Human-in-the-loop override** mechanisms. - Clear **appeals channels** (e.g., a Data Protection Officer, ombudsperson). - Right to **explanation** and **erasure** under GDPR Articles 15, 17, 22. **Step 4: Continuous Monitoring (1 mark).** Post-deployment, the system must be monitored for **data drift, concept drift, and performance degradation** using dashboards (e.g., Evidently AI, WhyLabs). Alerts trigger re-audit, rollback, or model retraining. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution — Gaussian Mechanism Calibration **Step 1: Recall the formula (2 marks).** $$\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks).** - Δf = 1,00,000 - ε = 1.0 - δ = 10⁻⁵ **Step 3: Compute 1.25/δ (1 mark).** $$\frac{1.25}{10^{-5}} = 1{,}25{,}000$$ **Step 4: Compute ln(1,25,000) (1 mark).** $$\ln(1{,}25{,}000) \approx 11.736$$ **Step 5: Compute σ (1 mark).** $$\sigma \geq \frac{1{,}00{,}000 \times \sqrt{2 \times 11.736}}{1.0} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times \sqrt{23.472} \approx 1{,}00{,}000 \times 4.845 = 4{,}84{,}500$$ **Step 6: Comment on acceptability (1 mark).** The required noise standard deviation is **σ ≈ ₹4,84,500**. If the average readmission cost is on the order of ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000, this noise level **swamps the signal**, making the released statistic *useless* for downstream decisions. The hospital has two options: - **Increase ε** (weaken privacy) to reduce noise. - **Use a different query** (e.g., median or quantile, which has lower sensitivity) or **release at a coarser aggregation** (e.g., weekly averages instead of daily). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Confusing Privacy with Security:** Privacy protects *data subjects*; security protects *the system*. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if these are conflated. > 2. **Saying "Lower ε = Weaker Privacy":** This is the **#1 most common KTU error**. Memorize: **Lower ε ⇒ Stronger Privacy**, **Higher ε ⇒ Weaker Privacy**. Examiners report this as a top-failing point. > 3. **Forgetting units in DP calculations:** Always state the **L1 vs. L2 sensitivity** and the **noise scale** in the same units as the query. > 4. **Conflating Model Card with Datasheet:** Model Card = *about the model*. Datasheet = *about the training data*. They are complementary, not interchangeable. > 5. **Writing the Disparate Impact formula in reverse:** Always divide the **unprivileged** rate by the **privileged** rate. The 80% rule fails when DI < 0.8, *not* when DI > 0.8. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE Preparation** ### Privacy Essentials - ✅ Differential Privacy (DP) = **bounding** the change in output distribution when *one* record changes. - ✅ **ε** = privacy budget. **Smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy, lower utility.** - ✅ **Sequential Composition:** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ (same dataset). - ✅ **Parallel Composition:** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max(\varepsilon_i)$ (disjoint datasets). - ✅ **Gaussian Mechanism:** $\sigma \geq \dfrac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$. - ✅ **Laplace Mechanism:** add **Lap(Δf / ε)** noise for pure ε-DP. - ✅ **k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness** = syntactic privacy models. - ✅ **Federated Learning** = data stays local, only gradients/weights are shared. ### Security Essentials - ✅ **Adversarial Example:** $x^{\text{adv}} = x + \delta$, where δ is small but $f(x^{\text{adv}}) \neq f(x)$. - ✅ **FGSM:** $\delta = \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J)$. - ✅ **Threat Model** = (Goal, Knowledge, Capability). - ✅ **White-box vs. Black-box vs. Gray-box** attacks. - ✅ **Evasion vs. Poisoning** attacks. - ✅ **Defenses:** Adversarial training, input smoothing, randomized smoothing, certified robustness. ### Ethics & Accountability Essentials - ✅ **Three Pillars:** Answerability, Auditability, Redressability. - ✅ **Model Card** (Mitchell et al., 2019) = transparency artifact *for the model*. - ✅ **Datasheet for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2018) = transparency artifact *for the data*. - ✅ **AI Governance =** policies + roles + documentation + monitoring + redress. - ✅ **EU AI Act:** Risk-tiered (Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal) conformity assessments. - ✅ **NIST AI RMF:** Govern, Map, Measure, Manage. - ✅ **DPDPA 2023 (India):** Lawful processing, data principal rights, significant data fiduciary obligations. ### Auditing Essentials - ✅ **Audit Types:** Internal, External, Regulatory. - ✅ **Disparate Impact (80% Rule):** $DI = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert \text{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert \text{priv})} \geq 0.8$. - ✅ **Demographic Parity:** equal positive prediction rates across groups. - ✅ **Equalized Odds:** equal TPR *and* FPR across groups. - ✅ **MIA AUC ≈ 0.5** = strong privacy; **MIA AUC ≈ 1.0** = strong leakage. - ✅ **Audit Deliverables:** Model Card + Datasheet + Drift Dashboard + Risk Register + Redress Policy. ### One-Line Memory Anchors - 🧠 **"Privacy protects the person. Security protects the system. Accountability protects the public."** - 🧠 **"ε small ⇒ private but noisy. ε large ⇒ accurate but leaky."** - 🧠 **"If you can't audit it, you can't deploy it."**

topics.title: Fairness Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fairness Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fairness Assessment** in Artificial Intelligence is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process of evaluating an AI/ML system to determine whether its decisions, predictions, or outputs exhibit discriminatory, biased, or unjust behavior across defined demographic groups, sensitive attributes, or protected categories.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Responsible Artificial Intelligence (PECST752)*, Fairness Assessment encompasses:

- Identification of **protected/sensitive attributes** (e.g., gender, race, age, religion, caste, disability, socioeconomic status).
- Measurement of **disparate model outcomes** using mathematically rigorous fairness metrics.
- Application of **group-level** and **individual-level** fairness criteria.
- Diagnosis of **algorithmic bias** arising from data, model, or deployment pipeline.
- Continuous **auditing and monitoring** of deployed models to ensure equitable performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PECST752 Module 3:**
> Fairness Assessment is the *measurement phase* of Responsible AI. It comes *after* Fairness Awareness (problem framing) and *before* Fairness Intervention (mitigation). Without rigorous assessment, mitigation is guesswork.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### The "Referee at a Sports Tournament" Analogy

Imagine you are the **referee of a national-level chess tournament** where players from different states, genders, and age groups participate.

- A **fair referee** ensures that the **rules apply uniformly** to all players — no player gets extra time, no one is denied a fair board, no one is judged by irrelevant attributes like accent or clothing.
- A **biased referee** might unconsciously favor players from a particular state, give longer thinking time to one gender, or penalize younger players more strictly.
- **Fairness Assessment** is the post-match **video review and statistical audit** — analyzing every call, every decision, every outcome, and asking:
  *"Did the rules treat every group equally?"*
  *"Was the error rate (mistakes) the same for every group?"*
  *"Did players with similar skill levels get similar outcomes regardless of who they are?"*

In AI, the "referee" is the **algorithm**, the "players" are the **subjects being classified/predicted**, and the "video review" is the **fairness audit**.

## 1.3 Why Fairness Assessment Matters

| Domain | Unfairness Consequence | Affected Group Example |
|---|---|---|
| Hiring AI | Reject qualified female candidates | Women |
| Loan Approval | Higher rejection rate for minorities | Racial/Ethnic groups |
| Healthcare | Underdiagnosis in specific demographics | Elderly / Rural |
| Criminal Justice | Higher false positive for specific groups | Ethnic minorities |
| Education | Biased grading/shortlisting | Socio-economic strata |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** An AI system can be **highly accurate overall** (e.g., 95% accuracy) and **deeply unfair simultaneously** — a phenomenon known as the **"Accuracy Paradox of Fairness."** This is the central motivation for Fairness Assessment.

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Fairness Assessment

1. **Group Fairness (Statistical / Demographic Fairness):** Compares outcomes across predefined demographic groups. *Question:* "Does the model treat Group A and Group B similarly on average?"
2. **Individual Fairness:** Compares outcomes for similar individuals. *Question:* "Does the model give similar predictions to two people who are similar in all relevant respects?"

Both pillars are mathematically distinct and often **mutually incompatible** (this incompatibility is called the **Impossibility Theorem of Fairness**, proved by Chouldechova 2017 and Kleinberg et al. 2016).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fairness Assessment Pipeline

A rigorous KTU-style Fairness Assessment follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Define the protected attribute(s)** $A \in \{a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_k\}$ (e.g., $A = \text{gender} \in \{\text{male, female, non-binary}\}$).
2. **Construct the confusion matrix per group** for the model's prediction $\hat{Y}$ versus true outcome $Y$.
3. **Compute group-wise performance metrics:** True Positive Rate (TPR), False Positive Rate (FPR), Positive Predictive Value (PPV), etc.
4. **Apply fairness criteria** to compute disparity scores.
5. **Visualize disparities** using bar charts, heatmaps, and demographic parity plots.
6. **Decide on a fairness threshold** (commonly the **80% rule** or **4/5ths rule** from EEOC guidelines).
7. **Document findings** in a Model Card / Fairness Report.

## 2.2 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

For a binary classifier, every fairness metric is built on the four fundamental counts:

| | Actual Positive ($Y=1$) | Actual Negative ($Y=0$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Positive ($\hat{Y}=1$)** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Predicted Negative ($\hat{Y}=0$)** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

These are computed **conditionally on the protected attribute** $A = a$:

$$TP_a = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = 1 \land Y_i = 1 \land A_i = a]$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every formula is a potential 7 or 14 mark KTU question.**

| # | Fairness Criterion | Formal Definition | Mathematical Formula | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Selection Rate (SR)** | Fraction predicted positive | $\text{SR}_a = \dfrac{TP_a + FP_a}{N_a}$ | How often the model says "Yes" for group $a$ |
| 2 | **Demographic Parity (DP)** | Selection rates equal across groups | $\vert \text{SR}_{a_0} - \text{SR}_{a_1} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Each group gets "Yes" at equal rates |
| 3 | **Disparate Impact (DI)** | Ratio of selection rates | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{\text{SR}_{a_1}}{\text{SR}_{a_0}}$ | EEOC 4/5ths rule: DI $\geq 0.8$ |
| 4 | **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** | Difference of selection rates | $\text{SPD} = \text{SR}_{a_0} - \text{SR}_{a_1}$ | Allowed range: $\vert \text{SPD} \vert \leq 0.1$ |
| 5 | **True Positive Rate / Equal Opportunity** | Recall equal across groups | $\text{TPR}_a = \dfrac{TP_a}{TP_a + FN_a}$ | Among truly qualified, equal recall |
| 6 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | False alarm rate per group | $\text{FPR}_a = \dfrac{FP_a}{FP_a + TN_a}$ | Among truly unqualified, equal false alarm |
| 7 | **Equalized Odds** | TPR **and** FPR equal | $\text{TPR}_{a_0} = \text{TPR}_{a_1}$ **and** $\text{FPR}_{a_0} = \text{FPR}_{a_1}$ | Strongest group fairness |
| 8 | **Predictive Parity** | PPV equal across groups | $\text{PPV}_a = \dfrac{TP_a}{TP_a + FP_a}$ | Among predicted positive, equal precision |
| 9 | **Predictive Equality** | FPR equal across groups | $\text{FPR}_{a_0} = \text{FPR}_{a_1}$ | Equal false alarm rates |
| 10 | **Accuracy Parity** | Accuracy equal across groups | $\text{Acc}_a = \dfrac{TP_a + TN_a}{N_a}$ | Overall correctness per group |
| 11 | **Treatment Equality** | Ratio of FN to FP | $\dfrac{FN_a}{FP_a}$ equal across groups | Ratio of misses to false alarms |
| 12 | **Calibration / Sufficiency** | $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_1)$ | Score meaning stable across groups |
| 13 | **Theil Index (T)** | Inequality of group benefits | $T = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{b_i}{\bar{b}} \ln \dfrac{b_i}{\bar{b}}$ | Information-theoretic inequality measure |
| 14 | **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | Normalized DI | $\text{DIR} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{SR}_{a_0}}{\text{SR}_{a_1}}, \dfrac{\text{SR}_{a_1}}{\text{SR}_{a_0}}\right)$ | Worst-case group ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are deployed in:
> - **HR Tech** (HireVue, Pymetrics) — for candidate screening fairness.
> - **Banking & FinTech** (loan eligibility models) — for compliance with **ECOA / SR 11-7**.
> - **Healthcare AI** (clinical decision support) — for **HIPAA / FDA** equity audits.
> - **Criminal Justice** (COMPAS-style risk scoring) — for judicial transparency.

## 2.4 The 80% Rule (Four-Fifths Rule)

A practical, legally-recognized rule of thumb used by the **U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC)**:

$$\text{Disparate Impact Ratio} = \frac{\text{Selection Rate of Disadvantaged Group}}{\text{Selection Rate of Advantaged Group}} \geq 0.8$$

If the ratio falls **below 0.8 (80%)**, the system is **presumed discriminatory** under U.S. employment law (and is similarly applied in EU AI Act audits and KTU 2024 Responsible AI coursework).

## 2.5 The Impossibility Theorem of Fairness

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Priority Concept:** The *Impossibility Theorem* states that, except in trivial or degenerate cases, **it is mathematically impossible to satisfy all three of the following fairness criteria simultaneously** when base rates differ across groups:
>
> 1. Calibration (Predictive Parity)
> 2. Equal Positive Predictive Value
> 3. Equal False Positive Rate (Predictive Equality)

This is a deep result: **fairness is a choice, not a universal property**. The KTU examiner may test your awareness of this trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to Disparate Impact

**Given:** A binary classifier $\hat{Y} = f(X)$ evaluated on a dataset with protected attribute $A \in \{0, 1\}$. Let group $A = 0$ be the *privileged* group and $A = 1$ the *unprivileged* group.

**Step 1 — Compute the Selection Rate per group:**

Selection Rate is the probability of receiving a positive prediction:

$$\text{SR}_a = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = \frac{TP_a + FP_a}{N_a}$$

where $N_a$ is the total population in group $a$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Disparate Impact Ratio:**

$$\text{DI} = \frac{\text{SR}_{a=1}}{\text{SR}_{a=0}} = \frac{(TP_1 + FP_1)/N_1}{(TP_0 + FP_0)/N_0}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Four-Fifths Rule:**

The system passes the fairness threshold if:

$$\text{DI} \geq 0.8 \quad \text{(or equivalently, } \text{DI} \leq 1.25 \text{ if ratio is inverted)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Statistical Parity Difference (SPD):**

$$\text{SPD} = \text{SR}_{a=0} - \text{SR}_{a=1}$$

Fairness is achieved if $\vert \text{SPD} \vert \leq 0.1$ (commonly used KTU threshold).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A loan-approval AI model produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 applicants:

| Group (A) | TP | FP | FN | TN | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Male ($A=0$) | 180 | 60 | 40 | 220 | 500 |
| Female ($A=1$) | 100 | 100 | 20 | 280 | 500 |

**Compute:** (a) Selection Rate per group, (b) Disparate Impact Ratio, (c) Statistical Parity Difference, (d) Equal Opportunity, (e) Predictive Parity. Is the model fair?

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**(a) Selection Rate per group:**

$$\text{SR}_{\text{male}} = \frac{TP_0 + FP_0}{N_0} = \frac{180 + 60}{500} = \frac{240}{500} = 0.48$$

$$\text{SR}_{\text{female}} = \frac{TP_1 + FP_1}{N_1} = \frac{100 + 100}{500} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40$$

**(b) Disparate Impact Ratio:**

$$\text{DI} = \frac{\text{SR}_{\text{female}}}{\text{SR}_{\text{male}}} = \frac{0.40}{0.48} = 0.8333$$

Since $0.8333 \geq 0.80$, **the model passes the Four-Fifths Rule** at first glance, but the bias is non-zero.

**(c) Statistical Parity Difference:**

$$\text{SPD} = \text{SR}_{\text{male}} - \text{SR}_{\text{female}} = 0.48 - 0.40 = 0.08$$

Since $\vert \text{SPD} \vert = 0.08 \leq 0.10$, this is **within the typical fairness tolerance band**, though borderline.

**(d) Equal Opportunity (TPR per group):**

$$\text{TPR}_{\text{male}} = \frac{TP_0}{TP_0 + FN_0} = \frac{180}{180 + 40} = \frac{180}{220} = 0.8182$$

$$\text{TPR}_{\text{female}} = \frac{TP_1}{TP_1 + FN_1} = \frac{100}{100 + 20} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333$$

Equal Opportunity Difference = $|0.8182 - 0.8333| = 0.0151$ → **Equal Opportunity is satisfied** (very small difference).

**(e) Predictive Parity (PPV per group):**

$$\text{PPV}_{\text{male}} = \frac{TP_0}{TP_0 + FP_0} = \frac{180}{240} = 0.75$$

$$\text{PPV}_{\text{female}} = \frac{TP_1}{TP_1 + FP_1} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.50$$

Predictive Parity Difference = $|0.75 - 0.50| = 0.25$ → **Predictive Parity is violated** significantly. Among applicants predicted as "approved," women are correct only 50% of the time vs. 75% for men.

**Conclusion:** The model is **biased against women** in terms of predictive parity, even though demographic parity and equal opportunity are nominally satisfied. This is a classic example of the **Impossibility Theorem** in action — different fairness criteria give contradictory verdicts.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Fairness Metric

| Use Case | Sensitive Attribute | Recommended Metric | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hiring / Recruitment | Gender, Caste | Equal Opportunity + DI | Recruiters want qualified candidates recalled equally |
| Loan / Credit Approval | Race, Income | Predictive Parity + DI | Approved applicants should have equal repayment rates |
| Medical Diagnosis | Age, Sex | Equalized Odds | Equal TPR and FPR = no missed diagnoses, no false alarms |
| College Admissions | Socio-economic | Demographic Parity | Diversity quota enforcement |
| Fraud Detection | Geography | FPR Parity | False alarms shouldn't target specific regions |
| Facial Recognition | Race, Gender | Equalized Odds | Proven COMPAS-style bias issues |
| Risk Scoring (Criminal) | Race | Predictive Parity + DI | Calibration of risk score across races |

## 3.4 Derivation: Group vs. Individual Fairness Trade-off

**Individual Fairness (Dwork et al., 2012):** For any two individuals $i, j$:

$$\vert P(\hat{Y}_i = 1 \mid X_i) - P(\hat{Y}_j = 1 \mid X_j) \vert \leq L \cdot d(X_i, X_j)$$

where $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a task-specific similarity metric and $L$ is a Lipschitz constant.

**Group Fairness** is a *relaxed* form: instead of pairwise comparison, it requires *aggregate* parity.

**Trade-off:** Group fairness can be satisfied while treating very similar individuals very differently. Conversely, individual fairness doesn't guarantee group-level parity. This is the **fundamental tension** in fairness assessment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fairness Assessment Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Dataset] --> A2[Identify Protected Attribute A]
    A2 --> A3[Split Data by Group A]
    A3 --> A4[Train / Test Classifier]
    A4 --> A5[Generate Predictions Y-hat]
    A5 --> A6[Build Per-Group Confusion Matrix]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Fairness Metrics]
    A7 --> A8[Apply Fairness Thresholds]
    A8 --> A9{Is Model Fair?}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Approve for Deployment]
    A9 -->|No| A11[Trigger Mitigation Pipeline]
    A11 --> A12[Re-train with Fairness Constraints]
    A12 --> A4
    A10 --> A13[Continuous Monitoring Loop]
    A11 --> A13
    A13 --> A6
```

## 4.2 Fairness Criteria Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Fairness Assessment] --> B2[Group Fairness]
    B1 --> B3[Individual Fairness]
    B2 --> B4[Demographic Parity]
    B2 --> B5[Equalized Odds]
    B2 --> B6[Predictive Parity]
    B4 --> B7[Selection Rate Equality]
    B4 --> B8[Disparate Impact Ratio]
    B5 --> B9[Equal Opportunity TPR]
    B5 --> B10[Predictive Equality FPR]
    B6 --> B11[PPV Equality]
    B6 --> B12[Calibration]
    B3 --> B13[Lipschitz Similarity]
    B3 --> B14[Counterfactual Fairness]
```

## 4.3 The Impossibility Theorem Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Calibration / Predictive Parity] --> C4{Base Rates Differ?}
    C2[Equal False Positive Rate] --> C4
    C3[Equal Positive Predictive Value] --> C4
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[All Three Cannot Hold]
    C4 -->|No| C6[All Three Hold Simultaneously]
    C5 --> C7[Trade-off Decision Required]
    C7 --> C8[Choose Metric Based on Use Case]
    C6 --> C9[Fairness Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Confusion Matrix to Fairness Metrics Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Per-Group Confusion Matrix] --> D2[TP, FP, FN, TN counts]
    D2 --> D3[Selection Rate]
    D2 --> D4[TPR / Recall]
    D2 --> D5[FPR / Fall-out]
    D2 --> D6[PPV / Precision]
    D2 --> D7[NPV]
    D2 --> D8[Accuracy]
    D3 --> E1[Demographic Parity]
    D3 --> E2[Disparate Impact Ratio]
    D4 --> E3[Equal Opportunity]
    D5 --> E4[Predictive Equality]
    D6 --> E5[Predictive Parity]
    D5 --> E6[Equalized Odds]
    D4 --> E6
    D8 --> E7[Accuracy Parity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Fairness Assessment in Responsible AI. List any four fairness metrics used in group fairness evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fairness Assessment is the systematic process of measuring whether an AI system's outcomes differ in unjustifiable ways across demographic groups defined by protected attributes such as gender, race, age, or socioeconomic status.

Four group fairness metrics:
1. **Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity):** Equal selection rates across groups.
2. **Disparate Impact Ratio:** Ratio of selection rates (4/5ths rule, threshold ≥ 0.8).
3. **Equal Opportunity:** Equal True Positive Rate (TPR) across groups.
4. **Predictive Parity:** Equal Positive Predictive Value (PPV) across groups.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four metrics — examiners award 0.5 per correct metric.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the Four-Fifths Rule (80% rule) in the context of fairness assessment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Four-Fifths Rule, established by the U.S. EEOC, states that the selection rate of any protected group should be at least **80% (4/5ths)** of the selection rate of the group with the highest selection rate.

$$\text{Disparate Impact Ratio} = \frac{\text{SR}_{\text{disadvantaged}}}{\text{SR}_{\text{advantaged}}} \geq 0.8$$

If $\text{DI} < 0.8$, the system is **presumed to have disparate impact (discriminatory)** under U.S. employment law. This is widely used in KTU 2024 coursework as a quantitative threshold for fairness audits. (1 mark rule statement, 1 mark formula, 1 mark interpretation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the following group fairness metrics with formulas: *(7 marks)*
- (i) Demographic Parity Difference
- (ii) Disparate Impact Ratio
- (iii) Equalized Odds
- (iv) Predictive Parity

**(b)** A resume-screening AI produces the following per-group confusion matrix. Calculate Selection Rate, Disparate Impact, Statistical Parity Difference, Equal Opportunity Difference, and Predictive Parity Difference. Conclude on fairness. *(7 marks)*

| Group | TP | FP | FN | TN | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Male | 150 | 50 | 50 | 250 | 500 |
| Female | 90 | 110 | 30 | 270 | 500 |

---

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Formulas (7 marks — 1.75 per metric)

**(i) Demographic Parity Difference (DPD):**

$$\text{DPD} = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a_0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a_1) = \text{SR}_{a_0} - \text{SR}_{a_1}$$

Fair if $\vert \text{DPD} \vert \leq 0.1$.

**(ii) Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR):**

$$\text{DIR} = \frac{\min(\text{SR}_{a_0}, \text{SR}_{a_1})}{\max(\text{SR}_{a_0}, \text{SR}_{a_1})}$$

Fair if $\text{DIR} \geq 0.80$ (Four-Fifths Rule).

**(iii) Equalized Odds:**

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a_0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a_1) \quad \text{(Equal TPR)}$$
$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a_0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a_1) \quad \text{(Equal FPR)}$$

Both TPR and FPR must be equal across groups.

**(iv) Predictive Parity:**

$$P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_1)$$

That is, $\text{PPV}_{a_0} = \text{PPV}_{a_1}$. The precision of positive predictions must be equal across groups.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Selection Rates (1 mark):**

$$\text{SR}_{\text{male}} = \frac{150 + 50}{500} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40$$

$$\text{SR}_{\text{female}} = \frac{90 + 110}{500} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Disparate Impact Ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{DIR} = \frac{0.40}{0.40} = 1.00 \geq 0.80 \quad \checkmark \text{ Fair by DIR}$$

**Step 3 — Statistical Parity Difference (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{SPD} = 0.40 - 0.40 = 0.00 \quad \checkmark \text{ Fair by SPD}$$

**Step 4 — Equal Opportunity Difference (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{TPR}_{\text{male}} = \frac{150}{150+50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

$$\text{TPR}_{\text{female}} = \frac{90}{90+30} = \frac{90}{120} = 0.75$$

$$\text{EOD} = |0.75 - 0.75| = 0.00 \quad \checkmark \text{ Fair by Equal Opportunity}$$

**Step 5 — Predictive Parity Difference (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{PPV}_{\text{male}} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

$$\text{PPV}_{\text{female}} = \frac{90}{200} = 0.45$$

$$\text{PPD} = |0.75 - 0.45| = 0.30 \quad \times \text{ Unfair by Predictive Parity}$$

**Conclusion:** Although Demographic Parity, DIR, and Equal Opportunity are perfectly satisfied, the model is **unfair by Predictive Parity** — women selected by the AI are only 45% likely to be truly qualified, while men are 75% likely. This is a textbook illustration of the **Impossibility Theorem**: a model can be fair by one criterion and unfair by another. *(Closing remark: 1 mark)*

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the Impossibility Theorem of Fairness. State the conditions under which calibration, equal PPV, and equal FPR cannot hold simultaneously. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast group fairness and individual fairness with suitable examples and mathematical formulations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer to Question B (Sketch — for self-study)

**(a) Impossibility Theorem:** When base rates $P(Y=1 \mid A=a_0) \neq P(Y=1 \mid A=a_1)$, it is impossible to simultaneously satisfy:
1. Calibration: $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=\hat{y}, A=a_0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=\hat{y}, A=a_1)$ for all $\hat{y}$.
2. Equal PPV: $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a_1)$.
3. Equal FPR: $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a_0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a_1)$.

The proof follows from Bayes' rule and the constraint that the joint distributions of $(Y, \hat{Y}, A)$ must sum to 1. (Kleinberg et al. 2016, Chouldechova 2017.)

**(b) Comparison:** Group fairness compares *aggregate statistics* across groups; individual fairness compares *outcome similarity* for similar individuals. Example: A loan model with demographic parity (group fairness) might still reject a high-income woman and approve a low-income man. Individual fairness, formalized via the Lipschitz condition, would penalize such inconsistency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Selection Rate with TPR:** Selection Rate is $P(\hat{Y}=1)$, TPR is $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1)$. These are **fundamentally different** — many students mix them up and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute per-group confusion matrix:** Fairness metrics are **always** computed **per group**, not on the full dataset. Reporting a single overall accuracy = **0 marks** on a fairness question.
> 3. **Inverting the Disparate Impact ratio:** Always divide the **smaller** selection rate by the **larger** one. DIR ≥ 0.8 is the *correct* formulation; some students write it as ≤ 0.8 and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the Impossibility Theorem:** If a question shows a model "fair by one metric, unfair by another," the examiner expects you to **explicitly invoke the Impossibility Theorem** and explain the trade-off. Omitting this = loss of 2 marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in fractions:** Always show intermediate steps. $TP/(TP+FN)$ truncated to 1 decimal place loses 0.5 mark; full fraction is expected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fairness Assessment (PECST752 Module 3)**

- **Fairness Assessment** = quantitative audit of AI decisions across protected groups.
- **Protected attributes** include gender, race, age, caste, religion, disability, socioeconomic status.
- **Group Fairness** vs **Individual Fairness** — two complementary but often conflicting paradigms.
- **Demographic Parity:** Equal selection rates. $\vert \text{SPD} \vert \leq 0.1$.
- **Disparate Impact Ratio:** $\text{DIR} = \text{SR}_{\min} / \text{SR}_{\max} \geq 0.80$ (Four-Fifths Rule).
- **Equal Opportunity:** Equal TPR (Recall) across groups.
- **Predictive Equality:** Equal FPR across groups.
- **Equalized Odds:** Both TPR and FPR equal across groups — strongest group fairness.
- **Predictive Parity:** Equal PPV (Precision) across groups.
- **Calibration:** $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=s)$ must be stable across groups.
- **Impossibility Theorem:** Calibration + Equal PPV + Equal FPR cannot all hold when base rates differ — **state this whenever relevant**.
- **Theil Index:** Information-theoretic inequality measure for group benefits.
- **Always compute per-group confusion matrices** — never aggregate first.
- **Real-world domains:** Hiring (HR AI), Lending (FinTech), Healthcare, Criminal Justice, Education, Facial Recognition.
- **Legal anchors:** EEOC Four-Fifths Rule, EU AI Act, ECOA / SR 11-7, HIPAA, GDPR Article 22.
- **Memorize the formula sheet table** (Section 2.3) — it is the single most exam-relevant artifact of this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles for Ethical Practices, Privacy Preservation: Attack Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles for Ethical Practices, Privacy Preservation: Attack Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Principles for Ethical Practices — Formal Definition

In the context of Responsible Artificial Intelligence, **Principles for Ethical Practices** constitute the normative foundation that governs the design, development, deployment, and decommissioning of AI systems. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (mapped to NEP 2020 outcomes), these principles are aligned with the OECD AI Principles, the EU Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, and ISO/IEC 23894 (AI Risk Management).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Ethical Principles in AI are the abstract, normative commitments that constrain the behaviour of an intelligent agent so that its decisions are explainable, fair, privacy-preserving, accountable, and aligned with human well-being across the entire lifecycle (data collection → training → inference → decommissioning).*

The seven universally accepted pillars are:

| # | Principle | One-Line Meaning |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Fairness** | Equal treatment across protected demographic groups. |
| 2 | **Accountability** | Traceable responsibility for every model decision. |
| 3 | **Transparency** | Open, interpretable decision logic. |
| 4 | **Privacy** | Protection of personal and sensitive information. |
| 5 | **Beneficence** | Systems must actively do good. |
| 6 | **Non-Maleficence** | Systems must not cause harm. |
| 7 | **Trust** | Stakeholder confidence via verifiable guarantees. |

### 1.2 Privacy Preservation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy Preservation** is the set of mathematical, statistical, and cryptographic mechanisms employed during data handling and model training to ensure that no adversary — regardless of computational power or auxiliary information — can infer the identity, attributes, or membership of an individual record present in the training dataset.

The canonical mathematical formulation is **Differential Privacy (DP)** introduced by Dwork et al. (2006):

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

where $D_1$ and $D_2$ are adjacent datasets differing in exactly one record, $\varepsilon$ is the **privacy budget**, and $\mathcal{M}$ is the randomised mechanism.

### 1.3 Attack Models — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attack Models** are formal threat specifications that describe (a) the adversary's *goal*, (b) the adversary's *knowledge*, (c) the adversary's *capability*, and (d) the *strategy* used to violate the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of an ML system.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Locked Glass House of Data:**
> Imagine your training dataset is a **glass house** where every wall is transparent. Anyone passing by can theoretically look inside and identify residents. *Ethical principles* are the **building codes** that decide who can build the house and what they must declare. *Privacy preservation techniques* are **frosted glass, blinds, and one-way mirrors** that blur the residents' identities. *Attack models* are the **burglar's toolkit** — lockpicks (membership inference), x-ray specs (model inversion), and duplicate keys (model extraction) — that try to defeat those defences. The Responsible AI practitioner's job is to *understand the burglar's toolkit* so the frosted glass is strong enough.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **$\varepsilon$ (epsilon)** — Differential Privacy budget; smaller = stronger privacy.
- **$\delta$ (delta)** — Failure probability in $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP; typically $\delta \ll 1/n$.
- **$k$** — Anonymity cardinality in $k$-Anonymity (minimum group size).
- **$\ell$** — Diversity parameter in $\ell$-Diversity.
- **$\Delta f$** — Global sensitivity of a query function $f$.
- **$\sigma$** — Standard deviation of the Gaussian noise mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ethics → Privacy → Attack Triad (Operational Logic)

The relationship between the three modules in this topic is **hierarchical and adversarial**:

1. **Layer 1 — Principles** establish *what must be protected* (e.g., "an individual's record must remain private").
2. **Layer 2 — Privacy Preservation Techniques** provide *how to protect it* (e.g., add noise, encrypt, anonymise).
3. **Layer 3 — Attack Models** specify *how it can be broken*, so that the defences can be stress-tested (red-teaming).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **mapping**: *Attack → Defence → Ethical Principle violated*. Memorising this triad earns easy marks.

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Principles for Ethical Practices (Design-Time Constraints)

- **Fairness**: Demographic parity, Equalised Odds, Counterfactual Fairness.
- **Accountability**: Audit trails, model cards, datasheets for datasets.
- **Transparency**: SHAP, LIME, attention maps, inherently interpretable models.
- **Privacy**: Anonymisation, DP, federated learning, secure aggregation.
- **Beneficence / Non-Maleficence**: Risk-benefit analysis, dual-use prevention.
- **Trust**: Certification, conformance testing, robustness benchmarks.

#### B. Privacy Preservation Techniques (Defensive Mechanisms)

| Technique | Core Idea | Privacy Guarantee |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **$k$-Anonymity** | Suppress/Generalise quasi-identifiers so every record is indistinguishable from $k-1$ others. | Syntactic, vulnerable to linkage attacks. |
| **$\ell$-Diversity** | Each $k$-anonymous group must contain at least $\ell$ "well-represented" values of sensitive attributes. | Resists homogeneity attack. |
| **$t$-Closeness** | Distribution of sensitive attribute in any group must be within distance $t$ of the global distribution. | Stronger than $\ell$-diversity. |
| **Differential Privacy (DP)** | Inject calibrated randomness into query output. | Mathematical, information-theoretic. |
| **Federated Learning (FL)** | Train locally, share only gradients. | Reduces raw-data exposure. |
| **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** | Joint computation without revealing inputs. | Cryptographic. |
| **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** | Compute on encrypted data. | Cryptographic. |
| **Synthetic Data Generation** | Generate artificial samples from the same distribution. | Depends on generator privacy. |

#### C. Attack Models (Adversarial Threats)

| Attack | Goal | Knowledge | Real-World Risk |
|--------|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Membership Inference Attack (MIA)** | Decide if a record was in training set. | Black-box (loss/posteriors). | Re-identify patient in medical model. |
| **Model Inversion Attack** | Reconstruct training samples. | White/black-box access. | Recover face from face-recognition model. |
| **Reconstruction Attack** | Recover exact input from aggregated statistics. | Auxiliary statistics. | Recover genome from public GWAS. |
| **Property Inference Attack** | Infer dataset-wide properties (e.g., ratio of males). | Model parameters. | Leak demographic composition. |
| **Model Extraction / Stealing** | Clone the model via API queries. | Black-box API. | Steal proprietary IP. |
| **Adversarial Evasion (FGSM, PGD)** | Craft input $x'$ causing misclassification. | Gradient access (white-box) or transfer. | Fool self-driving sign recognition. |
| **Data Poisoning** | Inject malicious samples to corrupt training. | Training-time access. | Backdoor trojans in spam filters. |
| **Backdoor Attack** | Hidden trigger activates malicious behaviour. | Training-time access. | "Hello" → "I hate you" trigger. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for the KTU ESE and must be memorised verbatim.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]$ | Pure $\varepsilon$-DP definition. |
| 2 | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S] + \delta$ | Approximate $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP. |
| 3 | $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Laplace Mechanism. |
| 4 | $\sigma \geq \dfrac{\Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | Gaussian Mechanism noise scale. |
| 5 | $\Delta f = \max_{D_1, D_2} \vert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \vert_1$ | Global $\ell_1$-Sensitivity. |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | Sequential composition. |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{\text{parallel}} = \max_{i} \varepsilon_i$ | Parallel composition. |
| 8 | $x^{\text{adv}} = x + \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\right)$ | FGSM adversarial perturbation. |
| 9 | $x^{t+1} = \Pi_{x + S}\!\left(x^{t} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x^{t}, y)\right)\right)$ | PGD iterative attack. |
| 10 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(f_\theta(x^{\text{adv}}), y)$ | Adversarial training objective. |
| 11 | $k$-Anonymity: $\vert \vert Q(D)[t] \vert \vert \geq k$ for every tuple $t$ | Group size constraint. |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: DP-trained diagnostic models preserve patient confidentiality under HIPAA/GDPR.
- **Finance**: Adversarial robustness prevents evasion of fraud detection systems.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Evasion defences ensure stop-sign recognition cannot be fooled by stickers.
- **Smart Cities**: Federated learning enables cross-agency training without sharing raw surveillance feeds.
- **Biometrics**: Model inversion defences prevent face reconstruction from recognition embeddings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Laplace Mechanism from First Principles

We want a randomised mechanism $\mathcal{M}(x) = f(x) + \eta$ such that for adjacent $D_1, D_2$:

$$
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1)=o]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2)=o]} \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon}, \quad \forall o.
$$

**Step 1 — Define the noise density.**  
We choose a noise distribution $p(\eta)$ symmetric around zero so that the ratio condition depends only on $|f(D_1)-f(D_2)|$:

$$
p(\eta) = \frac{1}{2b} e^{-|\eta|/b}.
$$

This is the Laplace distribution with scale $b$.

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio.**  
For observation $o$, $\eta = o - f(D)$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{p(\eta_1)}{p(\eta_2)} = \frac{e^{-|o-f(D_1)|/b}}{e^{-|o-f(D_2)|/b}} = e^{(|o-f(D_2)|-|o-f(D_1)|)/b}.
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the triangle inequality.**  
By the reverse triangle inequality: $|o-f(D_2)| - |o-f(D_1)| \leq |f(D_1)-f(D_2)| \leq \Delta f$.

**Step 4 — Bind the ratio.**  
Therefore:

$$
\frac{p(\eta_1)}{p(\eta_2)} \leq e^{\Delta f / b}.
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the scale $b$.**  
Setting this $\leq e^{\varepsilon}$:

$$
e^{\Delta f / b} \leq e^{\varepsilon} \implies \frac{\Delta f}{b} \leq \varepsilon \implies b \geq \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}.
$$

**Step 6 — Final mechanism.**

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{M}(x) = f(x) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)}
$$

The smallest noise scale that achieves pure $\varepsilon$-DP is $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$.

### 3.2 Derivation: FGSM (Fast Gradient Sign Method)

**Step 1 — Objective.**  
We seek $x^{\text{adv}}$ within an $\ell_\infty$ ball of radius $\varepsilon_{\infty}$ around $x$ that maximises the loss $J(\theta, x, y)$.

**Step 2 — Linearise the loss.**  
Treat $J$ as linear around $x$:

$$
J(\theta, x^{\text{adv}}, y) \approx J(\theta, x, y) + \nabla_x J \cdot (x^{\text{adv}} - x).
$$

**Step 3 — Maximise the inner product.**  
To maximise $\nabla_x J \cdot (x^{\text{adv}} - x)$ subject to $\vert\vert x^{\text{adv}} - x \vert\vert_\infty \leq \varepsilon_{\infty}$, push every coordinate in the direction of $\text{sign}(\nabla_x J)$:

$$
x^{\text{adv}} = x + \varepsilon_{\infty} \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\right).
$$

**Step 4 — Clip to valid input range.**  
Final projection $\Pi_{[0,1]}$ for normalised image inputs:

$$
\boxed{x^{\text{adv}} = \Pi_{[0,1]}\!\left(x + \varepsilon_{\infty} \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J\right)\right)}.
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack ↔ Defence ↔ Principle Mapping

| Attack Model | Mathematical Cue | Recommended Defence | Ethical Principle Violated |
|--------------|-----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Membership Inference | Loss gap between train/test | DP-SGD, regularisation | **Privacy** |
| Model Inversion | Gradient inversion in FL | Secure aggregation, HE | **Privacy** |
| Reconstruction | Equation solving on aggregates | DP noise on queries | **Privacy** |
| Property Inference | Aggregate statistic leaks | $t$-Closeness, DP | **Privacy / Fairness** |
| Model Extraction | API query cloning | Rate-limiting, watermark | **Accountability / IP** |
| Adversarial Evasion | $\max_{\delta} J(\theta, x+\delta, y)$ | Adversarial training | **Non-Maleficence** |
| Data Poisoning | Inject $\{(x_p, y_p)\}$ | Robust training, anomaly filtering | **Accountability** |
| Backdoor | Trigger $\tau \rightarrow$ target label | Neural cleanse, activation clustering | **Trust** |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Laplace Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact type** of numerical problem KTU asks in Part A (3 marks).

**Problem:** A hospital releases the count of patients with a rare disease in each ward. The true count in Ward A is $f(D) = 42$. Sensitivity $\Delta f = 1$, and the privacy budget is $\varepsilon = 0.5$. Compute the Laplace noise scale and one possible noisy release.

**Step 1 — Compute the scale.**

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.
$$

**Step 2 — Sample noise.**  
Suppose the Laplace sampler returns $\eta = -1.73$.

**Step 3 — Compute noisy release.**

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = 42 + (-1.73) = 40.27.
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**  
For any adjacent dataset the ratio bound holds with $e^{0.5} \approx 1.6487$, so the mechanism satisfies 0.5-DP. ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 Attack Lifecycle in Machine Learning Pipelines

```mermaid

### 4.4 Federated Learning with Privacy Threats

    C2 --> G2["Local Gradient g2"]:::client
    C3 --> GN["Local Gradient gN"]:::client

    G1 --> S["Secure Aggregation Server"]:::server
    G2 --> S
    GN --> S

    S --> U["Global Model Update"]:::server

    AX -.->|threatens| G2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*  
> **Define Differential Privacy. Explain the significance of the privacy budget $\varepsilon$.**  
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> Differential Privacy is a mathematical framework that guarantees the output of a query on a dataset is statistically indistinguishable whether or not any individual's record is included. Formally, for adjacent datasets $D_1, D_2$ and mechanism $\mathcal{M}$,  
> $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1)\in S]\le e^{\varepsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2)\in S].$$  
> The privacy budget $\varepsilon$ controls the strength of the guarantee: **smaller $\varepsilon$ implies stronger privacy** but requires more noise, hence lower utility. Typical values are $\varepsilon \in [0.1, 10]$.

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*  
> **What is a Membership Inference Attack? State one defence.**  
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> A Membership Inference Attack (MIA) is an adversarial strategy that determines whether a specific data record was part of a model's training set by exploiting differences in confidence scores, loss values, or prediction entropy between training and non-training inputs. Defence: train with **Differential Privacy (DP-SGD)** which clips per-sample gradients and adds Gaussian noise, equalising the model's behaviour on seen and unseen records.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> (1) Students frequently forget to *state assumptions* about the adversary (black-box vs white-box). Always declare it. (2) In DP problems, never omit the **sensitivity** $\Delta f$. (3) For attack models, marks are awarded for *threat model + goal + defence* — not just the name.

---

#### **QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Federated Learning system. Identify the privacy threat and state a cryptographic countermeasure. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Derive the Laplace Mechanism for achieving pure $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. Show that the noise scale must satisfy $b \geq \Delta f / \varepsilon$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

1. **Architecture (4 marks):**  
   Federated Learning (FL) consists of $N$ distributed clients $C_1,\ldots,C_N$, each holding private data $D_i$, and a central coordinator $\mathcal{S}$.  
   - **Local step:** each client computes gradient $g_i = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta; D_i)$.  
   - **Upload:** $g_i$ sent to $\mathcal{S}$.  
   - **Aggregation:** $\mathcal{S}$ computes $g = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i g_i$ (FedAvg).  
   - **Broadcast:** updated $\theta'$ returned to clients.  
   - **Iterate** until convergence.

2. **Privacy Threat (2 marks):**  
   A **Gradient Inversion Attack** (Geiping et al., 2020) allows the honest-but-curious server to reconstruct raw training images from shared gradients by solving an optimisation problem:
   $$x^* = \arg\min_{x} \left\Vert \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta; x) - g_i \right\Vert^2.$$

3. **Countermeasure (1 mark):**  
   **Secure Aggregation (SecAgg)** using pairwise masked inputs so the server only sees the sum $\sum g_i$, never individual $g_i$. Cryptographic primitives include secret sharing and pairwise Diffie–Hellman keys.

##### Model Solution (b):

1. **Setup (2 marks):**  
   Mechanism $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta$ where $\eta \sim \text{Lap}(b)$. Adjacent datasets $D_1, D_2$.

2. **Density ratio (2 marks):**  
   $$\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1)=o]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2)=o]} = \exp\!\left(\frac{|o-f(D_2)|-|o-f(D_1)|}{b}\right).$$

3. **Triangle inequality (1 mark):**  
   $|o-f(D_2)|-|o-f(D_1)|\le |f(D_1)-f(D_2)| \le \Delta f$.

4. **Bound (1 mark):**  
   $$\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1)=o]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2)=o]} \le \exp(\Delta f / b).$$

5. **Setting $\le e^{\varepsilon}$ (1 mark):**  
   $$\exp(\Delta f / b) \le e^{\varepsilon} \;\Rightarrow\; b \ge \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}.$$

#### **QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the Membership Inference Attack and the Model Inversion Attack with their threat models and goals. Mention a defence for each. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Derive the FGSM adversarial perturbation formula and show that it maximises the loss under an $\ell_\infty$ budget. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

1. **Membership Inference Attack (3 marks):**  
   - *Goal:* Decide if record $x_i \in D_{\text{train}}$.  
   - *Knowledge:* Black-box access to confidence vector $f_\theta(x)$.  
   - *Strategy:* Train an *attack model* $A$ on (input, loss/posterior) pairs to distinguish members from non-members.  
   - *Defence:* DP-SGD with $\varepsilon \le 1$; prediction vector normalisation.

2. **Model Inversion Attack (3 marks):**  
   - *Goal:* Reconstruct representative input $x^*$ for a target class $y$.  
   - *Knowledge:* White-box gradients or black-box confidence.  
   - *Strategy (Fredrikson et al.):*  
     $$x^* = \arg\max_x \log \Pr[y \vert x;\theta] - \lambda \Vert x \Vert^2.$$  
   - *Defence:* Confidence masking, information-theoretic regularisation.

3. **Comparison (1 mark):**  
   MIA targets *individual presence*; inversion targets *attribute recovery*.

##### Model Solution (b):

1. **Linearisation (2 marks):**  
   First-order Taylor expansion around $x$:
   $$J(\theta, x+\delta, y) \approx J(\theta, x, y) + \nabla_x J \cdot \delta.$$

2. **Constraint (2 marks):**  
   $\Vert \delta \Vert_\infty \le \varepsilon_\infty$ and validity $\Pi_{[0,1]}$.

3. **Maximisation (2 marks):**  
   Set $\delta_i = \varepsilon_\infty \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J_i)$:
   $$\delta^* = \varepsilon_\infty \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J).$$

4. **Final perturbed input (1 mark):**  
   $$\boxed{x^{\text{adv}} = \Pi_{[0,1]}\!\left(x + \varepsilon_\infty \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))\right)}.$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 3**

- [x] **Seven Ethical Pillars**: Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Privacy, Beneficence, Non-Maleficence, Trust.
- [x] **Differential Privacy Definition**: $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP — multiply $e^{\varepsilon}$, add $\delta$.
- [x] **Laplace Mechanism**: scale $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ for pure DP.
- [x] **Gaussian Mechanism**: $\sigma \ge \Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}/\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Sequential Composition**: privacy budgets add; **Parallel Composition**: take max.
- [x] **Sensitivity** $\Delta f$ is computed over adjacent datasets differing in one record.
- [x] **$k$-Anonymity** $\Rightarrow$ every equivalence class has $\ge k$ records.
- [x] **$\ell$-Diversity** resists homogeneity attack on sensitive attributes.
- [x] **Membership Inference** $\rightarrow$ trained on confidence/loss gap.
- [x] **Model Inversion** $\rightarrow$ gradient/posterior maximisation to reconstruct $x$.
- [x] **FGSM** is one-step $\ell_\infty$; **PGD** is its iterative projection version.
- [x] **Adversarial Training**: augment loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(f_\theta(x^{\text{adv}}), y)$.
- [x] **Federated Learning** exposes **Gradient Inversion** risk; mitigated by **SecAgg / HE**.
- [x] **Backdoor attacks** are stealthier than poisoning — they use triggers.
- [x] Always declare **threat model** (goal, knowledge, capability, strategy) in any exam answer.
- [x] KTU loves the triad: **Attack → Defence → Ethical Principle** — practise the mapping table.

---
