topics.title: Privacy-Preserving Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Privacy-Preserving Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Privacy-Preserving Learning (PPL)**, often synonymously referred to as **Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML)**, is the branch of Responsible Artificial Intelligence that deals with the design, training, inference, and deployment of machine learning models in such a way that the **confidentiality, integrity, and unlinkability** of the training data — and sometimes the model itself — is mathematically guaranteed, even against adversaries who may legitimately participate in the learning process or observe its outputs.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECST752 syllabus (Module 3)**, PPL encompasses four canonical pillars:

1. **Differential Privacy (DP)** – statistical noise-based data obfuscation.
2. **Federated Learning (FL)** – decentralized model training without data centralization.
3. **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** – computation directly on encrypted data.
4. **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** – cryptographic protocols for joint computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Privacy-Preserving Learning is a set of algorithmic and cryptographic techniques that allow machine learning models to be trained on sensitive datasets without exposing, leaking, or memorizing the individual data records, while still retaining acceptable model utility."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Blindfolded Chef"

Imagine a **world-class chef (the ML model)** who wants to learn the secret recipes of **100 master cooks (the data owners)**. The cooks refuse to hand over their recipe books because the recipes are proprietary. How can the chef still learn to cook amazingly?

| Real-World Analogy | Technical PPL Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cooks only share recipes with **random ingredients swapped** (e.g., "sugar" replaced with "salt" 50% of the time) | **Differential Privacy** — calibrated noise added to data |
| Each cook **trains a small mini-chef at home** on their own kitchen; only the **learned skills** are sent to the head chef | **Federated Learning** — local training, shared gradients |
| Recipes are **locked in a safe**; the chef gives instructions to operate on the locked safe | **Homomorphic Encryption** — computation on ciphertexts |
| Cooks perform the cooking **jointly in a locked kitchen**; no one sees another's recipe but the final dish is correct | **Secure Multi-Party Computation** — secret sharing |

The chef never sees the actual recipes (raw data), yet ends up producing a world-class dish (an accurate model). **That is the essence of Privacy-Preserving Learning.**

---

## 1.3 Why is Privacy-Preserving Learning Necessary?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Foundational Threats Addressed by PPL:**
>
> 1. **Membership Inference Attacks** — An adversary deduces whether a specific person's record was in the training set.
> 2. **Model Inversion / Reconstruction Attacks** — Sensitive training attributes are recovered from model weights or outputs.
> 3. **Data Leakage via Memorization** — Large language models and deep networks can verbatim memorize and regurgitate training samples.

Real-world cases such as the **Netflix Prize de-anonymization (2007)**, the **Strava heatmap military-base exposure (2018)**, and the **GPT-style memorization of emails** have made PPL a **regulatory and ethical necessity** under frameworks like the **EU GDPR**, **India's DPDP Act 2023**, and the **US HIPAA**.

---

## 1.4 The Two Central Metrics in PPL

Any KTU answer on PPL must explicitly mention the two governing quantitative parameters:

- **ε (Epsilon) — the Privacy Budget**: A smaller ε means **stronger privacy** but **more noise** and **lower utility**. A larger ε means weaker privacy but better model accuracy.
- **δ (Delta) — the Failure Probability**: A small slack term that allows the privacy guarantee to be broken with negligible probability. Standard recommendation: **δ << 1/n** where *n* is the dataset size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both **ε** and **δ** are **unitless** real numbers, with the convention that **ε ∈ (0, 1]** and **δ ∈ [0, 1]** for meaningful guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Operational Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Differential Privacy (DP)

DP is the **gold-standard mathematical framework** for privacy. Its core philosophy: *the output of a computation should be nearly identical whether or not any single individual's data is included in the input.*

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Define a **neighboring dataset** $D$ and $D'$ that differ in exactly one record.
2. Define a **randomized mechanism** $\mathcal{M}$ that adds calibrated noise.
3. Ensure that for all possible outputs $S$:
   - The probability ratio of producing $S$ from $D$ vs $D'$ is bounded by $e^{\varepsilon}$.
4. The mechanism is **ε-differentially private** if the bound holds for every $S$.

### Pillar 2: Federated Learning (FL)

FL is a **distributed training paradigm** introduced by Google in 2016 (H. B. McMahan et al.) where:

1. The central server broadcasts a **global model** to all clients.
2. Each client trains **locally** on its private data.
3. Clients send **only model updates (gradients/weights)**, never raw data.
4. The server **aggregates** the updates (typically via FedAvg).
5. The cycle repeats until convergence.

### Pillar 3: Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

HE allows a third party to perform computations on encrypted data **without ever decrypting it**. Categories include:

- **Partially Homomorphic** (PHE) — supports one operation infinitely (e.g., Paillier for addition).
- **Somewhat Homomorphic** (SHE) — supports limited additions and multiplications.
- **Fully Homomorphic** (FHE) — supports arbitrary computations (e.g., CKKS, BFV, BGV schemes).

### Pillar 4: Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)

Multiple parties jointly compute a function over their inputs while keeping those inputs **private**. Examples include **Yao's Garbled Circuits** and **Shamir's Secret Sharing**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for solving KTU 2024 PPL numerical/derivation problems. Memorize these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ε-Differential Privacy (pure)** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget (unitless) | Foundation of every DP mechanism |
| 2 | **Approximate DP (ε, δ)** | Same as above with additive slack $\delta$ | $\delta$ = failure prob. (unitless) | Practical relaxation used in DP-SGD |
| 3 | **Global Sensitivity ($\Delta f$)** | $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$ | $\Delta f$ = scalar sensitivity | Used to calibrate noise scale |
| 4 | **Laplace Mechanism Noise Scale** | $b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$ | $b$ = Laplace scale parameter | Adds Laplace$(0, b)$ noise |
| 5 | **Gaussian Mechanism Noise Scale** | $\sigma \ge \frac{\Delta f \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation of Gaussian | Used in DP-SGD for deep learning |
| 6 | **Sequential Composition** | $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | — | Privacy loss adds across $k$ queries |
| 7 | **Parallel Composition** | $\varepsilon_{total} = \max(\varepsilon_i)$ | — | Privacy loss bounded by max for disjoint sets |
| 8 | **FedAvg Aggregation** | $w_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}$ | $w$ = weights, $n_k$ = client $k$ samples | Standard FL aggregation rule |
| 9 | **DP-SGD Gradient Clipping** | $\tilde{g}_i = \frac{g_i}{\max(1, \Vert g_i \Vert_2 / C)}$ | $C$ = clipping threshold | Bounds per-sample sensitivity |
| 10 | **Privacy Amplification by Subsampling** | $\varepsilon' \approx \frac{q^2 \, T \, \varepsilon^2}{\,}$ (Tighter bounds via Abadi et al.) | $q$ = sampling rate, $T$ = steps | Stronger guarantee via random subsampling |
| 11 | **Renyi Differential Privacy (RDP)** | $D_{\alpha}(\mathcal{M}(D) \Vert \mathcal{M}(D'))$ | $\alpha$ = Renyi order | Used in modern DP-SGD analysis |
| 12 | **Moments Accountant** | Combines RDP orders to bound $(\varepsilon, \delta)$ | — | Tight tracking of cumulative privacy loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notational Rule (KTU 2024):** The expression $\Vert x \Vert_1$ (L1 norm / absolute value) must be written as `\Vert x \Vert_1` in LaTeX, **never** as `\vert x \vert_1`, to avoid table-rendering errors in markdown.

---

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **Laplace Mechanism (Eq. 4):** Used by the **US Census Bureau** since 2020 to release privacy-preserving demographic statistics.
- **Gaussian Mechanism (Eq. 5):** Powers **DP-SGD in PyTorch Opacus / TensorFlow TF Privacy** for training differentially private deep neural networks.
- **FedAvg (Eq. 8):** Deployed in **Google Gboard** for next-word prediction across millions of Android phones without sending keystrokes to the server.
- **DP-SGD Clipping (Eq. 9):** Used by **Apple** in its emoji usage analytics and by **Meta** in ad-conversion modeling.

---

## 2.4 The Privacy–Utility Trade-Off

The **fundamental tension** of PPL is captured by:

$$U(\varepsilon) = U_0 - \alpha \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \right)$$

Where $U(\varepsilon)$ is the model utility, $U_0$ is the non-private baseline utility, and $\alpha$ is a dataset-dependent constant. As $\varepsilon \to 0$, utility collapses; as $\varepsilon \to \infty$, privacy collapses. **The art of PPL is finding the sweet spot.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calibrating Laplace Noise for ε-Differential Privacy

**Problem Statement:** Given a counting query $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$, derive the noise scale required to make the mechanism $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta$ ε-differentially private, where $\eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)$.

---

### Step 1: Determine the Global Sensitivity of the Counting Query

The counting query operates on two neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ that differ in exactly one record. Suppose record $i$ flips from $x_i = 0$ to $x_i = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1 \\
&= \max_{D, D'} \left\vert \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j^{(D)} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j^{(D')} \right\vert \\
&= \max_{D, D'} \left\vert x_i^{(D)} - x_i^{(D')} \right\vert \\
&= \left\vert 1 - 0 \right\vert = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Since only one record changes by at most 1 (binary), the sum differs by at most 1. Therefore, **$\Delta f = 1$** for any counting query.

---

### Step 2: Recall the Definition of ε-Differential Privacy

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-DP if for all neighboring $D, D'$ and all measurable $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

**Explanation:** The privacy guarantee is the **multiplicative bound** on the output distribution. A smaller $\varepsilon$ tightens the bound (more privacy), a larger $\varepsilon$ loosens it (less privacy).

---

### Step 3: Write the Probability Density of the Laplace Mechanism

The Laplace density with scale $b > 0$ centered at 0 is:

$$\text{PDF}(z) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert z \vert}{b} \right)$$

**Explanation:** This is the symmetric, two-sided exponential distribution. The "scale" $b$ controls spread; larger $b$ means more noise.

For our mechanism $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta$, the density of the output $z$ is:

$$p_D(z) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert z - f(D) \vert}{b} \right)$$

---

### Step 4: Form the Likelihood Ratio

We need to bound:

$$\frac{p_D(z)}{p_{D'}(z)} \le e^{\varepsilon}$$

Substituting the Laplace densities:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_D(z)}{p_{D'}(z)} &= \frac{\frac{1}{2b} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert z - f(D) \vert}{b} \right)}{\frac{1}{2b} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert z - f(D') \vert}{b} \right)} \\
&= \exp\left( \frac{\vert z - f(D') \vert - \vert z - f(D) \vert}{b} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** The $\frac{1}{2b}$ terms cancel. We are left with the exponential of the **difference of absolute distances** between the noisy output $z$ and the two true query answers.

---

### Step 5: Apply the Triangle Inequality to Bound the Numerator

By the **reverse triangle inequality**:

$$\vert a \vert - \vert b \vert \le \vert a - b \vert$$

Setting $a = z - f(D')$ and $b = z - f(D)$:

$$\vert z - f(D') \vert - \vert z - f(D) \vert \le \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \le \Delta f$$

**Explanation:** The difference in absolute distances can never exceed the difference in the underlying values, which is bounded by global sensitivity.

---

### Step 6: Combine Steps 4 and 5 to Solve for $b$

From Steps 4 and 5:

$$\frac{p_D(z)}{p_{D'}(z)} \le \exp\left( \frac{\Delta f}{b} \right)$$

For this to be $\le e^{\varepsilon}$ for **all** $z$ and all neighboring $D, D'$:

$$\exp\left( \frac{\Delta f}{b} \right) \le e^{\varepsilon}$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{b} \le \varepsilon$$

Solving for $b$:

$$\boxed{\, b \ge \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \,}$$

**Final Result:** The minimum Laplace scale parameter that guarantees ε-DP is $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$. For our counting query with $\Delta f = 1$, we get $b = 1/\varepsilon$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: FedAvg Update Rule

**Problem Statement:** Derive the global model update formula for Federated Learning with $K$ clients under the FedAvg algorithm.

---

### Step 1: Local Update on Each Client

Each client $k$ initializes from the global model $w_t$ and performs $E$ epochs of SGD on its local dataset $\mathcal{D}_k$ of size $n_k$:

$$w_{t+1}^{(k)} = w_t - \eta \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(w_t)$$

**Explanation:** Each client independently minimizes its own local loss $\mathcal{L}_k$. The local data never leaves the device.

---

### Step 2: Server Aggregation

The server receives all $K$ client models and aggregates them as a **weighted average**, where the weight of client $k$ is proportional to the size of its local dataset:

$$w_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}$$

Where $n = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$ is the total number of samples across all clients.

**Explanation:** Clients with more data have more influence on the global model. This is mathematically equivalent to minimizing the **empirical risk** $\frac{1}{n} \sum_k n_k \mathcal{L}_k$.

---

### Step 3: Verification — Unbiasedness

If each client's local optimum is the global optimum $w^\ast$, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[w_{t+1}] &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} \cdot w^\ast \\
&= w^\ast \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} \\
&= w^\ast \cdot 1 = w^\ast
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** The FedAvg aggregation preserves the optimal solution in expectation, ensuring convergence in the non-private IID setting.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing PPL Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Comparison:** Be ready to write this comparison in 2-3 marks questions.

| Dimension | Differential Privacy | Federated Learning | Homomorphic Encryption | SMPC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Privacy Strength** | Strong (mathematical) | Weak (only hides raw data) | Very Strong | Very Strong |
| **Utility Loss** | Medium-High | Low | Low | Low |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | Very High | High |
| **Communication Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Very High | Very High |
| **Trust Assumption** | Trusted curator | Semi-honest server | Untrusted server | Untrusted peers |
| **Implementation Ease** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Real Deployment** | US Census, Apple | Google Gboard | Medical imaging (rare) | Crypto wallets |
| **Resistance to Inference Attack** | Yes (provable) | No (gradients can leak) | Yes | Yes |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example for KTU Board Practice

**Question:** A hospital wants to release the **average age of patients** in a dataset of 10,000 records. The global sensitivity of the mean query is $\Delta f = 1/10000 = 10^{-4}$. If the hospital chooses $\varepsilon = 0.1$, calculate the required Laplace noise scale.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= 10^{-4} \\
\varepsilon &= 0.1 \\
b &= \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{10^{-4}}{0.1} = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The hospital must add **Laplace noise with scale $b = 10^{-3}$** (i.e., $b = 0.001$) to the mean age to satisfy $\varepsilon = 0.1$ differential privacy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: End-to-End Differential Privacy Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensitive Dataset D] --> B[Define Neighboring Datasets D and D Prime]
    B --> C[Compute Global Sensitivity delta f]
    C --> D[Choose Privacy Budget epsilon]
    D --> E[Calibrate Noise Scale b equals delta f over epsilon]
    E --> F[Apply Laplace Mechanism M of D equals f of D plus eta]
    F --> G[Add Laplace zero b Noise eta]
    G --> H[Publish Noisy Output z]
    H --> I[Adversary Attempts Membership Inference]
    I --> J[Privacy Guarantee epsilon DP Holds]
    J --> K[Utility Slightly Reduced but Acceptable]

    style A fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style H fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style J fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#0000cc,color:#000
    style K fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram traces a single record from raw sensitive data through the DP pipeline to a privacy-preserving released statistic. The adversary at step I cannot reliably distinguish whether any individual record was in $D$ or $D'$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Federated Learning Architecture (FedAvg)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Central_Server
        S1[Global Model w sub t]
        S2[Aggregator FedAvg]
        S3[Updated Global Model w sub t plus 1]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
    end

    subgraph Client_1
        C1A[Local Data D sub 1]
        C1B[Local Training SGD on D1]
        C1A --> C1B
    end

    subgraph Client_2
        C2A[Local Data D sub 2]
        C2B[Local Training SGD on D2]
        C2A --> C2B
    end

    subgraph Client_3
        C3A[Local Data D sub 3]
        C3B[Local Training SGD on D3]
        C3A --> C3B
    end

    S1 -.broadcast.-> C1B
    S1 -.broadcast.-> C2B
    S1 -.broadcast.-> C3B

    C1B -.model update.-> S2
    C2B -.model update.-> S2
    C3B -.model update.-> S2

    style S1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C1A fill:#fff0e0,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C2A fill:#fff0e0,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C3A fill:#fff0e0,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The global model is broadcast to all clients (dashed arrows), each trains locally, and only model updates (not data) are sent back for aggregation. **Data never leaves the client device.**

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Comparative Block Architecture of PPL Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Statistical_Noise_Family
        DP1[Raw Data] --> DP2[Add Laplace Noise] --> DP3[Noisy Statistics]
    end

    subgraph Distributed_Family
        FL1[Client 1 Data] --> FL2[Local Model]
        FL3[Client 2 Data] --> FL2
        FL2 --> FL4[Aggregate Gradients] --> FL5[Global Model]
    end

    subgraph Cryptographic_Family
        HE1[Plaintext Data] --> HE2[Encrypt with Public Key] --> HE3[Compute on Ciphertext] --> HE4[Decrypt Result]
    end

    style DP1 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style DP3 fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style FL1 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style FL3 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style FL5 fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style HE1 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style HE2 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    style HE3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style HE4 fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** Three families of PPL — statistical (DP), distributed (FL), and cryptographic (HE) — each with their own data flow. Cryptographic methods have an additional encryption/decryption step making them computationally expensive.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: The Privacy–Utility Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[epsilon equals 0 Maximum Privacy] --> B[Very Noisy Output]
    B --> C[Low Model Accuracy]
    C --> D[High Distortion]
    D --> E[Privacy Preserved]

    F[epsilon equals large Weak Privacy] --> G[Low Noise]
    G --> H[High Model Accuracy]
    H --> I[Low Distortion]
    I --> J[Privacy Compromised]

    K[Optimal Sweet Spot epsilon around 1] --> L[Balanced Privacy and Utility]

    style A fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style K fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The trade-off curve forces a choice between privacy strength and model utility. The KTU answer must explicitly state that **neither extreme is acceptable in real Responsible AI deployments**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Define ε-Differential Privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget ε in the trade-off between privacy and model utility.**

---

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Target: 70–90 words):**

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **ε-differential privacy** if for all neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ (differing in one record) and all measurable output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

The parameter **ε (epsilon)** is the **privacy budget** that controls the strength of the guarantee. A **smaller ε** means **stronger privacy** (tighter bound on output distributions) but requires **more noise**, which **degrades model utility**. A **larger ε** means **weaker privacy** but **higher accuracy**. This is the fundamental **privacy–utility trade-off** at the heart of PPL.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formal definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for trade-off explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between Federated Learning and traditional centralized Machine Learning with respect to data locality, privacy, and communication overhead.**

---

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Target: 80–100 words):**

| Aspect | Centralized ML | Federated Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Locality** | Data is aggregated to a central server | Data **remains on client devices** |
| **Privacy** | High risk of breach at central server | Raw data **never leaves the device** |
| **Communication Overhead** | One-time upload of all data | **Repeated rounds** of model-update exchange |
| **Model Training Location** | Server-side | **Client-side local** training |
| **Privacy Guarantee** | None (data exposed) | Weak (gradients can still leak) — must be paired with **DP or HE** |

In Federated Learning, only **model parameters (weights/gradients)** are transmitted, preserving data privacy at the cost of higher communication bandwidth across multiple training rounds.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both are provided below for practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Laplace Mechanism** for achieving ε-differential privacy. Derive the noise scale $b$ required to make a counting query of global sensitivity $\Delta f$ ε-differentially private. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **architecture of Federated Learning** using the **FedAvg algorithm**. Write the global aggregation formula and explain why FedAvg is considered a privacy-preserving technique. State **two limitations** of vanilla Federated Learning without additional cryptographic protection. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Definition of Laplace Mechanism (1 Mark)**
The Laplace Mechanism adds noise drawn from a zero-mean Laplace distribution with scale $b$ to the true query output:

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)$$

**Step 2: Definition of Global Sensitivity (1 Mark)**
For two neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$$

**Step 3: PDF of Laplace Distribution (1 Mark)**

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert z \vert}{b} \right)$$

**Step 4: Form the Likelihood Ratio (1 Mark)**

$$\frac{p_D(z)}{p_{D'}(z)} = \exp\left( \frac{\vert z - f(D') \vert - \vert z - f(D) \vert}{b} \right)$$

**Step 5: Apply the Reverse Triangle Inequality (1 Mark)**

$$\vert z - f(D') \vert - \vert z - f(D) \vert \le \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \le \Delta f$$

**Step 6: Bound by $e^{\varepsilon}$ and Solve (2 Marks)**

$$\exp\left( \frac{\Delta f}{b} \right) \le e^{\varepsilon} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta f}{b} \le \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\, b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \,}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: FL Architecture Description (2 Marks)**
Federated Learning consists of a **central server** and $K$ **clients** (e.g., mobile devices, hospitals). The server maintains a global model $w_t$ at round $t$. Each client $k$ has a private local dataset $\mathcal{D}_k$ of size $n_k$.

**Step 2: FedAvg Algorithm Steps (2 Marks)**

1. Server initializes $w_0$ and broadcasts to all clients.
2. Each client $k$ trains locally for $E$ epochs using SGD on $\mathcal{D}_k$, producing local model $w_{t+1}^{(k)}$.
3. Clients transmit $w_{t+1}^{(k)}$ to the server (no raw data).
4. Server aggregates:

$$w_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}$$

5. Repeat for $T$ rounds.

**Step 3: Why FedAvg is Privacy-Preserving (1 Mark)**
Raw training data **never leaves the client device**. Only aggregated model parameters traverse the network, reducing the attack surface compared to centralized training.

**Step 4: Two Limitations (2 Marks)**
1. **Gradient Leakage:** Sophisticated attacks (e.g., Deep Leakage from Gradients, 2019) can reconstruct training images from shared gradients, exposing private data.
2. **No Formal Privacy Guarantee:** FedAvg alone provides no provable ε-DP bound. It must be combined with **DP-SGD, secure aggregation, or HE** to achieve mathematical privacy guarantees.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)**? Compare **Partially**, **Somewhat**, and **Fully Homomorphic Encryption** schemes. Explain why HE is computationally expensive. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **DP-SGD (Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent)** algorithm used to train deep learning models. Explain the role of **gradient clipping** and **Gaussian noise injection** in achieving $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Definition of Homomorphic Encryption (2 Marks)**
Homomorphic Encryption is a cryptographic scheme that allows computations to be performed directly on **ciphertext**, producing an encrypted result that, when decrypted, matches the result of the same computation performed on the plaintext. Formally:

$$\text{Dec}_k(\text{Eval}(f, \text{Enc}_k(m))) = f(m)$$

Where $\text{Enc}_k$ and $\text{Dec}_k$ are encryption/decryption with key $k$, and $f$ is an arbitrary function.

**Step 2: Comparison of HE Variants (3 Marks)**

| Type | Supported Operations | Example Schemes | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Partially HE (PHE)** | Either unlimited addition OR unlimited multiplication, but not both | Paillier (additive), RSA (multiplicative), ElGamal | E-voting, simple aggregations |
| **Somewhat HE (SHE)** | Limited number of additions AND multiplications | BGV, BFV, CKKS (leveled) | Private ML inference on shallow networks |
| **Fully HE (FHE)** | Arbitrary additions and multiplications | TFHE, FHEW, CKKS (bootstrapped) | Arbitrary private ML and cloud computing |

**Step 3: Why HE is Computationally Expensive (2 Marks)**
1. **Ciphertext Expansion:** Plaintext bits expand to thousands of bits in ciphertext (especially in FHE schemes like BFV/BGV).
2. **Bootstrapping:** FHE requires periodic **bootstrapping** to refresh noise in the ciphertext — this is the dominant cost, often taking seconds to minutes per gate operation.
3. **Polynomial Arithmetic:** Operations are performed on large polynomials in rings $\mathbb{Z}_q[x] / (x^n + 1)$, which is orders of magnitude slower than plaintext arithmetic.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: What is DP-SGD (1 Mark)**
DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) is a modification of standard SGD that injects calibrated Gaussian noise into clipped per-sample gradients, providing $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy for the trained model.

**Step 2: Per-Sample Gradient Computation (1 Mark)**
For each training example $x_i$ with loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta, x_i)$, compute the gradient $g_i = \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta, x_i)$ independently.

**Step 3: Gradient Clipping (2 Marks)**
To bound the per-sample sensitivity, each gradient is clipped to have L2 norm at most $C$:

$$\tilde{g}_i = \frac{g_i}{\max\left(1, \frac{\Vert g_i \Vert_2}{C}\right)}$$

This ensures $\Vert \tilde{g}_i \Vert_2 \le C$ for all $i$, bounding the influence of any single training example.

**Step 4: Noise Injection (1 Mark)**
Gaussian noise calibrated to the clipping threshold $C$ and privacy parameters $(\varepsilon, \delta)$ is added:

$$\tilde{g} = \frac{1}{L} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{L} \tilde{g}_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I) \right)$$

Where the noise scale is $\sigma \ge \frac{\sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ (from Eq. 5 in the cheat sheet).

**Step 5: Privacy Accounting (1 Mark)**
The Moments Accountant (or RDP) tracks the cumulative privacy loss across $T$ training steps, yielding a final $(\varepsilon, \delta)$ bound.

**Step 6: Practical Deployments (1 Mark)**
Implemented in **PyTorch Opacus** and **TensorFlow Privacy**, used by **Meta**, **Apple**, and **Google** for privacy-preserving model training.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in PECST752 Module 3 PPL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the triangle inequality step** in the Laplace derivation — students often jump from likelihood ratio directly to $b = \Delta f/\varepsilon$ without showing the intermediate bound. **Cost: 2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Confusing ε with δ:** ε is the privacy budget (controls noise level), δ is the failure probability (small slack). Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchanging them.
> 3. **Claiming FL alone is sufficient:** Vanilla FedAvg is **not** provably private. Examiners expect mention that it must be combined with DP or secure aggregation. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Writing \vert x \vert in tables:** Markdown breaks. Use `\Vert x \Vert` instead. Examiners won't penalize this on paper, but for digital submissions it's critical.
> 5. **Skipping real-world deployment examples:** Examiners reward citing **Google Gboard, US Census, Apple, Meta** — these are high-yield, low-effort marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] **ε-Differential Privacy** = multiplicative bound $e^{\varepsilon}$ on output distributions of neighboring datasets.
- [ ] **Global Sensitivity** = maximum L1 change in query output between neighboring datasets.
- [ ] **Federated Learning** = distributed training where raw data never leaves clients.
- [ ] **Homomorphic Encryption** = computation on encrypted data without decryption.
- [ ] **DP-SGD** = SGD with per-sample gradient clipping + Gaussian noise.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (Memorize Verbatim)
- [ ] **Laplace Noise Scale:** $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$
- [ ] **Gaussian Noise Scale:** $\sigma \ge \Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$
- [ ] **FedAvg:** $w_{t+1} = \sum_k (n_k / n) \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}$
- [ ] **Sequential Composition:** $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$
- [ ] **Gradient Clipping:** $\tilde{g}_i = g_i / \max(1, \Vert g_i \Vert_2 / C)$

### ✅ Key Trade-offs
- [ ] **Privacy ↔ Utility:** Smaller ε → more noise → less accuracy.
- [ ] **Privacy ↔ Computation:** HE and SMPC are strongest but slowest.
- [ ] **FL ↔ Privacy Guarantees:** FL reduces exposure but provides no formal DP; must be combined with DP-SGD or secure aggregation.

### ✅ Real-World Deployments (Examiner-Friendly Citations)
- [ ] **Google Gboard** — Federated Learning for next-word prediction.
- [ ] **US Census 2020** — Differential Privacy ( Disclosure Avoidance System).
- [ ] **Apple** — Differential Privacy in emoji analytics, Safari URL suggestions.
- [ ] **Meta** — DP-SGD for ad conversion modeling.
- [ ] **OpenMined/PySyft** — Open-source HE + FL frameworks.

### ✅ KTU-Specific High-Yield Comparisons
- [ ] DP vs FL vs HE vs SMPC (strength, cost, trust model).
- [ ] Membership inference attack — defended by DP only.
- [ ] Centralized ML vs FL — data locality, privacy, communication.

### ✅ Quick Mnemonics
- [ ] **"PFEH"** = **P**illar order — **P**rivacy, **F**ederated, **E**ncryption, **H**... wait, the four pillars are **DP, FL, HE, SMPC**.
- [ ] **"Smaller ε, Stronger Privacy, Louder Noise"** — three S's.
- [ ] **"FedAvg = Weighted Average of Local Models"** — remember the $n_k / n$ weighting.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Privacy-Preserving Learning Notes**

topics.title: Differential Privacy: Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST752 - RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Privacy: Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Privacy (DP)** is a rigorous, mathematical framework that provides quantifiable, provable guarantees of individual privacy when releasing statistical information or training machine learning models on sensitive datasets. It ensures that the *presence or absence of any single individual's data* in a dataset does not significantly affect the output of an analysis.

Mathematically, a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy** if for all neighbouring datasets $D$ and $D'$ that differ in exactly one record, and for all possible output sets $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

The relaxation, called **$(\epsilon, \delta)$-Differential Privacy**, introduces a small failure probability $\delta$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta$$

where:
- $\epsilon$ (epsilon) = **privacy budget** (smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy)
- $\delta$ = probability of catastrophic privacy breach (typically $\delta \ll 1/n$)
- $D$ and $D'$ = **adjacent/neighbouring datasets** (differ by one record)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Blurred Group Photo":**
> Imagine a class photo where every student's face is blurred. You can still count heads, determine the average height, or notice if "most students are wearing red." However, you **cannot identify** any specific individual, even if you already know 99 of the 100 students. Differential Privacy works the same way: the statistical answer remains useful, but any single person's data is **mathematically hidden** within the noise.

**Another Intuition — The Hospital Survey:**
A hospital wants to share disease statistics. If a patient's record is *removed*, the published statistic (e.g., "12% have diabetes") should barely change. If an attacker cannot detect this removal by examining the output, then the patient's privacy is preserved.

---

### 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Privacy Budget ($\epsilon$)** | Quantifies cumulative privacy loss; smaller = stronger privacy |
| **Sensitivity ($\Delta f$)** | Maximum change in query output from one record change |
| **Mechanism ($\mathcal{M}$)** | The randomized algorithm that injects calibrated noise |
| **Neighbouring Datasets** | Datasets differing in at most one record |
| **Global Sensitivity** | Worst-case change in function output over all possible pairs |
| **Local DP** | Noise added on the user side before reaching a central server |
| **Central DP** | Trusted curator adds noise; raw data is collected first |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (Highlighted):**
> - Typical $\epsilon$ range: **0.1 to 10** (with $\epsilon \le 1$ considered strong privacy)
> - Typical $\delta$ range: **$\delta \le 10^{-5}$ or $\delta \le 1/n^2$** where $n$ = dataset size

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — "How DP Works"

The workflow of differential privacy can be decomposed into **five sequential stages**:

1. **Define the Query Function $f$** — Specify what statistic is being computed (sum, mean, count, histogram, ML gradient, etc.).
2. **Compute Global Sensitivity $\Delta f$** — Determine the maximum change in $f$ when one record is added or removed.
3. **Choose a Noise Mechanism** — Select Laplace (for pure $\epsilon$-DP) or Gaussian (for $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP).
4. **Calibrate Noise Magnitude** — Scale the noise to the ratio $\Delta f / \epsilon$.
5. **Track Privacy Budget via Composition** — Sum or compose $\epsilon$ values across multiple queries to prevent cumulative leakage.

---

### 2.2 Sensitivity — The Heart of Differential Privacy

**Global Sensitivity** of a function $f : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ is defined as:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D' : d(D,D')=1} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert$$

- $\Delta f_1$ = **$L_1$-sensitivity** (used with Laplace mechanism)
- $\Delta f_2$ = **$L_2$-sensitivity** (used with Gaussian mechanism)

**Common Sensitivity Examples:**

| Query Type | $L_1$-Sensitivity | $L_2$-Sensitivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Count (records in [0,1]) | **1** | **1** |
| Sum (values in [0,1]) | **1** | **1** |
| Mean (values in [0,1], $n$ records) | $1/n$ | $1/n$ |
| Histogram with $k$ bins | **2** | $\sqrt{2}$ |

---

### 2.3 The Two Canonical Noise Mechanisms

#### A. Laplace Mechanism (Pure $\epsilon$-DP)

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \text{where } \eta \sim \text{Lap}\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

- Noise drawn from a **zero-mean Laplace distribution** with scale parameter $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$
- Achieves **pure $\epsilon$-DP** (no $\delta$)
- Best for **count, sum, and histogram** queries

#### B. Gaussian Mechanism (Approximate $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP)

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \text{where } \eta \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^2 I\right), \quad \sigma \ge \frac{\Delta f_2 \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$$

- Best for **floating-point queries, ML training, and deep learning** (e.g., DP-SGD)
- Requires careful tuning of $\sigma$ relative to $\epsilon$ and $\delta$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU Exams**

| Concept | Formula | Key Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pure $\epsilon$-DP | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | No $\delta$ term |
| $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta$ | Allows small failure |
| Global Sensitivity | $\Delta f = \max \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert$ | Worst-case change |
| Laplace Noise Scale | $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$ | Pure $\epsilon$-DP |
| Gaussian Noise Std. Dev. | $\sigma = \Delta f_2 \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$ | Approximate DP |
| Basic Composition | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i$ | $k$ queries |
| Advanced Composition | $\epsilon' = \sqrt{2k \ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \epsilon + k\epsilon(e^{\epsilon}-1)$ | Tighter bound |
| Privacy Loss Random Variable | $\mathcal{L} = \ln\left(\frac{\Pr[M(D)=o]}{\Pr[M(D')=o]}\right)$ | Bounded by $\epsilon$ |
| Post-Processing Invariance | $\mathcal{M}' = g \circ \mathcal{M}$ preserves DP | Cannot reduce privacy loss |
| Parallel Composition | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \epsilon_i$ | Disjoint dataset partitions |

> **Note on symbols used in the table:** All absolute-value / norm notations have been written using $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ (norm) form. When rendering absolute values such as $\vert x \vert$, the LaTeX shortcodes $\vert$ or $\mid$ are recommended to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Apple** uses **local DP** to collect emoji, vocabulary, and health data from iPhones.
- **Google** uses DP in **RAPPOR** (Chrome browser telemetry) and **DP-SGD** for ML model training.
- **US Census Bureau** adopted DP for the **2020 Census** to protect individual demographic records.
- **Meta** employs DP in ad measurement systems to prevent user-level leakage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Laplace Noise Achieves $\epsilon$-DP

**Goal:** Prove that adding $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \epsilon)$ noise to $f(D)$ ensures $\epsilon$-DP.

**Step 1 — PDF of Laplace Distribution**

For a zero-mean Laplace random variable $\eta$ with scale $b$:

$$p(\eta) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert \eta \vert}{b}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Probability Density Ratio for Adjacent Datasets**

Let $D$ and $D'$ differ in one record. The output of the mechanism is $o = f(D) + \eta$ and $o = f(D') + \eta$ for the *same* $\eta$ (since $\eta$ is sampled independently of $D$).

The ratio of output densities is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(\mathcal{M}(D)=o)}{p(\mathcal{M}(D')=o)} &= \frac{p(o - f(D))}{p(o - f(D'))} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert o - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert o - f(D') \vert}{b}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left(\frac{\vert o - f(D') \vert - \vert o - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Reverse Triangle Inequality**

By the reverse triangle inequality:

$$\vert o - f(D') \vert - \vert o - f(D) \vert \le \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \le \Delta f$$

**Step 4 — Bound the Ratio**

Substituting the upper bound:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(\mathcal{M}(D)=o)}{p(\mathcal{M}(D')=o)} &\le \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta f / \epsilon}\right) \\[4pt]
&= e^{\epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ratio is bounded by $e^{\epsilon}$, satisfying the definition of $\epsilon$-DP. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A hospital dataset contains 10,000 patients. Each patient has a binary attribute (has disease = 1, no disease = 0). The hospital wants to publish the **count of diseased patients** using $\epsilon$-DP with $\epsilon = 0.5$.

**Sub-question (i): Compute the Sensitivity**

The query $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ (count). Changing one record changes the sum by at most 1.

$$\Delta f_1 = 1, \quad \Delta f_2 = 1$$

**Sub-question (ii): Determine the Required Noise Scale**

Using the Laplace mechanism:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-question (iii): Calibrate the Noise Distribution**

The output released is:

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}(0, 2)$$

For a true count of, say, 2,345 diseased patients, the released answer might be anywhere in the range $[2345 - 10, 2345 + 10]$ with overwhelming probability.

**Sub-question (iv): Apply Composition for Multiple Queries**

Suppose the hospital releases **three** independent counts (disease, smoker, vaccinated), each with $\epsilon = 0.5$. By basic composition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \epsilon_i = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

By advanced composition (with $\delta' = 10^{-5}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon' &= \sqrt{2 \cdot 3 \cdot \ln(1/10^{-5})} \cdot 0.5 + 3 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (e^{0.5} - 1) \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{6 \cdot 11.513} \cdot 0.5 + 1.5 \cdot 0.6487 \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{69.078} \cdot 0.5 + 0.973 \\[4pt]
&= 8.311 \cdot 0.5 + 0.973 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.156 + 0.973 = 5.129
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** For small $\epsilon$, the basic composition $\sum \epsilon_i$ is tighter; the advanced bound becomes useful only when $k$ is large and $\epsilon$ is small per query.

---

### 3.3 Privacy Budget Composition — Sequential vs Parallel

| Composition Type | Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sequential** | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \epsilon_i$ | Same dataset, multiple queries |
| **Parallel** | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \epsilon_i$ | Disjoint dataset partitions |
| **Advanced** | $\epsilon' \approx \sqrt{2k\ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \epsilon + k\epsilon(e^{\epsilon}-1)$ | Tight asymptotic bound |
| **Rényi DP** | Uses Rényi divergence of order $\alpha$ | State-of-the-art accounting (e.g., Opacus) |

---

### 3.4 Properties of Differential Privacy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Foundational Properties (Examinable):**
> 1. **Sequential Composition:** Privacy loss *adds up* across multiple analyses.
> 2. **Parallel Composition:** Privacy loss is the *maximum* over disjoint data partitions.
> 3. **Post-Processing Immunity:** Any post-hoc computation cannot worsen privacy.
> 4. **Group Privacy:** $k$ changed records degrade privacy to $k\epsilon$-DP.
> 5. **Robustness to Auxiliary Information:** Holds even if the attacker knows everything except the target record.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Workflow of a Differential Privacy System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the full operational pipeline — from raw data ingestion to the noise-injected, privacy-preserving release.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensitive Dataset D] --> B[Curator / Trusted Server]
    B --> C[Query Function f - e.g. Sum, Mean, Count]
    C --> D[Sensitivity Computation - Delta f]
    D --> E[Privacy Budget Selection - epsilon]
    E --> F{Mechanism Choice}
    F -->|Pure epsilon DP| G[Laplace Mechanism - Scale Delta f over epsilon]
    F -->|Approximate epsilon delta DP| H[Gaussian Mechanism - Sigma tuned by epsilon delta]
    G --> I[Noisy Output M of D]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Privacy Budget Tracker - Composition]
    J --> K[Sanitized Public Release]
    K --> L[Downstream Analyst or Model Trainer]
    L --> M[Useful Statistics or Trained Model]
    J -->|Budget Exceeded| N[STOP - Reject Further Queries]
    J -->|Budget Remaining| O[Allow More Queries]
```

> **Reading the Flow:** The curator computes sensitivity, calibrates noise, releases the perturbed result, and tracks the *running* privacy budget. When the cumulative $\epsilon$ exceeds the allowed threshold, further queries are blocked to preserve the privacy guarantee.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Centralized vs Local Differential Privacy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Centralized["Centralized DP Model"]
        U1[User 1 - Raw Data] --> T1[Trusted Server]
        U2[User 2 - Raw Data] --> T1
        U3[User 3 - Raw Data] --> T1
        T1 --> N1[Add Calibrated Noise Here]
        N1 --> R1[Publish Noisy Aggregate]
    end
    subgraph Local["Local DP Model"]
        V1[User 1 - Raw Data] --> W1[Add Noise Locally on Device]
        V2[User 2 - Raw Data] --> W2[Add Noise Locally on Device]
        V3[User 3 - Raw Data] --> W3[Add Noise Locally on Device]
        W1 --> X[Untrusted Server Collects Noisy Reports]
        W2 --> X
        W3 --> X
        X --> Y[Publish Aggregated Noisy Result]
    end
```

> **Trade-off Summary:**
> - **Centralized DP:** Higher accuracy, but requires a fully trusted curator.
> - **Local DP:** No trust assumption on server, but requires larger noise (lower utility). Used by Apple and Google RAPPOR.

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Laplace vs Gaussian Noise Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LaplaceSide["Laplace Mechanism - Pure epsilon DP"]
        L1[Sharp Peak at 0] --> L2[Heavy Tails]
        L2 --> L3[Probability Mass spread by scale b = Delta f over epsilon]
    end
    subgraph GaussianSide["Gaussian Mechanism - epsilon delta DP"]
        G1[Smooth Bell Curve at 0] --> G2[Lighter Tails]
        G2 --> G3[Std Dev sigma = Delta f2 times sqrt 2 ln 1.25 over delta over epsilon]
    end
    L3 --> Comp[Composition with prior queries]
    G3 --> Comp
    Comp --> Out[Final Sanitized Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to choose between mechanisms in a KTU exam, default answer — *use Laplace for pure $\epsilon$-DP on low-dimensional queries (count/sum); use Gaussian only when $\delta > 0$ is acceptable, e.g., for ML training with DP-SGD.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget $\epsilon$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is $\epsilon$-differentially private if for all neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ and all output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

The **privacy budget $\epsilon$** quantifies the *maximum allowable information leakage* per individual. A **smaller $\epsilon$** enforces **stronger privacy** (output barely changes whether or not a record is present), while a larger $\epsilon$ allows more utility at the cost of weaker privacy. Common practice: $\epsilon \le 1$ for strong privacy. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for neighbour definition, 1 for role of $\epsilon$.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is global sensitivity? Compute the $L_1$-sensitivity of the *count* query and the *mean* query (over $n$ records bounded in $[0,1]$).

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Global sensitivity** is the maximum change in a query's output over all pairs of neighbouring datasets:
$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert$$

- **Count query:** Removing/adding one record changes the count by exactly 1. Hence $\Delta f_{\text{count}} = 1$.
- **Mean query:** The mean shifts by at most $1/n$ when one record is perturbed. Hence $\Delta f_{\text{mean}} = 1/n$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for count, 1 for mean.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the **Laplace Mechanism** in detail. Derive the noise scale $b$ required to achieve $\epsilon$-DP. (7 marks)

**(b)** A researcher runs a survey on 5,000 patients to count how many have diabetes. Each patient contributes a 0/1 value. Use the Laplace mechanism with $\epsilon = 1.0$. (i) Compute the sensitivity; (ii) state the noise distribution; (iii) compute the total $\epsilon$ consumed if 4 such queries are run sequentially. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Laplace Mechanism Derivation (7 marks):**

The Laplace mechanism releases:

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)$$

with PDF: $p(\eta) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \eta \vert}{b}\right)$.

For adjacent datasets $D, D'$, the density ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(\mathcal{M}(D) = o)}{p(\mathcal{M}(D') = o)} &= \exp\!\left(\frac{\vert o - f(D') \vert - \vert o - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

By the reverse triangle inequality, the numerator is at most $\vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \le \Delta f$. To satisfy $\epsilon$-DP, the ratio must be $\le e^{\epsilon}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) &\le e^{\epsilon} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta f}{b} &\le \epsilon \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow b &\ge \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the minimum noise scale is $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$,** and the mechanism achieves **pure $\epsilon$-DP** with no $\delta$ term. The noise must scale *linearly* with sensitivity and *inversely* with the privacy budget. (7 marks — full derivation)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Sensitivity:**
For a count query, each record contributes at most 1. Hence $\Delta f = 1$. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Noise Distribution:**
With $\epsilon = 1.0$ and $\Delta f = 1$:

$$\eta \sim \text{Lap}(0, b), \quad b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1$$

The released count is $f(D) + \text{Lap}(0, 1)$. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Sequential Composition for 4 Queries:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} \epsilon_i = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

After 4 queries, the cumulative privacy budget is $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 4.0$. The curator should stop further queries to prevent exceeding the acceptable privacy loss. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Compare **Local** and **Central** Differential Privacy with neat diagrams. Discuss the privacy-utility trade-off in each. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Gaussian Mechanism** for $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP. Given a query with $L_2$-sensitivity $\Delta f_2 = 2$, $\epsilon = 1$, $\delta = 10^{-5}$, compute the standard deviation $\sigma$ of the noise. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Local vs Central Differential Privacy (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralized DP | Local DP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trust Model** | Trusted curator collects raw data | Untrusted server; noise added on user device |
| **Noise Magnitude** | Smaller (more utility) | Larger (less utility) |
| **Accuracy** | High | Lower |
| **Examples** | US 2020 Census, DP-SGD | Apple emoji collection, Google RAPPOR |
| **Privacy Strength** | Stronger at same $\epsilon$ | Weaker at same $\epsilon$ |

- In **Centralized DP**, the curator sees raw data and is responsible for adding noise. This is accurate but requires absolute trust in the curator.
- In **Local DP**, each user perturbs their own data before transmission. This provides stronger trust guarantees but requires a larger noise budget to maintain equivalent accuracy.
- **Trade-off:** Centralized = better utility, weaker trust assumption; Local = weaker utility, stronger trust assumption. *(7 marks — 2 for diagram description, 2 for each model explanation, 1 for trade-off conclusion)*

---

**(b) Gaussian Mechanism Derivation (7 marks):**

The Gaussian mechanism releases:

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$$

For $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP, the standard deviation must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\ge \frac{\Delta f_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting $\Delta f_2 = 2$, $\epsilon = 1$, $\delta = 10^{-5}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\ge \frac{2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 \times 10^{5})}}{1} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(125000)} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 11.736} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \sqrt{23.472} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 4.844 \\[4pt]
&\approx 9.689
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma \approx 9.69$. The released result will be $f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, 9.69^2)$. *(7 marks — 2 for mechanism description, 3 for formula substitution, 2 for numerical answer)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to define "neighbouring datasets"** — examiners will deduct 1 mark if you don't explicitly state that $D$ and $D'$ differ in exactly one record.
> 2. **Confusing $L_1$ and $L_2$ sensitivity** — Laplace uses $L_1$; Gaussian uses $L_2$. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\delta$ term** — when discussing the Gaussian mechanism, students often quote only the $\epsilon$ bound, missing the $\delta$ relaxation.
> 4. **Misapplying composition** — Parallel composition requires *disjoint* data partitions; using it for sequential queries yields an incorrect (overly optimistic) bound.
> 5. **Missing the post-processing property** — any function applied to a DP output remains DP. Students often overlook this elegant property.
> 6. **Units in the noise scale** — the scale parameter $b$ in Laplace distribution is *not* a standard deviation; it equals $\Delta f / \epsilon$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Differential Privacy: Working**

- **Core Definition:** $\epsilon$-DP bounds the *output ratio* between neighbouring datasets by $e^{\epsilon}$.
- **Privacy Budget $\epsilon$:** Smaller is stronger; typical range 0.1–10. Strong privacy = $\epsilon \le 1$.
- **Failure Probability $\delta$:** Only present in $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP; must satisfy $\delta \ll 1/n$.
- **Sensitivity $\Delta f$:** Worst-case change in query output from one record difference.
  - Count / Sum: $\Delta f = 1$
  - Mean: $\Delta f = 1/n$
  - Histogram: $\Delta f = 2$ ($L_1$)
- **Laplace Mechanism:** Pure $\epsilon$-DP; noise scale $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$.
- **Gaussian Mechanism:** $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP; $\sigma = \Delta f_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$.
- **Sequential Composition:** $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \epsilon_i$ — used for multiple queries on the *same* dataset.
- **Parallel Composition:** $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \epsilon_i$ — used for *disjoint* data partitions.
- **Post-Processing:** DP is immune to post-hoc transformations; you cannot *reduce* privacy loss by processing.
- **Group Privacy:** Changing $k$ records weakens the guarantee to $k\epsilon$-DP.
- **Centralized vs Local DP:** Central = trusted curator + high utility; Local = no trust + lower utility.
- **Real-World Deployments:** Apple (local DP), Google RAPPOR, US Census 2020, DP-SGD (PyTorch Opacus, TF Privacy).
- **Advanced Tools:** Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP) for tight privacy accounting in DP-SGD.
- **Key Trade-off:** Noise ↑ for stronger privacy ⇒ Utility ↓. Choose $\epsilon$ based on the *acceptable privacy-utility balance* for the use case.
- **Three Lines to Memorize for Proofs:**
  1. $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$
  2. $b = \Delta f / \epsilon$
  3. $\sigma = \Delta f_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# The Laplace Mechanism — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (The Laplace Mechanism)**
> Given a numeric query function $f: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ and a privacy budget $\varepsilon > 0$, the **Laplace Mechanism** $M_L$ outputs a privacy-preserving answer by adding independent random noise drawn from a **zero-mean Laplace distribution** with scale parameter $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ to every component of the true query output. Formally:
>
> $$M_L(D) = f(D) + \big(Lap(b),\; Lap(b),\; \dots,\; Lap(b)\big)$$
>
> where $\Delta f$ is the **$L_1$-sensitivity** of $f$ and $Lap(b)$ denotes an i.i.d. Laplace random variable with location $\mu = 0$ and scale $b$. The mechanism satisfies **$\varepsilon$-differential privacy** (pure $\varepsilon$-DP).

### 1.1.1 Sensitivity: The "Privacy Thermometer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ($L_1$-Sensitivity)**
> The $L_1$-sensitivity of a function $f: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ over neighbouring databases $D \sim D'$ (differing in at most one record) is:
>
> $$\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1 = \max_{D \sim D'} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert f(D)_i - f(D')_i \vert$$

Sensitivity measures the **maximum influence a single individual can have on the query output**. The smaller the sensitivity, the less noise is required, and therefore the higher the utility of the mechanism.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a classroom of 100 students where the teacher wants to find the **average marks** of the class, but the students are privacy-conscious and refuse to share their actual marks. If one student is removed or added, the average will shift — but only slightly (at most by 1 mark, because one student can change the mean by at most 1/100 if marks are 0–100).

Now, instead of reporting the true average, the teacher reports the true average **plus a small random "wiggle"** — drawn from the Laplace distribution. The wiggle is calibrated so that:

- An **eavesdropper** listening to the answer **cannot tell whether any one student was in the class or not** — the answer looks statistically almost the same.
- Yet the answer remains **close enough** to the true average to be useful (e.g., the school can still make a passing decision).

The "wiggle size" (scale $b$) is governed by two opposing forces:
1. **Privacy budget** $\varepsilon$ — smaller $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stricter privacy ⇒ larger wiggle.
2. **Sensitivity** $\Delta f$ — higher sensitivity ⇒ more noise required.

> [!TIP]
> **Mental Model:** Think of Laplace noise as a "fuzzy blanket" draped over the true answer. The thickness of the blanket is proportional to the sensitivity and inversely proportional to the privacy budget.

## 1.3 Laplace Distribution — Quick Facts

The Laplace (or double-exponential) distribution with location $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ and scale $b > 0$ has:

| Property | Expression |
|---|---|
| Probability Density Function (PDF) | $f(x \mid \mu, b) = \dfrac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{b}\right)$ |
| Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | $F(x) = \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{sgn}(x-\mu)\left(1 - e^{-\vert x-\mu \vert / b}\right)$ |
| Mean | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \mu$ |
| Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = 2b^{2}$ |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma = b\sqrt{2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Laplace and not Gaussian?** The Laplace distribution is **unimodal, symmetric, and has exponentially decaying tails**. The exponential tails make the **privacy loss ratio $\Pr[M(D)=x]/\Pr[M(D')=x]$** easy to bound in closed form using the triangle inequality — a property Gaussian noise cannot provide for pure $\varepsilon$-DP (Gaussian gives only $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP).

## 1.4 Standard Symbols Used in KTU Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|
| $D, D'$ | Adjacent databases (differ in 1 record) | Set of records |
| $f$ | Deterministic query function | $f : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ |
| $\Delta f$ | $L_1$-sensitivity of $f$ | Real scalar (or vector) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Privacy budget (privacy loss parameter) | Real, $\varepsilon > 0$ |
| $\delta$ | Failure probability (for relaxed DP) | Real, $0 \le \delta < 1$ |
| $b$ | Laplace scale parameter | $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ |
| $M_L(D)$ | Private randomised response | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^k$ |
| $\alpha$ | Utility error tolerance | Real, $\alpha > 0$ |
| $\beta$ | Utility failure probability | Real, $0 < \beta < 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Construction — The Laplace Mechanism

The mechanism operates in **four clean steps**:

1. **Receive query function $f$ and privacy budget $\varepsilon$.**
   The curator accepts a request — for example, "compute the histogram of ages" or "compute the sum of salaries".

2. **Compute the $L_1$-sensitivity $\Delta f$ of $f$.**
   Sensitivity is a **property of the function**, *not* the data. Examples:
   - **Counting query** (e.g., "How many users have a disease?"): $\Delta f = 1$ because adding/removing one record changes the count by at most 1.
   - **Sum query** (e.g., "What is the total income?") with bounded records $r_i \in [a, b]$: $\Delta f = b - a$.
   - **Mean query**: $\Delta f = (b - a) / n$.

3. **Sample independent Laplace noise** $Y_i \sim Lap(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ for $i = 1, \dots, k$.

4. **Release** $M_L(D) = f(D) + (Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sampling in Step 3 is **independent across coordinates** and **independent of the database** (given $\varepsilon$ and the function $f$). This independence is what enables the **composition theorems** of differential privacy to combine multiple Laplace mechanisms with cumulative privacy cost.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this table verbatim — these are the only equations you need for any Laplace Mechanism numerical or proof-based question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Laplace PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)$ | $b > 0$ |
| 2 | $L_1$-Sensitivity | $\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$ | Over neighbours |
| 3 | $L_2$-Sensitivity | $\Delta_2 f = \max_{D \sim D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_2$ | Used by Gaussian Mech. |
| 4 | Laplace scale (for $\varepsilon$-DP) | $b = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$ | Per-coordinate |
| 5 | Mean of $M_L(D)$ | $\mathbb{E}[M_L(D)] = f(D)$ | Unbiased |
| 6 | Variance of $M_L(D)$ | $\text{Var}[M_L(D)] = 2 \left(\dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)^{2}$ | Per-coordinate |
| 7 | $L_1$-error bound (probabilistic) | $\Pr\!\left[\Vert M_L(D) - f(D) \Vert_1 \ge \alpha\right] = 2\, e^{-\alpha \varepsilon / \Delta f}$ | Chernoff on $k$-dim |
| 8 | $(\alpha, \beta)$-Utility | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2k}{\beta}\right)$ | Solve for $\alpha$ given $\beta$ |
| 9 | Total noise std. dev. | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\Delta f \sqrt{2k}}{\varepsilon}$ | Worst-case aggregate |
| 10 | Sequential composition (basic) | Total $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \varepsilon_j$ | Laplace-specific |
| 11 | Parallel composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max_{j} \varepsilon_j$ | Disjoint partitions |
| 12 | Privacy loss random variable | $L_{M}(D, D') = \ln \dfrac{\Pr[M(D)=\cdot]}{\Pr[M(D')=\cdot]}$ | Bounded by $\varepsilon$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-Saver Tip:** When asked "Show that the Laplace Mechanism satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP", you must explicitly mention the **triangle inequality** and the fact that noise is **independent of the data**. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if either is missing.

## 2.3 Why the Laplace Mechanism Satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP — Conceptual Flow

The proof rests on the **exponential-family structure** of Laplace noise. Concretely, for any $x \in \mathbb{R}^k$:

$$\Pr[M_L(D) = x] \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1}{b}\right)$$

So the **likelihood ratio** between neighbouring databases is:

$$\frac{\Pr[M_L(D) = x]}{\Pr[M_L(D') = x]} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\Vert x - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1}{b}\right)$$

The numerator inside the exponent is bounded (using the **reverse triangle inequality**) by:

$$\Vert x - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1 \;\le\; \Vert f(D') - f(D) \Vert_1 \;\le\; \Delta f$$

Hence the ratio is at most $\exp(\Delta f / b)$. Setting $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ makes the ratio at most $\exp(\varepsilon)$ — exactly the condition for $\varepsilon$-DP. The symmetric bound (using $D' \to D$) is automatic by symmetry of the ratio.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Laplace Mechanism underpins several production-grade and research-grade systems:

| Application Domain | Use Case | Reference Function $f$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Census & Government Statistics** | Releasing commuting-time histograms | Histogram query |
| **Healthcare ML** | Private logistic regression gradients | Gradient sum |
| **Recommender Systems** | Private user-preference aggregates | Counting per category |
| **Federated Analytics** | Local-to-central aggregation of counts | Sum with bounded range |
| **Smart Metering** | Household energy consumption releases | Histogram (bucket counts) |
| **Social Network Analysis** | Degree distribution of friendship graph | Degree histogram |

In **R** or **Python** for DP-toolkit use, the Laplace Mechanism is the **default** mechanism whenever the query is numerical, bounded-range, and requires **pure** $\varepsilon$-DP (as opposed to the Gaussian Mechanism, which is used for $\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP).

## 2.5 Limitations of the Laplace Mechanism

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners often award 1–2 bonus marks for noting the **limitations**. The Laplace Mechanism:
> 1. **Fails for unbounded-sensitivity functions** (e.g., "median" or "trimmed mean") because $\Delta f$ is infinite. Workarounds use **smooth sensitivity** or the **exponential mechanism**.
> 2. **Poor utility in high dimensions** — noise scales as $O(k)$, so for $k \gg 1$, the signal-to-noise ratio degrades. The **Tree Aggregation Protocol** or **Matrix Mechanism** address this.
> 3. **Provides only pure $\varepsilon$-DP** — for compositional settings (e.g., deep learning with millions of queries), it is too strict; the Gaussian Mechanism offers better composition under $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Laplace Mechanism Satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP

**Theorem.** *The Laplace Mechanism $M_L(D) = f(D) + (Y_1, \dots, Y_k)$ with $Y_i \sim Lap(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ i.i.d. satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy.*

**Proof.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **central derivation** for any "show that" question on the Laplace Mechanism in KTU exams. Master it line by line.

**Step 1 — Set up the likelihood ratio.**

For any measurable subset $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[M_L(D) \in S] &= \int_{S} \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert}{b}\right) \, dx_1 \dots dx_k \\
\Pr[M_L(D') \in S] &= \int_{S} \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x_i - f(D')_i \vert}{b}\right) \, dx_1 \dots dx_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce to a pointwise ratio.**

The constant $\prod (1/2b)$ cancels, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} &= \frac{\int_S \prod_{i=1}^{k} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert}{b}\right) dx}{\int_S \prod_{i=1}^{k} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x_i - f(D')_i \vert}{b}\right) dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a ratio of integrals. The classical trick (from Dwork et al., 2006) is to **bound it pointwise** by the ratio of integrands, using the fact that the denominator is the integral of a positive function, and the exponential is *monotonically decreasing* in the absolute deviation.

**Step 3 — Apply the pointwise bound.**

For any $x \in \mathbb{R}^k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i=1}^{k} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert}{b}\right) &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{b}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert\right) \\
&= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for $D'$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\prod_{i=1}^{k} e^{-\vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert / b}}{\prod_{i=1}^{k} e^{-\vert x_i - f(D')_i \vert / b}} &= \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\Big[\Vert x - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1\Big]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the reverse triangle inequality.**

The reverse triangle inequality in $L_1$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert x - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert x - f(D) \Vert_1 &\le \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1 \\
&\le \Delta f \quad \text{(by definition of sensitivity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\prod_i e^{-\vert x_i - f(D)_i \vert / b}}{\prod_i e^{-\vert x_i - f(D')_i \vert / b}} &\le \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose the scale parameter.**

Set $b = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) &= \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta f / \varepsilon}\right) = \exp(\varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

Since the integrand of the numerator is **pointwise bounded** by $e^{\varepsilon}$ times the integrand of the denominator, the integrals satisfy the same bound:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[M_L(D) \in S] &\le e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[M_L(D') \in S]
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry (swap $D$ and $D'$), the reverse inequality also holds with the same bound, so:

$$
\boxed{\; e^{-\varepsilon} \le \frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} \le e^{\varepsilon} \quad \forall\, S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{k}, \; \forall\, D \sim D'\;}
$$

Hence $M_L$ is $\varepsilon$-differentially private. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: $(\alpha, \beta)$-Utility Bound

**Theorem.** *The Laplace Mechanism with scale $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ is $(\alpha, \beta)$-useful, where:*

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2k}{\beta}\right)$$

**Proof.**

**Step 1 — Bound the total error.**

The total $L_1$-error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert M_L(D) - f(D) \Vert_1 &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert Y_i \vert, \quad Y_i \sim Lap(b) \text{ i.i.d.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use a tail bound on each $|Y_i|$.**

For a single $Y_i \sim Lap(b)$ with $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[\vert Y_i \vert \ge t] &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{b}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{t\,\varepsilon}{\Delta f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a union bound across $k$ coordinates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left[\exists\, i \in [k] : \vert Y_i \vert \ge t\right] &\le \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Pr[\vert Y_i \vert \ge t] = k \exp\!\left(-\frac{t\,\varepsilon}{\Delta f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Set the total error budget.**

Let $\Vert M_L(D) - f(D) \Vert_1 \ge kt$. The total $L_1$-error is at most $k t$ if **every** coordinate satisfies $|Y_i| \le t$. By the complementary event:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left[\Vert M_L(D) - f(D) \Vert_1 \ge kt\right] &\le k\exp\!\left(-\frac{t\,\varepsilon}{\Delta f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Set $\alpha = k t$ and solve for $t$.**

Let $\alpha = kt \Rightarrow t = \alpha / k$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left[\Vert M_L(D) - f(D) \Vert_1 \ge \alpha\right] &\le k\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{k\,\Delta f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Set the RHS equal to $\beta$ and solve for $\alpha$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{k\,\Delta f}\right) &\le \beta \\
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{k\,\Delta f}\right) &\le \frac{\beta}{k} \\
-\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{k\,\Delta f} &\le \ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{k}\right) \\
\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{k\,\Delta f} &\ge -\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{k}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{k}{\beta}\right) \\
\alpha &\ge \frac{k\,\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{k}{\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Refine the constant using a sharper union bound.**

A sharper derivation (using the tail bound $\Pr[|Y_i|\ge t]=e^{-t/b}$ on each of the $2k$ half-lines) yields:

$$
\boxed{\; \alpha \;=\; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2k}{\beta}\right) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \beta \;=\; 2k\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha\,\varepsilon}{\Delta f}\right) \;}
$$

This is the canonical $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility statement for the Laplace Mechanism. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A hospital releases the daily count of patients with disease $X$ from a database of size $n = 1000$. Each row contributes a binary value in $\{0, 1\}$. Using the Laplace Mechanism with $\varepsilon = 0.5$, find (a) the sensitivity, (b) the noise scale, (c) the standard deviation of the released answer, and (d) the $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility bound for $\beta = 0.05$.

**Step (a) — Sensitivity:**

The function is $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ (count). Each $x_i \in \{0,1\}$, so removing one record changes the count by exactly 1. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Noise scale:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Standard deviation:**

For $k = 1$ dimension:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma = b\sqrt{2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility with $k = 1$, $\beta = 0.05$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 1}{0.05}\right) = \frac{1}{0.5}\,\ln(40) = 2 \times 3.689 \approx 7.378
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With probability at least $1 - 0.05 = 95\%$, the released count will be within $\pm 7.378$ of the true count. For a hospital with $\sim 1000$ patients, this represents an error of $< 0.74\%$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — "When to Use What"

| Query Type | Sensitivity $\Delta f$ | Mechanism to Use | Scale $b$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Count** | 1 | Laplace | $1/\varepsilon$ |
| **Histogram (k bins)** | 2 | Laplace | $2/\varepsilon$ |
| **Sum (bounded $[0, \Delta]$)** | $\Delta$ | Laplace | $\Delta/\varepsilon$ |
| **Mean (bounded $[0, \Delta]$, $n$ records)** | $\Delta / n$ | Laplace | $\Delta/(n\varepsilon)$ |
| **Median** | Unbounded | Exponential Mechanism | — |
| **Mode** | Unbounded | Exponential Mechanism | — |
| **Gradient of deep net** | $\sim \mathcal{O}(1/\text{clip})$ | Gaussian (DP-SGD) | $\sigma = \Delta_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}/\varepsilon$ |
| **Variance** | Bounded, computable | Laplace | Analytical formula |
