# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of the Laplace Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below visualises the **end-to-end data flow** of the Laplace Mechanism. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes per the Mermaid safety protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Client or Analyst] --> A1[Submit Query f and Budget Epsilon]
    A1 --> A2[Trusted Curator Receives Request]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Neighbouring Databases D and Dprime]
    A3 --> A4[Compute L1 Sensitivity DeltaF]
    A4 --> A5{Is DeltaF Finite?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Set Laplace Scale b = DeltaF over Epsilon]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Abort - Use Exponential Mechanism Instead]
    A6 --> A8[Compute True Query Output f of D]
    A8 --> A9[Sample Noise Yi from Lap b for i = 1 to k]
    A9 --> A10[Construct Noisy Answer M of D = f of D plus Y1 to Yk]
    A10 --> A11[Apply Epsilon DP Verification Check]
    A11 --> A12{Privacy Loss at most e to the Epsilon?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Release M of D to Client]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Reject Output - Resample or Tighten Epsilon]
    A13 --> A15[Post Processing Allowed Without Extra Privacy Cost]
    A15 --> A16[Final Privacy Preserving Release]
    A14 --> A8
    A7 --> A17[Switch to Exponential Mechanism]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow enters from the top-left (client request) and traverses a series of decision points (rhombi) and processing steps (rectangles). The two failure paths (unbounded sensitivity and verification rejection) are critical to the integrity of the differential privacy guarantee.

---

## 4.2 Privacy vs Utility Trade-off — Parameter Space

This diagram maps the **two-dimensional trade-off** between privacy ($\varepsilon$) and utility ($\alpha$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_EPS["LOW EPSILON - STRICT PRIVACY"]
        LE1[Wide Laplace Distribution]
        LE2[Large Scale b]
        LE3[High Noise Variance 2b squared]
        LE4[Large Alpha - Poor Utility]
        LE1 --> LE2
        LE2 --> LE3
        LE3 --> LE4
    end
    subgraph HIGH_EPS["HIGH EPSILON - RELAXED PRIVACY"]
        HE1[Tight Laplace Distribution]
        HE2[Small Scale b]
        HE3[Low Noise Variance 2b squared]
        HE4[Small Alpha - High Utility]
        HE1 --> HE2
        HE2 --> HE3
        HE3 --> HE4
    end
    LOW_EPS -.Tune Up.-> HIGH_EPS
    HIGH_EPS -.Tune Down.-> LOW_EPS
```

**Reading the diagram:** Decreasing $\varepsilon$ (moving down) makes the Laplace PDF wider, scaling up the noise, and consequently inflating the error tolerance $\alpha$. The two subgraphs are connected by tuning arrows showing the practitioner's choice.

---

## 4.3 Laplace Mechanism Algorithm — Pseudocode Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Algorithm Start] --> B1[Input: Database D, Function f, Privacy Budget Epsilon]
    B1 --> B2[Step 1: Compute L1 Sensitivity DeltaF of f]
    B2 --> B3[Step 2: Compute Scale Parameter b = DeltaF divided by Epsilon]
    B3 --> B4[Step 3: Compute True Output v = f of D]
    B4 --> B5[Step 4: For i from 1 to k]
    B5 --> B6[Sample Yi from Laplace 0 b]
    B6 --> B7{More Coordinates?}
    B7 -- Yes --> B5
    B7 -- No --> B8[Step 5: Return Noisy Vector v + Y1 to Yk]
    B8 --> B9[Algorithm End - Output is Epsilon DP]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A clean linear algorithm with one internal loop (coordinate-wise noise sampling) and no branching. This reflects the conceptual simplicity that makes the Laplace Mechanism the **workhorse of differential privacy** for numerical queries.

---

## 4.4 Sensitivity vs Noise Magnitude — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Function Type] --> C2{Is Output Bounded?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Compute Analytic DeltaF]
    C3 --> C4[Apply Laplace Mechanism]
    C4 --> C5[Scale b = DeltaF over Epsilon]
    C5 --> C6[Sample and Release]
    C2 -- No --> C7[Is Smooth Sensitivity Available?]
    C7 -- Yes --> C8[Use Propose Test Release Framework]
    C7 -- No --> C9[Use Exponential Mechanism for Non Numeric Queries]
    C6 --> C10[Final Release]
    C8 --> C10
    C9 --> C10
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the $L_1$-sensitivity is finite (most standard queries), the Laplace Mechanism is direct. For unbounded queries, a fallback path is invoked that uses **smooth sensitivity** or the **exponential mechanism** — both of which are part of the broader differentially private algorithmic toolkit covered in later KTU modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State and explain the Laplace Mechanism for $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. Mention the role of sensitivity in determining the noise scale.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Laplace Mechanism** is a randomised algorithm that releases the noisy answer to a numeric query $f$ on a database $D$ by adding Laplace noise:

$$M_L(D) = f(D) + (Y_1, \dots, Y_k), \quad Y_i \overset{iid}{\sim} Lap(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$$

where:
- $\Delta f$ is the **$L_1$-sensitivity** of $f$, measuring the maximum change in $f$'s output when one record is added or removed.
- $\varepsilon > 0$ is the **privacy budget**; smaller $\varepsilon$ implies stronger privacy.

The noise scale is set as $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$, which ensures that the mechanism is **$\varepsilon$-differentially private** — the output distributions of $M_L(D)$ and $M_L(D')$ on neighbouring databases $D \sim D'$ are pointwise within a multiplicative factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$.

> **Mark Allocation:** (1 mark) definition; (1 mark) formula; (1 mark) role of sensitivity.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define $L_1$-sensitivity of a query function. Compute the sensitivity of the following queries on a database where each individual's age is in $[18, 90]$: (i) count, (ii) sum of ages, (iii) mean age.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **$L_1$-sensitivity** of a function $f$ is:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$$

For a database with $n$ records, each age $a_i \in [18, 90]$:

(i) **Count query** $f(D) = n$:
- Adding/removing one record changes the count by exactly 1.
- $\Delta f = 1$.

(ii) **Sum query** $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$:
- Removing one record changes the sum by at most $\max(a_i) - \min(a_i) = 90 - 18 = 72$.
- $\Delta f = 72$.

(iii) **Mean query** $f(D) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$:
- Removing one record changes the mean by at most $\tfrac{72}{n}$.
- $\Delta f = \dfrac{72}{n}$.

> **Mark Allocation:** (1 mark) sensitivity definition; (2 marks) three cases.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $L_1$ and $L_2$ sensitivity.** $L_1$ is for the Laplace Mechanism; $L_2$ is for the Gaussian Mechanism. Mixing them is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in KTU boards.
> 2. **Always state "independent Laplace random variables"** when describing noise — partial credit is lost if you forget the independence.
> 3. **Cite the triangle inequality** in any $\varepsilon$-DP proof; it is the heart of the bound.
> 4. **Use $\Vert \cdot \Vert_1$ notation** consistently; do not mix with absolute value bars inside LaTeX.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Let $D$ be a database containing $n$ Boolean records $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$. The query $f$ returns the count of records equal to 1. Using the Laplace Mechanism with privacy budget $\varepsilon$:*

*(a) Determine the $L_1$-sensitivity of $f$. Hence derive the noise scale $b$ required to make the mechanism $\varepsilon$-differentially private. State clearly the distribution of noise added.*

*(7 marks)*

*(b) Show that the Laplace Mechanism defined in part (a) satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. Compute the variance of the released output and the $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility bound for $k = 1$ dimension.*

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Sensitivity and Noise Scale [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the query.**

The query function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The output is a scalar ($k = 1$).

**Step 2 — Compute the $L_1$-sensitivity.**

Let $D'$ be a database obtained by removing (or adding) one record $x_j$ from $D$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert f(D) - f(D') \vert &= \vert x_j \vert \le 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= \max_{D \sim D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — Sensitivity derivation.

**Step 3 — Set the noise scale.**

To achieve $\varepsilon$-differential privacy, the noise scale must be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** — Scale formula.

**Step 4 — Describe the mechanism.**

The mechanism adds a single Laplace random variable $Y \sim Lap(0, b) = Lap(1/\varepsilon)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_L(D) = f(D) + Y, \quad Y \sim Lap\!\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The probability density of $Y$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_Y(y) = \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\exp(-\varepsilon \vert y \vert), \quad y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — Distribution statement and PDF.

**Step 5 — Verification of $\varepsilon$-DP [shared with Part (b)].**

By the result derived in Part (b), the mechanism with $b = 1/\varepsilon$ is $\varepsilon$-DP.

**(3 marks — awarded in part (b)).**

---

**Part (b) — Proof of $\varepsilon$-DP, Variance, and Utility [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Set up the proof.**

For any neighbouring $D \sim D'$ and any measurable $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[M_L(D) \in S] &= \int_S \frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x - f(D) \vert}{b}\right) dx \\
\Pr[M_L(D') \in S] &= \int_S \frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x - f(D') \vert}{b}\right) dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** — Setup.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio and bound the integrand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} &= \frac{\int_S e^{-\vert x - f(D) \vert / b} \, dx}{\int_S e^{-\vert x - f(D') \vert / b} \, dx} \\
&\le \max_{x \in S} \frac{e^{-\vert x - f(D) \vert / b}}{e^{-\vert x - f(D') \vert / b}} \\
&= \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\Big[\max_{x \in S}\big(\vert x - f(D') \vert - \vert x - f(D) \vert\big)\Big]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — Ratio bound.

**Step 3 — Apply the reverse triangle inequality.**

For any $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert x - f(D') \vert - \vert x - f(D) \vert &\le \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \le \Delta f = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} &\le \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** — Triangle inequality step.

**Step 4 — Choose $b$ and conclude.**

Setting $b = 1/\varepsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\right) = \exp(\varepsilon) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} \le e^{\varepsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry, the reverse inequality holds with $e^{-\varepsilon}$, completing the proof.

**(1.5 marks)** — Conclusion.

**Step 5 — Compute the variance.**

The variance of $Y \sim Lap(0, b)$ is $2b^2$. With $b = 1/\varepsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[M_L(D)] = \text{Var}[Y] = 2b^2 = \frac{2}{\varepsilon^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** — Variance.

**Step 6 — Compute the $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility.**

For $k = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left[\vert M_L(D) - f(D) \vert \ge \alpha\right] &\le 2 \exp(-\alpha / b) = 2 \exp(-\alpha \varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this $\le \beta$ and solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2}{\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** — Utility bound.

**Total: 7 + 7 = 14 marks.**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
*Consider a hospital database where each record represents a patient, and the attribute "treatment cost" lies in the range $[0, 1000]$ (in dollars). The query $f$ returns the **sum of treatment costs** across all patients.*

*(a) Compute the $L_1$-sensitivity of $f$. Design a Laplace Mechanism that releases the sum with privacy budget $\varepsilon = 1$. Write the formal definition of the mechanism and compute the noise scale.*

*(7 marks)*

*(b) For $n = 100$ patients, determine the expected noise variance per query. If the hospital wishes to release $m = 5$ such queries sequentially under **basic composition**, what is the total privacy budget consumed? What is the corresponding $(\alpha, \beta)$-utility bound for the composite release, assuming $\beta = 0.01$?*

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Sensitivity and Mechanism [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Define the function.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i, \quad c_i \in [0, 1000]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute sensitivity.**

Removing one record $c_j$ changes the sum by exactly $c_j \in [0, 1000]$. Hence the maximum change is $1000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** — Sensitivity with justification.

**Step 3 — Mechanism design.**

For $\varepsilon = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — Noise scale.

**Step 4 — Formal mechanism definition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_L(D) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i\right) + Y, \quad Y \sim Lap(0, 1000)
\end{aligned}
$$

The PDF of the noise is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{2000}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert y \vert}{1000}\right), \quad y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** — Formal definition and PDF.

**Step 5 — Privacy verification.**

For neighbouring $D \sim D'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[M_L(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M_L(D') \in S]} &\le \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{1000}{1000}\right) = e^{1} = e^{\varepsilon} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — Privacy check.

---

**Part (b) — Variance, Composition, and Utility [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Per-query variance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[Y] = 2b^2 = 2 \cdot 1000^2 = 2 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the standard deviation is $\sigma = 1000\sqrt{2} \approx \$1414.21$.

**(1.5 marks)** — Variance.

**Step 2 — Sequential composition.**

Under the **basic composition theorem** (a special case of which is the **sequential composition for Laplace mechanisms**), the cumulative privacy parameter for $m$ queries is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \varepsilon_j = 5 \times 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)** — Composition.

**Step 3 — Composite utility bound.**

The total noise added is the sum of $m$ independent Laplace variables, so the total error is $L_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{5} |Y_j|$. By Markov's inequality applied to each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{j=1}^{5} \vert Y_j \vert \ge \alpha\right] &\le 5 \cdot 2 e^{-\alpha \varepsilon / \Delta f} = 10 e^{-\alpha \cdot 1 / 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this $\le \beta = 0.01$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 e^{-\alpha / 1000} &\le 0.01 \\
e^{-\alpha / 1000} &\le 10^{-3} \\
\frac{\alpha}{1000} &\ge \ln(1000) \approx 6.908 \\
\alpha &\ge 6908
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the closed-form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon_{\text{total}}}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{2m}{\beta}\right) = \frac{1000}{5}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{10}{0.01}\right) = 200 \times \ln(1000) \approx 1381.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3.5 marks)** — Utility bound.

**Interpretation:** Releasing 5 queries with budget $\varepsilon = 1$ each under basic composition consumes the same budget as one query with $\varepsilon = 5$. The error bound is approximately $\$1382$, which is on the order of a single patient's treatment cost. Higher accuracy would require either a smaller $m$, a larger per-query $\varepsilon$, or an advanced composition theorem (e.g., **Dwork-Rothblum advanced composition**).

**Total: 7 + 7 = 14 marks.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this before your exam!**

- **Core formula to memorise:** $M_L(D) = f(D) + Lap(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$, applied **coordinate-wise**.
- **Sensitivity** $\Delta f$ is a property of the **function** $f$, not the data. Always compute it analytically before sampling noise.
- **Counting query:** $\Delta f = 1$. **Sum query** with bounded range $[a,b]$: $\Delta f = b - a$. **Mean** with $n$ records and bounded range $[a,b]$: $\Delta f = (b-a)/n$.
- **Mean of $M_L(D)$ is unbiased:** $\mathbb{E}[M_L(D)] = f(D)$.
- **Variance of each coordinate:** $2b^2 = 2(\Delta f / \varepsilon)^2$. Scales as $1/\varepsilon^2$ — halving the privacy budget **quadruples** the noise.
- **$(\alpha, \beta)$-utility** for $k$-dimensional output: $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2k}{\beta}\right)$.
- **Privacy proof skeleton** (write this verbatim):
  1. Form the likelihood ratio.
  2. Bound the integrand pointwise.
  3. Apply the **reverse triangle inequality**.
  4. Substitute $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$.
  5. Conclude ratio $\le e^{\varepsilon}$.
- **Sequential composition:** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_j \varepsilon_j$.
- **Parallel composition:** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max_j \varepsilon_j$ (for disjoint data partitions).
- **Post-processing invariance:** Any function $g$ of $M_L(D)$ is automatically $\varepsilon$-DP — no extra privacy cost.
- **Why not Gaussian?** Gaussian noise gives only $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP, not pure $\varepsilon$-DP. Use Gaussian only when **advanced composition** benefits outweigh the relaxation to approximate DP.
- **Laplace Mechanism limitation:** Fails for queries with **infinite $L_1$-sensitivity** (e.g., median, mode). Use the **Exponential Mechanism** in those cases (covered in next topic).
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing $L_1$ sensitivity $\Delta f$ (Laplace) with $L_2$ sensitivity $\Delta_2 f$ (Gaussian). Memorise: **"Laplace = $L_1$"** and **"Gaussian = $L_2$"**.
- **Always mention independence** of the Laplace samples across coordinates and across queries (in composition).
- **Use $\Vert \cdot \Vert_1$** (LaTeX norm notation), not bare absolute-value bars, in exam answers.
- **Distribution PDF to memorise:** $f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{2b}\exp(-\vert x - \mu \vert / b)$; $\text{Var}(X) = 2b^2$.
- **Engineering takeaway:** The Laplace Mechanism is the **default first choice** for $\varepsilon$-DP on **bounded-sensitivity, numeric, low-to-moderate-dimensional** queries.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Federated Learning (FL) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Federated Learning (FL)** is a decentralized machine learning paradigm in which multiple clients (e.g., mobile devices, hospitals, banks) collaboratively train a shared global model **without exposing their raw local data** to a central server or to each other. Only model updates (gradients or weights) are exchanged, preserving data privacy, locality, and regulatory compliance.

The formal mathematical setting (McMahan et al., 2017, the foundational FL paper):  
We have $K$ clients, each holding a local dataset $\mathcal{D}_k$ of size $n_k$. The total dataset is $\mathcal{D} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{D}_k$ with $n = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$. The goal is to learn a global model $w^\star$ that minimizes:

$$w^\star = \arg\min_{w} \, F(w) \;=\; \arg\min_{w} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} F_k(w)$$

where $F_k(w)$ is the local empirical risk (loss) on client $k$.

---

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Hospital Collaboration" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine 10 hospitals want to train an AI model to detect tumors from X-ray images, but privacy laws (HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023) prevent them from sharing patient data.**

- **Old (Centralized) way:** All hospitals mail their X-rays to a central lab → privacy breach, legal risk, huge bandwidth.
- **Federated way:** Each hospital keeps its X-rays locally. A coordinator sends the *current AI model* to each hospital. Each hospital trains the model on its own data and sends back only the **learned improvements** (numbers), not the X-rays. The coordinator averages these improvements into a better global model and repeats.  
- **Result:** No hospital ever sees another hospital's patients, yet everyone benefits from a smarter shared model. This is the **spirit of Responsible AI** — privacy-preserving collaboration.

The standard industry example is **Google Gboard** — your phone learns your typing patterns *locally* and only sends tiny model updates (not your typed messages) to Google to improve autocomplete globally.

---

## 1.3 Security in AI Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security in AI Systems** refers to the study of vulnerabilities, adversarial threats, attack vectors, and defense mechanisms that target machine learning models throughout their lifecycle — during training, deployment, and inference. It is a sub-discipline of **Trustworthy / Responsible AI** alongside fairness, privacy, and explainability.

Three canonical pillars of AI security are:

| Pillar | Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Protect training data and model parameters | Model inversion attack |
| **Integrity** | Ensure model outputs are correct and untampered | Adversarial patch on a stop sign |
| **Availability** | Keep the system usable under attack | Model DoS via sponge examples |

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Topics Belong Together

> [!NOTE]
> Federated Learning is **both a privacy solution and a new attack surface**. By decentralizing training, it solves some privacy problems (no raw data leaves the device) but **opens new security risks** (malicious clients can poison the global model, leak data via gradient inversion, etc.). Hence KTU places them in the same module.

Key security risks specific to FL:
- **Gradient Leakage Attacks** — gradients can be inverted to reconstruct raw training images (Zhu et al., 2019).
- **Byzantine / Model Poisoning Attacks** — malicious clients send crafted updates to corrupt the global model.
- **Backdoor Attacks** — a trigger pattern is planted so the model misclassifies only specific inputs.
- **Membership Inference Attacks** — adversary infers whether a particular sample was used in training.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **ε (epsilon)** — privacy budget in **Differential Privacy (DP)**. Lower ε ⇒ stronger privacy. Typical values: ε ∈ [0.1, 10].
- **δ (delta)** — failure probability in DP. Standard: δ < 1/n (negligible).
- **L_p norm ball radius** — magnitude of allowed adversarial perturbation, denoted **ε_adv** (commonly ε = 8/255 for image classifiers).
- **FL communication rounds (T)** — typically 100 to 1000.
- **Client sampling rate (C)** — fraction of clients selected per round, commonly C ∈ [0.1, 0.3].

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Federated Learning Works — The Operational Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **FL is an iterative, communication-based optimization algorithm.** The server orchestrates rounds; clients compute locally; aggregation fuses knowledge.

**Step-by-step FL round (round t):**

1. **Client Selection** — Server samples a subset $S_t \subseteq \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ of clients with sampling rate $C$.
2. **Global Broadcast** — Server transmits current global weights $w_t$ to all selected clients.
3. **Local Training** — Each selected client $k$ runs $E$ epochs of (stochastic) gradient descent on its local data:
$$w_{t+1}^{k} \;\leftarrow\; w_t \;-\; \eta \, \nabla F_k(w_t)$$
where $\eta$ is the learning rate.
4. **Update Upload** — Each client returns $\Delta w_t^k = w_{t+1}^k - w_t$ (or full $w_{t+1}^k$) to the server.
5. **Aggregation (FedAvg)** — Server computes the new global weights as a weighted average:
$$w_{t+1} \;=\; \sum_{k \in S_t} \frac{n_k}{n_{S_t}} \, w_{t+1}^k$$
6. **Convergence Check** — If loss $F(w_{t+1})$ has stabilized or $t = T$, stop; else $t \leftarrow t+1$ and go to Step 1.

### 2.1.1 Why this works (the "How")

- **Weighted averaging** preserves the contribution of larger clients (more data ⇒ higher weight).
- **Local epochs $E > 1$** reduce communication cost (the main FL bottleneck).
- **Client sampling** handles device availability (phones go offline).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — it covers ~80% of FL exam questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Global objective | $F(w) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} F_k(w)$ | Weighted sum of local risks |
| 2 | Local SGD update | $w_{t+1}^{k} = w_t - \eta \nabla F_k(w_t)$ | Performed on client $k$ |
| 3 | **FedAvg Aggregation** | $w_{t+1} = \sum_{k \in S_t} \frac{n_k}{n_{S_t}} w_{t+1}^{k}$ | Heart of FL — exam favorite |
| 4 | Local loss | $F_k(w) = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{(x_i,y_i)\in\mathcal{D}_k} \ell(w; x_i, y_i)$ | Empirical risk on client $k$ |
| 5 | Adversarial perturbation | $x' = x + \delta,\quad \text{s.t. } \Vert\delta\Vert_p \leq \varepsilon_{adv}$ | $L_p$ ball constraint |
| 6 | Differential Privacy (Gaussian mech.) | $\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I)$ | Noise for privacy |
| 7 | DP composition (basic) | $\varepsilon_{total} \approx \sum_i \varepsilon_i$ | Sequential composition |
| 8 | Membership Inference advantage | $\text{Adv} = \vert \Pr[M(x_{in})=1] - \Pr[M(x_{out})=1]\vert$ | Higher ⇒ more leakage |
| 9 | Gradient inversion reconstruction | $\hat{x} \approx x^\star = \arg\min_x \Vert \nabla \ell(w;x) - g \Vert^2$ | Recovered from gradient $g$ |
| 10 | Robust aggregation (Krum) | Select client closest to $K{-}B{-}2$ nearest neighbors | Byzantine-resilient |

> [!TIP]
> In markdown tables, the **absolute-value / norm symbol** is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert_p$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

## 2.3 Security Threat Taxonomy in AI

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects a clean **3-stage taxonomy** aligned with the ML pipeline.

### Stage A — Training-Phase Threats
- **Data Poisoning** — attacker tampers with training labels/features.
- **Model Poisoning** — attacker (often a malicious FL client) submits malicious gradients.
- **Backdoor Injection** — model behaves normally except on trigger-bearing inputs.

### Stage B — Inference-Phase Threats
- **Adversarial Examples (Evasion)** — crafted $x'$ fools the deployed model.
- **Model Extraction / Stealing** — attacker replicates the model via API queries.
- **Membership Inference** — attacker infers if a sample was in training data.

### Stage C — Deployment / System-Level Threats
- **Model Inversion** — reconstruct training data from model outputs.
- **Sponge / Energy Attacks** — increase inference latency for DoS.
- **Supply-Chain Attacks** — compromised pre-trained models (e.g., backdoored checkpoints on HuggingFace).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | FL Application | Security Concern Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| Healthcare (NHS, AIIMS) | Multi-hospital tumor detection | Patient privacy (HIPAA/DPDP) |
| Finance (RBI consortiums) | Cross-bank fraud detection | Anti-money-laundering without data sharing |
| Mobile (Google Gboard) | Next-word prediction | On-device personalization |
| Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla fleet) | Road-condition learning | Edge intelligence without uploading video |
| Smart Manufacturing | Predictive maintenance across plants | Industrial IP protection |

Security research directly impacts **adversarial robustness** in self-driving cars (stop-sign attacks), **medical safety** (misdiagnosis from perturbed scans), and **biometric authentication** (face-recognition spoofing).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the FedAvg Update Rule

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU favorite** — understand every line, not just the final formula.

### 3.1.1 Setup

We have $K$ clients. In round $t$, a subset $S_t$ of clients is selected. Let $n_{S_t} = \sum_{k \in S_t} n_k$.

Each client $k$ starts from the broadcast global weights $w_t$ and runs $E$ local SGD epochs with learning rate $\eta$ on loss $F_k$.

### 3.1.2 Local Update Derivation

**Step 1 — Gradient at current weights:**  
For a single client $k$, compute the stochastic gradient on a sampled mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_k$:

$$g_t^k \;=\; \nabla_w F_k(w_t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{B}_k \vert} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{B}_k} \nabla_w \, \ell(w_t; x_i, y_i)$$

**Step 2 — Single SGD step:**  
Update local weights along the negative gradient:

$$w_{t+1}^{k} \;=\; w_t \;-\; \eta \, g_t^k$$

**Step 3 — Repeat for $E$ epochs:**  
The full local trajectory after $E$ epochs:

$$w_{t+1}^{k} \;=\; w_t \;-\; \eta \sum_{e=0}^{E-1} g_{t,e}^{k}$$

where $g_{t,e}^{k}$ is the gradient at epoch $e$.

### 3.1.3 Aggregation Derivation

**Step 4 — Upload to server:**  
Each client sends $w_{t+1}^k$ (or $\Delta w_t^k = w_{t+1}^k - w_t$) to the central server.

**Step 5 — Server-side weighted average:**  
The server's aggregation goal is to minimize a weighted combination of client losses:

$$F_{S_t}(w) \;=\; \sum_{k \in S_t} \frac{n_k}{n_{S_t}} F_k(w)$$

Taking the gradient and equating to zero at the optimum:

$$\nabla F_{S_t}(w^\star) \;=\; \sum_{k \in S_t} \frac{n_k}{n_{S_t}} \nabla F_k(w^\star) \;=\; 0$$

Since local SGD produces $w_{t+1}^k$ that approximately satisfies $\nabla F_k(w_{t+1}^k) \approx 0$, a natural proxy solution is to **weight by data size**:

**Step 6 — Final FedAvg rule:**

$$\boxed{\,w_{t+1} \;=\; \sum_{k \in S_t} \frac{n_k}{n_{S_t}} \, w_{t+1}^{k}\,}$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — exactly the kind KTU asks in 14-mark questions.**

Suppose $K = 3$ clients with data sizes $n_1 = 100$, $n_2 = 200$, $n_3 = 300$.  
After round $t$, the clients return weights:

$$w_{t+1}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad w_{t+1}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad w_{t+1}^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute normalized weights:**

$$n = 100 + 200 + 300 = 600$$

$$\frac{n_1}{n} = \frac{100}{600} = 0.1667, \quad \frac{n_2}{n} = 0.3333, \quad \frac{n_3}{n} = 0.5000$$

**Apply FedAvg:**

$$w_{t+1} = 0.1667 \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + 0.3333 \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5000 \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute each component:**

$$w_{t+1}^{(1)} = 0.1667 \times 0.2 = 0.0333, \quad w_{t+1}^{(1)} = 0.1667 \times 0.4 = 0.0667$$

$$w_{t+1}^{(2)} = 0.3333 \times 0.3 = 0.1000, \quad w_{t+1}^{(2)} = 0.3333 \times 0.5 = 0.1667$$

$$w_{t+1}^{(3)} = 0.5000 \times 0.4 = 0.2000, \quad w_{t+1}^{(3)} = 0.5000 \times 0.6 = 0.3000$$

**Sum:**

$$w_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0333 + 0.1000 + 0.2000 \\ 0.0667 + 0.1667 + 0.3000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3333 \\ 0.5333 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification** — without weighting (simple average) would give $\begin{bmatrix}0.3000 \\ 0.5000\end{bmatrix}$, so FedAvg shifts toward the largest client's vector, as expected.

---

## 3.2 Adversarial Example Generation — FGSM Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) is a canonical evasion attack — extremely high-yield for KTU.

### 3.2.1 Objective

Given a clean input $x$ with true label $y$, craft $x' = x + \delta$ such that the model misclassifies $x'$, while keeping $\Vert\delta\Vert_\infty \leq \varepsilon_{adv}$.

### 3.2.2 Linearization of Loss

For a neural network with loss $\ell(w; x, y)$, linearize around $x$:

$$\ell(w; x+\delta, y) \;\approx\; \ell(w; x, y) \;+\; \delta^\top \nabla_x \ell(w; x, y)$$

### 3.2.3 Maximize the Loss

We want $\delta$ that maximizes this linear approximation under the $\ell_\infty$ budget.

The optimal direction is the sign of the gradient (the dual norm of $\ell_\infty$ is $\ell_1$):

$$\delta^\star \;=\; \varepsilon_{adv} \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \ell(w; x, y)\right)$$

### 3.2.4 Final FGSM Perturbation

$$\boxed{\,x' \;=\; x \;+\; \varepsilon_{adv} \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \ell(w; x, y)\right)\,}$$

### 3.2.5 Interpretation

- $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ clips each pixel's gradient to $\{-1, +1\}$.
- Multiplying by $\varepsilon_{adv}$ caps pixel-wise perturbation.
- **Standard value:** $\varepsilon_{adv} = 8/255$ on images scaled to $[0, 1]$.

### 3.2.6 Numerical Mini-Example

Let gradient w.r.t. one pixel be $g = -0.42$ and $\varepsilon_{adv} = 0.03$.

$$\text{sign}(g) = -1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta = -0.03$$

If original pixel $x = 0.50$, then $x' = 0.50 + (-0.03) = 0.47$ — a 3 % change that can flip the model's prediction with high probability.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: FL vs Centralized ML vs Split Learning

| Property | Centralized ML | Federated Learning | Split Learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data location | Single server | Distributed, local | Distributed |
| Communication cost | Low (data uploaded once) | High (gradients each round) | Medium (activations) |
| Privacy | Poor (raw data exposed) | Better (only gradients) | Better |
| Heterogeneity handling | Trivial | Hard (non-IID data) | Moderate |
| Attack surface | Central server only | Every client is a vector | Cut-layer server |
| Typical convergence | Few epochs, many iterations | Many rounds, few local epochs | Comparable to FL |

---

## 3.4 Convergence Intuition for FedAvg

Under standard assumptions (smoothness $L$, bounded variance $\sigma^2$, partial participation $C$), FedAvg satisfies:

$$F(w_T) - F(w^\star) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} + \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

The $\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ term dominates when **client heterogeneity** (non-IID data) is high — a key trade-off students must remember.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**Define Federated Learning. List any two of its key advantages over centralized machine learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Federated Learning is a decentralized machine learning approach where multiple clients collaboratively train a shared global model **without sharing their raw local data**. Only model updates (gradients or weights) are exchanged with a central server, which aggregates them using algorithms like FedAvg.

**Two advantages (1 mark — ½ mark each):**

1. **Privacy preservation** — Raw data never leaves the client device, reducing exposure to breaches and ensuring regulatory compliance (GDPR, DPDP Act).
2. **Reduced communication of raw data** — Only small model updates are transmitted, lowering bandwidth costs.
3. *(any other valid: handles heterogeneous data sources, edge personalization, etc.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**What is an adversarial example? Mention the $\ell_\infty$ constraint used in FGSM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **adversarial example** (1.5 marks) is a carefully perturbed input $x' = x + \delta$ that causes a trained model to misclassify, while remaining visually (or semantically) indistinguishable from the original $x$.

> The **$\ell_\infty$ constraint** (1.5 marks) requires the perturbation $\delta$ to satisfy $\Vert\delta\Vert_\infty \leq \varepsilon_{adv}$, meaning every single pixel can change by at most $\varepsilon_{adv}$. For image classifiers normalized to $[0,1]$, the standard value is $\varepsilon_{adv} = 8/255$.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B requires **internal choice**. Two full alternatives are provided below.
